subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding Acute Leukaemia,

db.general_explanation: Auer rods are specific to myeloid blasts. Karyotype is prognostic, blast percentage is diagnostic criteria, Sudan black stains myeloid cells (though often negative in lymphoblasts, the provided answer key marks this T), and uric acid is elevated in high turnover states.

answer_raw: A. Auer Rods are present in myeloblasts.
B. Karyotype of blast cells is important for prognosis.
C. Percentage of blast cells in bone marrow is important for diagnosis.
E. Uric acid levels are elevated.
ai.clinical_core: Acute leukemia is defined by the rapid proliferation of immature blast cells (>20% in marrow). Diagnosis involves morphology, cytogenetics, and metabolic markers.

options.text: A. Auer Rods are present in myeloblasts.
options.ai_explanation: Auer rods are pathognomonic for AML (myeloid lineage).

options.text: B. Karyotype of blast cells is important for prognosis.
options.ai_explanation: Cytogenetics (e.g., t(8;21), t(15;17)) is the single most important prognostic factor in acute leukemia.

options.text: C. Percentage of blast cells in bone marrow is important for diagnosis.
options.ai_explanation: A blast count of ≥20% in the bone marrow or blood is the WHO criterion for acute leukemia.
Sudan Black B stains myeloid granules; it is positive in AML and negative in ALL.

options.text: E. Uric acid levels are elevated.
options.ai_explanation: High cell turnover and tumor lysis lead to hyperuricemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Acute leukaemia

db.general_explanation: Auer rods are observed in myeloid blast cells in AML/APL, not lymphoblasts. Myeloid/monocytic blasts are typically weakly positive or negative for PAS; a granular PAS pattern is characteristic of lymphoblastic leukaemia.

answer_raw: A. Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is common in children
B. Cytogenetics are considered as a prognostic marker
E. Therapy induced leukaemia shows a poor prognosis
ai.clinical_core: Acute leukemias are characterized by the rapid proliferation of immature white blood cells (blasts).

options.text: A. Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia is common in children
options.ai_explanation: ALL is the most common malignancy in childhood.

options.text: B. Cytogenetics are considered as a prognostic marker
options.ai_explanation: Chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., t(15;17), t(9;22)) are critical for risk stratification.
Auer rods are pathognomonic for myeloblasts (AML).

options.text: D. Myeloblasts stain positive with Periodic acid Schiff stain
options.ai_explanation: PAS positivity is characteristic of lymphoblasts; myeloblasts are usually MPO positive.

options.text: E. Therapy induced leukaemia shows a poor prognosis
options.ai_explanation: Secondary AML (after chemo/radiation) typically has complex cytogenetics and poor outcomes.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 10-year-old child with Which of the following is most speorfic for the diagnosis of acute myeloblastic leakage

db.general_explanation: Presence of Auer rods is pathognomonic/most specific for acute myeloid leukemia.

ai.answer_raw: E. Presence of auger rods in blast cells
ai.clinical_core: Acute Myeloid Leukemia (AML) is diagnosed by the presence of >20% myeloblasts in the marrow. Auer rods are definitive for myeloid differentiation.

options.text: A. Presence of nucleated RBC in peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Seen in many conditions (stress, hemolysis, marrow infiltration).

options.text: B. Presence of myelocytes in peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Seen in CML and leukemoid reactions.
Common in all acute leukemias and many other conditions.
LDH is a non-specific marker of cell turnover.

options.text: E. Presence of auger rods in blast cells
options.ai_explanation: Auer rods are pathognomonic for AML.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Alpha thalassemia trait

db.general_explanation: A. F (splenomegaly results in diseased person not trait). B. T. C. F (due to reduced rate of synthesis). D. T. E. T.

answer_raw: E. Warrant routing partner screening before marriage
ai.clinical_core: Alpha thalassemia trait (two-gene deletion) causes mild microcytic anemia. It is important to identify for genetic counseling.

options.ai_explanation: Splenomegaly is not a feature of alpha thalassemia trait (it is seen in HbH disease).

options.text: B. Is diagnosed by hemoglobin HPLC
options.ai_explanation: HPLC is often normal in alpha trait; diagnosis usually requires DNA analysis.

options.text: C. Is a defect in red cell membrane protein
options.ai_explanation: It is a defect in globin chain synthesis.
Characterized by low MCV and MCH with mild or no anemia.

options.text: E. Warrant routing partner screening before marriage
options.ai_explanation: Screening is essential to prevent HbH disease or Hydrops Fetalis in offspring.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 55 years old male presented with sob on exertion, His Hb level is 8g/dL. What are the investigations that favorer iron deficiency anaemia.

db.general_explanation: Pencil cells, low ferritin, low transferrin saturation, and high soluble transferrin receptors are markers of iron deficiency. Hb A2 of 5% suggests Beta-thalassemia trait.

answer_raw: A. Pencil cells in peripheral blood film
ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is characterized by microcytic hypochromic indices. Diagnostic markers include low ferritin (most specific), low transferrin saturation, and elevated soluble transferrin receptors.

options.text: A. Pencil cells in peripheral blood film
options.ai_explanation: Pencil-shaped poikilocytes are a characteristic morphological finding in the peripheral film of IDA patients.
Serum ferritin is the most sensitive and specific biochemical marker for depleted iron stores.
An elevated HbA2 (>3.5%) is diagnostic of Beta-thalassemia trait, not iron deficiency.
Low transferrin saturation (typically <16%) indicates inadequate iron supply for erythropoiesis.
Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) levels rise in response to cellular iron deficiency.

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F regarding pernicrcus anaemia?

db.general_explanation: Pernicious anemia features oval macrocytes, giant metamyelocytes, atrophic gastritis, and anti-parietal cell antibodies.

answer_raw: A. Oval macroastes in peripheral blood
E. high levels cl serum ferritin
ai.clinical_core: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune condition caused by the lack of intrinsic factor due to gastric parietal cell atrophy, leading to Vitamin B12 deficiency. It is characterized by megaloblastic anemia, atrophic gastritis, and potential neurological complications.

options.text: A. Oval macroastes in peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Oval macrocytes are a hallmark of megaloblastic erythropoiesis seen in the peripheral blood film.

options.text: B. Giant metamyelocytes can be feand in the peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Giant metamyelocytes are characteristic of the bone marrow in megaloblastic anemia, not the peripheral blood.
Type A atrophic gastritis is the underlying cause of pernicious anemia, leading to loss of parietal cells.
Anti-parietal cell antibodies (likely intended by 'inhibitors') are present in about 90% of patients.

options.text: E. high levels cl serum ferritin
options.ai_explanation: Ineffective erythropoiesis in megaloblastic anemia leads to increased iron turnover and elevated serum ferritin.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 65-year-old woman presents with tiredness. Her investigations are as follows : Hb - 8.0 g/dl, MCV - 120 fl, Reticulocyte count - 10%, Serum bilirubin - 4.0 mg/dl.

db.general_explanation: The patient has macrocytic anemia (MCV 120) with signs suggestive of Vitamin B12 deficiency.

ai.clinical_core: The combination of anemia, high MCV (due to reticulocytosis), high reticulocyte count, and jaundice (high bilirubin) is diagnostic of hemolytic anemia.

options.ai_explanation: Fits the profile of regenerative anemia (high retics) and hemolysis (high bilirubin).
Usually has low reticulocyte counts.
Characterized by very low reticulocyte counts.
Causes macrocytosis but typically with a low reticulocyte count (ineffective erythropoiesis).
Can cause macrocytosis but not significant hemolysis/reticulocytosis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding anaemia in preganancy,

db.general_explanation: A. T (dilutional anaemia due to plasma volume increase). B. F (not every mother has deficiency if supplements taken). C. F (<11g/dl). D. T. E. F (should be <75fl for microcytic; not diagnostic, requires iron studies).

answer_raw: A. Anaemia which is occur due to dilution associated normocytic red blood cells
B. Folate deficiency co exist with iron deficiency
D. Iron suppliment is given to all pregnant mothers in sri lanka
ai.clinical_core: Anemia in pregnancy is often multifactorial, involving physiological hemodilution and increased nutritional demands for iron and folate.
ai.reference: NICE Guidelines on Antenatal Care; Hoffbrand's Essential Hematology, Chapter 3

options.text: A. Anaemia which is occur due to dilution associated normocytic red blood cells
options.ai_explanation: Physiological anemia of pregnancy is due to a greater increase in plasma volume compared to red cell mass.

options.text: B. Folate deficiency co exist with iron deficiency
options.ai_explanation: Combined deficiencies are common due to high fetal demand.

options.text: C. Heamoglobin level below 11.5 dl/l in 1st trimester is define as anaemia
options.ai_explanation: WHO defines anemia in pregnancy as Hb < 11.0 g/dL.

options.text: D. Iron suppliment is given to all pregnant mothers in sri lanka
options.ai_explanation: Routine iron and folic acid supplementation is standard antenatal care in Sri Lanka.

options.text: E. MCV <80fl is diagnostic of iron deficiency anaemia
options.ai_explanation: Microcytosis is suggestive but not diagnostic, as it can also be seen in thalassemia trait.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 70yrs old female patient presents with lethargy, loss of weight and loss of appetite. Her blood picture reported as “Leuco-erythroblastic blood picture”. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: A leuco-erythroblastic blood picture is typically indicative of marrow infiltration, most commonly by disseminated malignancy (metastatic disease).

ai.clinical_core: In an elderly patient with weight loss and a leucoerythroblastic blood picture, bone marrow infiltration by a metastatic solid tumor (myelophthisis) is a primary concern.

options.ai_explanation: Does not cause a leucoerythroblastic picture.
Metastatic cancer to the bone marrow is a common cause of leucoerythroblastosis in the elderly.
Causes cytopenias and dysplasia, but not typically a leucoerythroblastic picture.
Miliary TB can cause it, but malignancy is more common in this clinical context.
Does not cause a leucoerythroblastic picture.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following give rise to a leuko erythroblastic blood picture,

db.general_explanation: Leukoerythroblastic blood film (presence of erythroblast & granulocyte precursors) is due to metastatic infiltration of bone marrow or certain benign & neoplastic blood disorders. Causes include metastatic neoplasm, myelofibrosis, acute/chronic myeloid leukemia, myeloma, lymphoma, miliary TB, severe megaloblastic anaemia, severe haemolysis, and osteoporosis.

ai.clinical_core: Leukoerythroblastic blood pictures occur when the bone marrow is under extreme stress or is being replaced by fibrosis or tumor.
ai.reference: Dacie and Lewis Practical Hematology, Chapter 3

options.ai_explanation: Causes a surge in erythropoietin and marrow activity, pushing out immature cells.
Characterized by blasts, not a full range of myeloid and erythroid precursors.
Marrow is empty; no precursors are seen.
The classic cause due to marrow displacement and extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Severe systemic illness and hemolysis can trigger this picture.

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F Regarding anaemias?

db.general_explanation: G6PD is X-linked recessive. Thalassaemia trait usually does not require transfusion. Transfusion-related iron overload, splenectomy for hereditary spherocytosis, and Mycoplasma causing cold-agglutinin AIHA are correct.

answer_raw: C. Transfusion related iron overload is a known complication of chronre haemolytrcanaemia.
D. Splenectomy is a treatment modality of children with hereditary spherogtosis?
E. Mycoplasma infection causes autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.
ai.clinical_core: Anemias have diverse etiologies and treatments. Chronic hemolysis often leads to iron overload and may require surgical intervention.

options.text: A. G6 PD deficiency is inherited from autosomal dominant.
options.ai_explanation: G6PD deficiency is X-linked recessive.

options.text: B. thalassaemia trait people need regular blood transfusion
options.ai_explanation: Thalassemia trait is a carrier state and does not require transfusions.

options.text: C. Transfusion related iron overload is a known complication of chronre haemolytrcanaemia.
options.ai_explanation: Repeated transfusions in chronic anemia (like Thal Major) inevitably lead to iron overload.

options.text: D. Splenectomy is a treatment modality of children with hereditary spherogtosis?
options.ai_explanation: Splenectomy reduces red cell destruction in HS, though it is usually delayed until after age 6.

options.text: E. Mycoplasma infection causes autoimmune haemolytic anaemia.
options.ai_explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a classic cause of cold agglutinin disease (AIHA).

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F regarding dalteparin:

db.general_explanation: Correct answers: LMWH (T), Longer duration (T), Routine STEMI treatment (F), Contraindicated in pregnancy (F), Monitoring not essential (T)

answer_raw: A. It is a low molecular weight heparin
B. It has longer duration action compared to unfractionated heparin
C. It’s routinely used to treat STEMI
E. Regular monitoring is not essential
ai.clinical_core: Dalteparin is an LMWH with predictable pharmacokinetics, making it superior to UFH for many indications.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Clinical Pharmacology Section

options.text: A. It is a low molecular weight heparin
options.ai_explanation: Dalteparin is a standard LMWH.

options.text: B. It has longer duration action compared to unfractionated heparin
options.ai_explanation: LMWHs have a longer half-life, allowing for once or twice daily dosing.

options.text: C. It’s routinely used to treat STEMI
options.ai_explanation: LMWHs are used as anticoagulation in the management of acute coronary syndromes.
LMWHs are the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy as they do not cross the placenta.

options.text: E. Regular monitoring is not essential
options.ai_explanation: Unlike UFH, LMWH does not require routine APTT monitoring due to its predictable response.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Clopidogrel:

db.general_explanation: Correct answers: Stroke prophylaxis (T), ACS (T), GI bleeding (T), ADP pathway inhibition (T), Prostaglandin synthesis (F)

answer_raw: A. Is useful in 2ndry prophylaxis for stroke
B. Is used in acute coronary syndrome
C. Has no major effect on GI bleeding when compared to aspirin.
D. Inhibit ADP pathway of PLT
ai.clinical_core: Clopidogrel is a thienopyridine antiplatelet agent that irreversibly inhibits the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets. It is used in the management of ACS and for secondary prevention of ischemic stroke and MI.

options.text: A. Is useful in 2ndry prophylaxis for stroke
options.ai_explanation: Clopidogrel is a standard alternative or adjunct to aspirin for secondary stroke prevention.

options.text: B. Is used in acute coronary syndrome
options.ai_explanation: It is a component of dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) used in ACS.

options.text: C. Has no major effect on GI bleeding when compared to aspirin.
options.ai_explanation: Clinical trials (like CAPRIE) showed clopidogrel has a slightly lower risk of GI bleeding compared to aspirin, though the risk is not zero.

options.text: D. Inhibit ADP pathway of PLT
options.ai_explanation: Clopidogrel's mechanism of action is the irreversible inhibition of the P2Y12 ADP receptor.
This is the mechanism of aspirin and NSAIDs (COX inhibition), not clopidogrel.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are true or false regarding the mode of action of antiplatelet drugs?

db.general_explanation: Ticlopidine also works by inhibiting ADP dependent platelet aggregation.

answer_raw: C. Clopidogrel – inhibits ADP mediated platelet aggregation
ai.clinical_core: Antiplatelet drugs target various pathways of platelet activation and aggregation, including COX-1, P2Y12 (ADP) receptors, and GPIIb/IIIa receptors.

options.ai_explanation: Abciximab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks the GPIIb/IIIa receptor.
Aspirin primarily inhibits COX-1 at low doses to achieve its antiplatelet effect.

options.text: C. Clopidogrel – inhibits ADP mediated platelet aggregation
options.ai_explanation: Clopidogrel irreversibly blocks the P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets.
Dipyridamole inhibits phosphodiesterase, increasing cAMP levels in platelets.
Ticlopidine, like clopidogrel, is an ADP receptor antagonist.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 45-year-old man presented with recurrent upper respiratory tract infections, gum bleeding and shortness of breath on exertion. He had no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. His bone marrow biopsy was hypocellular with no increase of reticulin. Which of the following is most compatible with his diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Hypocellular bone marrow with no reticulin increase and pancytopenia features (infections, bleeding, anemia) are diagnostic of aplastic anaemia.

ai.clinical_core: Aplastic anemia is characterized by pancytopenia and a hypocellular bone marrow where hematopoietic tissue is replaced by fat, without fibrosis (reticulin).

options.ai_explanation: Marrow is typically hypercellular with plasma cells.
Marrow is hypercellular (megaloblastic).
Marrow is usually hypercellular or normocellular with dysplastic features.
Hypocellular marrow with no increase in reticulin is the definition of aplastic anemia.
Characterized by an increase in reticulin/collagen fibers.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding auto immune hemolytic anaemia,

db.general_explanation: Direct Coomb's is the gold standard. Cold AIHA is associated with Mycoplasma. IgG typically causes extravascular hemolysis, while IgM causes intravascular hemolysis. Corticosteroids are first-line for warm AIHA.

answer_raw: B. Mycoplasma infections cause cold hemolytic agglutination.
E. Warm hemolytic anaemia treated with steroids.
ai.clinical_core: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA) is classified into warm (IgG-mediated) and cold (IgM-mediated) types. Warm AIHA usually causes extravascular hemolysis in the spleen and responds to steroids, while cold AIHA is often triggered by infections like Mycoplasma and involves complement-mediated hemolysis.

options.ai_explanation: The DAT (Direct Coombs) is the definitive test to detect antibodies or complement on the red cell surface.

options.text: B. Mycoplasma infections cause cold hemolytic agglutination.
options.ai_explanation: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is a classic trigger for the production of cold agglutinins (anti-I antibodies).
IgG-coated cells are usually removed by splenic macrophages (extravascular hemolysis).

options.text: D. Hemolysis in cold hemolytic anaemia occur in spleen.
options.ai_explanation: Cold AIHA hemolysis is primarily complement-mediated and often occurs in the liver or intravascularly.

options.text: E. Warm hemolytic anaemia treated with steroids.
options.ai_explanation: Corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for warm-type AIHA.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Patient with beta thalassaemia major has severe anaemia from late infancy. What is the primary pathophysiological cause for anaemia in beta thalassaemia?

db.general_explanation: Altered ratio of alpha/beta synthesis in beta thalassemia causes reduced beta chain synthesis and excess alpha chain synthesis. Excess alpha chains accumulate on rbc causing ineffective erythropoiesis.

ai.answer_raw: B. Accumulation of insolube alpha globins
ai.clinical_core: In beta thalassemia, the lack of beta-globin chains leads to an excess of unpaired alpha-globin chains, which precipitate and cause intramedullary destruction of RBC precursors.

options.text: A. Defect in red blood cell membrane protein
options.ai_explanation: This describes hereditary spherocytosis.

options.text: B. Accumulation of insolube alpha globins
options.ai_explanation: Unpaired alpha chains are toxic to erythroid precursors, causing ineffective erythropoiesis.

options.text: C. Defect in haem moiety of Hb molecule
options.ai_explanation: This describes sideroblastic anemias or porphyrias.
Iron overload is a complication, not the primary cause of the anemia.

options.text: E. Destruction of RBC inside enlarged spleen
options.ai_explanation: While splenomegaly contributes, the primary cause is ineffective erythropoiesis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 24-year-old female was found to have beta thalassemia trait

db.general_explanation: A. F (Hb around 8.5-12). B. F (Protective against malaria). C. T (Hypercoagulable state/ischaemia). D. F (Usually no symptoms). E. T (Mild reduction in MCV occurs).

answer_raw: A. Hb count 13mg/dl is compatible with the diagnosis.
D. Mild splenomegaly can be presented.
E. MCV 64fl is compatible with the diagnosis.
ai.clinical_core: Beta-thalassemia trait is a carrier state characterized by mild microcytic anemia or normal hemoglobin with significantly reduced MCV and elevated HbA2. It is generally asymptomatic.

options.text: A. Hb count 13mg/dl is compatible with the diagnosis.
options.ai_explanation: Assuming 'mg/dl' is a typo for 'g/dl', a hemoglobin of 13 g/dl is normal for a female and is frequently seen in thalassemia carriers.

options.text: B. Has a high chance to get cerebral malaria if she get expose to Plasmodium falciparum.
options.ai_explanation: Thalassemia trait actually confers a degree of protection against severe falciparum malaria.

options.text: C. Likely to have leg ulcers in future.
options.ai_explanation: Leg ulcers are complications of severe hemolytic anemias like Thalassemia Major or Sickle Cell Disease, not the trait.

options.text: D. Mild splenomegaly can be presented.
options.ai_explanation: While usually asymptomatic, a small percentage of individuals with thalassemia trait may have palpable mild splenomegaly.

options.text: E. MCV 64fl is compatible with the diagnosis.
options.ai_explanation: A very low MCV (often <70 fl) relative to a near-normal Hb is a hallmark of thalassemia trait.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 5 month old baby was brought to the hospital with lethargy and irritability. On examination she was pale, with mild hepatomegaly. Hb – 6 g/dl, WBC – 15,000/mm3, Platelets – 250,000/mm3, Hb A – 2%, Hb F 93%, Hb A2 – 5%. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this boy?

db.general_explanation: Thalassaemia major presents in the first 6 months of life.

ai.clinical_core: Beta thalassemia major presents in infancy as fetal hemoglobin (HbF) fails to switch to adult hemoglobin (HbA). The diagnosis is confirmed by high HbF and absent or very low HbA.

options.ai_explanation: The electrophoresis showing 93% HbF and only 2% HbA in a 5-month-old is diagnostic of Beta Thal Major.
Trait usually has high HbA2 (3.5-7%) but mostly HbA, not 93% HbF.
HbH disease is an alpha-thalassemia variant; it would not show this HbF/HbA pattern.
This is a red cell membrane defect, not a hemoglobinopathy; electrophoresis would be normal.
This is a red cell membrane defect; electrophoresis would be normal.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following parameter/s suggest/s optimal management in a five year old child with transfusion dependent beta-thalassemia?

db.general_explanation: Maxillary hyperplasia indicates bone marrow expansion. Pre-transfusion Hb should be 9-10.5 g/dl. Organomegaly is a sign of iron overload. Optimal serum ferritin is around 300 ng/dl.

answer_raw: A. Height for age between mean and -1SD (stranded deviation)
ai.clinical_core: Optimal management of Thalassemia Major aims to maintain growth and prevent iron overload through adequate transfusion and chelation.

options.text: A. Height for age between mean and -1SD (stranded deviation)
options.ai_explanation: Normal growth indicates adequate transfusion and controlled iron overload.
This is a sign of *inadequate* transfusion leading to bone marrow expansion.
Target pre-transfusion Hb is usually 9.5-10.5 g/dL to suppress extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Maintaining ferritin <1000 ng/dL is a goal of effective chelation therapy.

options.text: E. Spleen size 10 cm below the left costal margin
options.ai_explanation: Massive splenomegaly indicates poor control or hypersplenism.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following statements is/are true or false regarding individuals with βThalassaemia trait?

db.general_explanation: They have mild anaemia but do not require transfusions. Restriction of sports is relevant for haemophilia due to risk of haemarthrosis.

answer_raw: B. They are unlikely to have elevated serum ferritin.
C. They are unlikely to have splenomegaly
ai.clinical_core: Beta thalassemia trait is a carrier state characterized by mild microcytic anemia and is usually asymptomatic. It must be distinguished from iron deficiency.

options.text: A. They are likely to develop symptoms as they get older.
options.ai_explanation: Thalassemia trait is a stable genetic condition and does not typically progress to symptomatic disease.

options.text: B. They are unlikely to have elevated serum ferritin.
options.ai_explanation: Ferritin is usually normal in thal trait; elevated ferritin suggests iron overload, which is not a feature of the trait.

options.text: C. They are unlikely to have splenomegaly
options.ai_explanation: Splenomegaly is a feature of Thalassemia Intermedia or Major, not the trait.

options.text: D. Blood transfusion is recommended if the haemoglobin level is less than 10 g/dl.
options.ai_explanation: Transfusion is not indicated for thalassemia trait, as the anemia is mild and stable.

options.text: E. Strenuous sports like rugby and football are best discouraged.
options.ai_explanation: Individuals with thalassemia trait have no exercise restrictions.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 35 years-old with easy bruising coagulation profile is as follow. PT = 10 min, APTT = 38 min, BT = 12 min : prolonged. What is/are the cause is for this condition.

db.general_explanation: Correct answers: DIC (T), ITP (F), Renal failure (F), Warfarin (F), VWF (T)

ai.clinical_core: Von Willebrand Disease (VWD) is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It typically presents with a prolonged Bleeding Time (due to defective platelet adhesion) and a prolonged APTT (due to secondary Factor VIII deficiency), while the PT remains normal.

options.ai_explanation: DIC typically presents with prolongation of both PT and APTT, along with thrombocytopenia.
ITP causes a prolonged Bleeding Time due to low platelets, but the APTT and PT are normal.
Renal failure causes platelet dysfunction leading to a prolonged Bleeding Time, but it does not typically prolong the APTT.
Warfarin primarily prolongs the PT/INR by inhibiting Vitamin K-dependent factors.
VWD causes prolonged BT (platelet dysfunction) and prolonged APTT (low Factor VIII), matching the provided profile.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 13-day-old neonate is brought to the hospital with oozing from his umbilical stump. The baby’s coagulation profile is as follows: PT - 12s, APPT - 35s, TT - 12s, Platelet - 250,000/mm3. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Factor XIII deficiency is known for delayed umbilical stump bleeding despite normal routine screening tests.

ai.clinical_core: Factor XIII deficiency is a rare bleeding disorder where routine coagulation tests (PT, APTT, TT) and platelet counts are normal, but patients present with delayed bleeding, such as umbilical stump oozing.

options.ai_explanation: Hemophilia A would cause a prolonged APTT.
vWD usually presents with a prolonged bleeding time and sometimes a prolonged APTT.
Vitamin K deficiency (Hemorrhagic disease of the newborn) causes a prolonged PT and APTT.
Factor XIII stabilizes the fibrin clot; deficiency leads to normal screening tests but severe bleeding, classically from the umbilical stump.
Factor XI deficiency typically causes a prolonged APTT.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 23 year old known patient of Haemophilia A presented with haemarthrosis. Which of the following is the best product available for his immediate management?

db.general_explanation: Factor VIII concentrate is the standard and most specific replacement therapy for Haemophilia A.

ai.clinical_core: The management of acute bleeding in Hemophilia A requires immediate replacement of the deficient factor to achieve hemostatic levels.

options.ai_explanation: Used for patients with inhibitors (antibodies) to Factor VIII, not as first-line for standard Hemophilia A.
Contains Factor VIII but is no longer preferred due to lower purity and lack of viral inactivation compared to concentrates.
Only useful in mild Hemophilia A; it would not be sufficient for significant hemarthrosis in many patients.
Specific Factor VIII concentrate is the gold standard for immediate management of bleeding in Hemophilia A.
Contains Factors II, VII, IX, and X; it does not contain Factor VIII.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 14 year old girl presented with menorrhagia. Her coagulation profile is as follows. PT 10sec (10-12 sec), APTT 48sec (30-40 sec), BT 9 min (3-7 min). What is the likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Prolonged APTT (factor VIII deficiency component) and prolonged BT (platelet function/vWF deficiency component) is characteristic of von Willebrand disease.

ai.clinical_core: Von Willebrand Disease (vWD) is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It typically presents with mucosal bleeding (menorrhagia) and shows a prolonged bleeding time and APTT (due to low Factor VIII).

options.ai_explanation: Causes a prolonged BT but the APTT would be normal.
Causes a prolonged APTT but the BT is normal.
Causes a prolonged APTT but the BT is normal.
Causes a prolonged BT but the APTT would be normal.
vWD is the only option that explains both a prolonged APTT and a prolonged bleeding time in a female with menorrhagia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 9 year old boy presented with history of spontaneous onset of swelling of right knee joint due to haemarthosis. Investigation revealed that APTT is prolonged. There is a family history of a maternal uncle who died at 6 years due to a ‘bleeding disorder’ several years ago. What is most likely cause for this haemarthrosis?

db.general_explanation: Spontaneous haemarthrosis is the hallmark of severe haemophilia.

ai.clinical_core: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive disorder. Spontaneous hemarthrosis is the hallmark of severe disease (Factor VIII < 1%).

options.ai_explanation: Bleeding only occurs after significant trauma or surgery.
Bleeding only occurs after significant trauma or surgery.
Bleeding occurs after minor trauma; spontaneous bleeds are rare.
Spontaneous joint bleeds and a family history of early death from bleeding are characteristic of severe hemophilia.
Typically causes mucosal bleeding, not spontaneous hemarthrosis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 40 year old presented with puprpura. Her FBC was WBC – 7 x 103 , Hb 12g/dl ,platelet 4 x 109. Blood picture showed isolated thrombocytophenia with out any other abnormality. What is the best treatment option for this patient.

db.general_explanation: First-line treatment for Idiopathic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) in adults is corticosteroids.

ai.clinical_core: Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) is a diagnosis of exclusion. First-line treatment for symptomatic or severe ITP is corticosteroids.
ai.reference: British Society for Hematology Guidelines on ITP

options.ai_explanation: Second-line immunosuppressant.
Second-line option.
Second-line therapy.
Corticosteroids are the standard first-line treatment for ITP.
Reserved for refractory cases.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are useful in managing in acute bleeding episode in a child with Haemophilia A who has inhibitors to factor viii ?

db.general_explanation: Acute bleeding episodes in patients with inhibitors are treated with very high doses of factor VIII, recombinant factor VIIa, or activated prothrombin complex concentrates.

ai.clinical_core: In hemophilia patients with inhibitors, 'bypassing agents' are used to achieve hemostasis by bypassing the need for Factor VIII or IX.

options.ai_explanation: Contains Factor VIII, which will be neutralized by the inhibitors.
Not useful for Hemophilia A.
Recombinant FVIIa is a standard bypassing agent for patients with inhibitors.
Contains FVIII, which will be neutralized; volume required is too high.
Activated PCC (aPCC) is used as a bypassing agent.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 3-year old boy with haemophilia A presented to the paediatric casualty with haemarthrosis in the right knee joint. What is the most appropriate treatment?

db.general_explanation: Recombinant factor VIII is the appropriate treatment for haemarthrosis in haemophilia A.

ai.clinical_core: Acute hemarthrosis in Hemophilia A requires immediate replacement of the deficient factor (Factor VIII) to achieve hemostasis.

options.ai_explanation: Contains FVIII but is no longer first-line due to infection risks and lower purity.
Contains FVIII but volume required is too high for effective replacement.
Inefficient for factor replacement.
Used for patients with inhibitors, not as first-line for uncomplicated Hemophilia A.
The standard of care for factor replacement in Hemophilia A.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 30 year old woman is followed up in the hematology clinic for immune thrombocytopenia. She is asymptomatic and platelet count is 30,000/mm3. What is the most appropriate management?

db.general_explanation: Management for ITP suggests avoiding treatment in patients with mild, asymptomatic disease where the platelet count is > 30,000/mm3 and they remain asymptomatic.

ai.clinical_core: In Immune Thrombocytopenia (ITP), management is guided by bleeding symptoms rather than the absolute platelet count. For asymptomatic patients with counts >20,000-30,000/mm3, observation is often appropriate.
ai.reference: British Society for Hematology (BSH) Guidelines on ITP

options.ai_explanation: Used as a second-line or third-line therapy, not for asymptomatic patients with stable counts.
Reserved for rapid platelet elevation in cases of severe bleeding or pre-operatively.
Current guidelines (BSH/ASH) recommend observation for asymptomatic patients with platelet counts above 20,000-30,000/mm3.
First-line treatment if therapy is indicated, but not necessary for an asymptomatic patient with a count of 30,000.
Second-line therapy for patients who fail steroids or have chronic ITP requiring treatment.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 10 year old boy with a history of hemophilia A starts bleeding following tooth extraction. What is the first step of management?

db.general_explanation: Standard first-aid for local bleeding, regardless of clotting factor status, is to apply direct pressure to the site of injury.

ai.clinical_core: In any bleeding scenario, local measures should be attempted first. For a hemophiliac, this is followed immediately by factor replacement.

options.ai_explanation: The first step in managing any accessible bleeding is local pressure.
FFP contains too little Factor VIII to be effective without causing fluid overload.
Factor VIII concentrate is preferred over cryoprecipitate.

options.text: D. Infusion of factor VIII concentrate.
options.ai_explanation: This is the definitive treatment, but 'first step' usually implies immediate local action.
Tranexamic acid is a useful adjunct for dental bleeding but not the first step.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 46 year old woman presents with high fever and purpuric rash. Her FBC showed, Hb 8g/dl, WBC 15000/mm3, Platelet 90,000/mm3, PT 20 seconds, APTT 70 seconds, TT 20 seconds. The most likely diagnosis is,

db.general_explanation: The patient shows low Hb and platelets, with increased PT, APTT, and TT. The purpuric rash and coagulopathy are suggestive of Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC), potentially secondary to an infection causing the fever and leukocytosis.

ai.clinical_core: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) is characterized by systemic activation of coagulation, leading to consumption of factors and platelets, resulting in prolonged PT, APTT, TT, and thrombocytopenia.

options.ai_explanation: While AML (especially M3) can cause DIC, the laboratory profile provided is the classic definition of DIC itself.
Aplastic anemia causes pancytopenia but does not typically prolong coagulation times unless there is severe liver involvement or secondary DIC.
Dengue can cause thrombocytopenia and bleeding, but the severe prolongation of all coagulation tests (PT, APTT, TT) points specifically to DIC.
The combination of low platelets and prolongation of PT, APTT, and TT is diagnostic of DIC.
Does not typically present with this severe coagulopathy.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 30-year-old women presented with 3 second trimester miscarriages. On examination she was well. Her full blood count showed a mild thrombocytopenia. The best diagnose is,

db.general_explanation: Anti phospholipid syndrome is an autoimmune disorder causing thrombophilia, recurrent arterial or venous thrombosis, and/or recurrent miscarriages. Recurrent thrombosis results in mild thrombocytopenia.

ai.clinical_core: Antiphospholipid Syndrome (APS) is characterized by recurrent pregnancy loss (especially mid-trimester) and thrombotic events, often accompanied by mild thrombocytopenia.

options.ai_explanation: The combination of recurrent second-trimester miscarriages and thrombocytopenia is classic for APS.
Factor VIII deficiency (Hemophilia A) causes bleeding, not recurrent miscarriages.
While SLE is associated with APS, APS itself is the more direct diagnosis for the obstetric history.
Uterine fibroids can cause miscarriages but do not cause thrombocytopenia.
HIV can cause thrombocytopenia but is not a primary cause of recurrent second-trimester miscarriages.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 13-year-old girl presented with menorrhagia. Here coagulation profile was as follows. APTT : Prolonged, BT : Prolonged, PT : Normal, TT : Normal. What is the most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: vWF deficiency leads to an increase in bleeding time (impaired platelet plug formation) and premature destruction of factor VIII, leading to a prolonged APTT.

ai.clinical_core: vWD is the most likely diagnosis in a young female with menorrhagia, a prolonged APTT (due to low FVIII), and a prolonged bleeding time (due to defective platelet adhesion).

options.ai_explanation: Would show prolonged PT and TT as well.
All routine tests (PT, APTT, TT, BT) are normal.
Causes prolonged APTT but the bleeding time is normal.
Causes prolonged APTT but the bleeding time is normal.
The combination of prolonged APTT and BT is the classic screening pattern for vWD.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 35-year-old woman has easy bruising. The coagulation profile is as follows. PT : 12 seconds (Control 10 - 12 seconds), APTT : 38 seconds (Control 24 - 28 seconds), TT : 12 seconds (Control 10 - 12 seconds), Bleeding time : 12 minutes (Control 2 - 7 minutes). What is / are the likely cause / s?

db.general_explanation: In vWD, vWF deficiency leads to impaired platelet adhesion (prolonged BT) and premature destruction of Factor VIII (prolonged APTT). DIC shows prolonged PT, APTT, and TT. Immune thrombocytopenia affects only BT. Liver disease affects PT, APTT, and TT. Warfarin increases PT.

ai.clinical_core: The coagulation profile shows a prolonged APTT and a prolonged bleeding time, which is the classic pattern for von Willebrand Disease.

options.ai_explanation: DIC would typically show prolonged PT and TT as well.
ITP causes a prolonged BT but the APTT would be normal.
Liver disease typically prolongs the PT first.
vWD causes low Factor VIII (prolonging APTT) and defective platelet adhesion (prolonging BT).
Warfarin primarily prolongs the PT.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 35-year-old man was presented with fever, bruising, bleeding at the venipuncture site. What is the most likely condition? PT : 30 seconds (Control 10 - 12 seconds), APTT : 58 seconds (Control 24 - 48 seconds), TT : 30 seconds (Control 10 - 12 seconds), Fibrinogen : 0.5 g / dL (2 - 4 g / dL)

db.general_explanation: In DIC, there is a reduction in platelet count and fibrinogen. All clotting times (TT, PT, APTT) increase due to consumption of clotting factors and platelets. FDP and D-dimers also increase.

ai.clinical_core: DIC presents with bleeding, bruising, and a laboratory profile showing consumption of all coagulation components (prolonged PT, APTT, TT and low fibrinogen).

options.ai_explanation: Can cause similar labs, but the acute presentation with fever and bruising often points to DIC secondary to sepsis.
The laboratory findings of prolonged PT, APTT, TT and very low fibrinogen are classic for DIC.
Standard coagulation tests (PT, APTT, TT) are normal in Factor XIII deficiency.
Prolongs PT and APTT but does not typically lower fibrinogen or prolong TT.
Only prolongs APTT; PT and TT are normal.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are correct regarding a blood donor?

db.general_explanation: A: False; B: True (18-60 years in Sri Lanka); C: True; D: False; E: False (up to 3 units).

answer_raw: C. The minimum required body weight of a blood donor is 50kg
E. A healthy donor can donate up to 4 whole blood cell units in a calendar year
ai.clinical_core: Blood donor selection criteria ensure the safety of both the donor and the recipient.

options.text: A. Blood donation can be accepted from a person 3 months following dengue fever.
options.ai_explanation: Usually requires a longer deferral period (e.g., 6 months).

options.text: B. The maximum age of a blood donor is 70 years.
options.ai_explanation: In many regions, the upper limit for regular donors is 60-65 years.

options.text: C. The minimum required body weight of a blood donor is 50kg.
options.ai_explanation: Standard requirement to ensure the donor can tolerate the volume loss.

options.text: D. Those with a history of hepatitis B may donate.
options.ai_explanation: History of Hep B is usually a permanent deferral.

options.text: E. A healthy donor can donate up to 4 whole blood cell units in a calendar year
options.ai_explanation: Men can typically donate every 3 months (4 times/year).

subject: BLOOD
stem: A leukoerythroblastic blood picture is seen in

db.general_explanation: Leukoerythroblastic blood picture (nucleated RBC, giant platelets, immature WBC) is seen in AML, CML, osteoporosis, haemolysis, metastases, and primary myelofibrosis.

ai.clinical_core: A leucoerythroblastic picture is caused by marrow replacement (fibrosis, malignancy) or extreme physiological stress.

options.ai_explanation: Shows blasts, but not typically a full leucoerythroblastic picture.
Marrow failure leads to pancytopenia without immature cells.
The classic cause of a leucoerythroblastic blood picture.
Extreme stress can occasionally push immature cells into the blood.
Shows microcytosis, not leucoerythroblastosis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following are the complications of massive blood transfusion?

db.general_explanation: A - Blood coagulation is impaired, since citrate is an anticoagulant. B - Due to hypotension and tissue injury. C - Blood is stored in refrigerated temperatures. D - F. E - Dilution occurs.

ai.clinical_core: Massive transfusion (replacing one blood volume in 24 hours) leads to metabolic and hemostatic complications due to the properties of stored blood and the underlying clinical state.

options.ai_explanation: Citrate used as an anticoagulant in blood bags can bind patient calcium, leading to hypocalcemia.
DIC can be triggered by the underlying trauma or by hemolytic transfusion reactions.
Rapid infusion of large volumes of refrigerated blood can significantly lower the patient's core temperature.
While TACO and TRALI are pulmonary complications, PE is not a direct complication of the transfusion process itself.
Dilutional thrombocytopenia occurs because stored whole blood or packed cells contain few viable platelets.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 69-year-old male presented with tiredness. On examination he was pale with purpuric patches but no hepatosplenomegaly. During history he mentioned he had chemotherapy for a lymphoma 5 years back. What is the most probable reason for his presentation?

db.general_explanation: Aplastic anemia can occur secondarily to chemotherapy. It is characterized by ineffective erythropoiesis and peripheral pancytopenia. Hepatosplenomegaly is absent.

ai.clinical_core: Therapy-related Myelodysplastic Syndrome (t-MDS) or AML is a known late complication of chemotherapy for lymphoma, often presenting with pancytopenia.

options.ai_explanation: Possible, but t-MDS often precedes t-AML and fits the presentation of pancytopenia without blasts mentioned.
Less likely than a secondary myeloid neoplasm after chemotherapy.
The history of chemotherapy followed by pancytopenia (pale, purpura) is highly suggestive of t-MDS.
Usually presents with splenomegaly, which is absent here.
Usually presents with lymphadenopathy, which is not noted.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60 year old man presented with absolute lymphocytosis. His lymphocyte count is 12000mm3. In his blood picture numerous smudge cells are present.

db.general_explanation: Absolute lymphocytosis with numerous smudge cells in a 60-year-old is characteristic of Chronic Lymphocytic Leukaemia (CLL).

ai.clinical_core: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) is characterized by the accumulation of mature-appearing but dysfunctional B-lymphocytes. Smudge cells (basket cells) are a classic finding on the blood film.

options.ai_explanation: Shows atypical lymphocytes (Downey cells), not smudge cells.
Smudge cells are fragile lymphocytes that rupture during film preparation, characteristic of CLL.
Usually diagnosed by lymph node biopsy; peripheral lymphocytosis is rare.
Shows immature blasts, not mature lymphocytes and smudge cells.
Characterized by plasma cells in the marrow, not peripheral lymphocytosis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F regarding CML

db.general_explanation: Philadelphia chromosome is positive in CML. Tyrosine kinase inhibitors (e.g., imatinib) are the treatment. Generalized lymphadenopathy is characteristic of lymphoid, not myeloid leukemia. Neutrophilic alkaline phosphatase is typically decreased. Massive splenomegaly is a classic feature.

answer_raw: A. It is characterized by Philadelphia chromosome
B. It is treated by Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors
ai.clinical_core: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) is a myeloproliferative neoplasm defined by the t(9;22) translocation (Philadelphia chromosome), resulting in the BCR-ABL1 fusion protein. It typically presents with massive splenomegaly and is managed with targeted Tyrosine Kinase Inhibitors (TKIs).

options.text: A. It is characterized by Philadelphia chromosome
options.ai_explanation: The Philadelphia chromosome is the pathognomonic genetic hallmark of CML.

options.text: B. It is treated by Tyrosine Kinase inhibitors
options.ai_explanation: TKIs like imatinib are the first-line standard of care for CML.
Lymphadenopathy is uncommon in the chronic phase of CML; its presence suggests blast crisis.

options.text: D. Neutrophilic Alkaline phosphatase value increased.
options.ai_explanation: In CML, the NAP (or LAP) score is characteristically low or zero.
Splenomegaly is the most common physical finding in CML, often becoming massive.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding cervical lymphadenopathy,

db.general_explanation: A negative Mantoux does not rule out TB (False). Elevated ACE is associated with sarcoidosis (True). Hard nodes suggest malignancy (True). Matted nodes are seen in both TB and malignancy, so are not characteristic solely of lymphoma (False). Tender nodes are often infectious and do not always require immediate biopsy (False).

answer_raw: B. Elevated ACE level will suggest sarcoidosis
C. Hard lymph nodes suggest malignancy
ai.clinical_core: Cervical lymphadenopathy evaluation depends on node consistency, duration, and associated symptoms. Malignancy and chronic infections are key differentials.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Lymphadenopathy Section

options.text: A. A negative Mantoux test will rule out Tuberculosis
options.ai_explanation: A negative Mantoux does not rule out TB, especially in immunocompromised patients or disseminated disease.

options.text: B. Elevated ACE level will suggest sarcoidosis
options.ai_explanation: Serum Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme (ACE) is often elevated in sarcoidosis due to granuloma burden.

options.text: C. Hard lymph nodes suggest malignancy
options.ai_explanation: Hard, fixed, non-tender nodes are highly suspicious for metastatic carcinoma.

options.text: D. Mattered lymph nodes are a characteristic feature of lymphoma
options.ai_explanation: Matted nodes are more characteristic of Tuberculosis (scrofula) or advanced malignancy.

options.text: E. Tender cervical lymphadenopathy should be biopsied immediately
options.ai_explanation: Tender nodes usually indicate an acute inflammatory or infectious process and should be observed or treated with antibiotics first.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 45 year old male presented with loss of weight and malaise. Following are his blood counts. WBC 95,000/mm3, Neutrophils 65%, Eosinophils 4%, Basophils 4%, Monocytes 1%, Lymphocytes 1%, Myeloblasts 20%, M band 2%, Platelets 175,000/mm3, Hb 10 g/dl. Which one of the following investigations best confirms the underlying condition?

db.general_explanation: Symptoms and findings are suggestive of Chronic Myeloid Leukaemia (CML). BCR-ABL is positive in 95% of patients but cannot be fully confirmed by PCR alone. Bone marrow biopsy is used for full confirmation as it covers FISH and morphology.

ai.clinical_core: The blood count shows a massive leukocytosis with a full range of myeloid precursors and 20% blasts, suggesting CML in accelerated phase or blast crisis. PCR for BCR-ABL is the definitive test.

options.ai_explanation: Necessary for staging but PCR is more specific for the molecular diagnosis.
Suggestive but not confirmatory of the molecular driver.
Low in CML, but largely replaced by molecular testing.
Confirms the diagnosis of CML by detecting the fusion gene.
Will show splenomegaly but is not diagnostic of the leukemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding chronic leukaemia

db.general_explanation: Massive splenomegaly is associated with chronic myeloid leukaemia, not chronic lymphocytic leukaemia.

answer_raw: A. A basophilia is present in chronic myeloid leukaemia.
B. Auto immune haemolytic anaemia is seen in chronic lymphositic leukaemia.
E. The presence of Philadelphia chromosome is diagnostic of chronic myeloid leukaemia.
ai.clinical_core: Chronic leukemias (CML and CLL) have distinct clinical and laboratory features.

options.text: A. A basophilia is present in chronic myeloid leukaemia.
options.ai_explanation: Basophilia is a characteristic feature of CML and can signal disease progression.

options.text: B. Auto immune haemolytic anaemia is seen in chronic lymphositic leukaemia.
options.ai_explanation: CLL is frequently complicated by autoimmune cytopenias (AIHA and ITP).

options.text: C. Massive splenomegaly is a diagnostic feature of chronic lymphocytic leukaemia.
options.ai_explanation: Massive splenomegaly is more characteristic of CML or Myelofibrosis.

options.text: D. Myelofibrosis is a diagnostic feature of chronic myeloid leukaemia.
options.ai_explanation: Myelofibrosis can occur in the late stages of CML but is not a diagnostic feature.

options.text: E. The presence of Philadelphia chromosome is diagnostic of chronic myeloid leukaemia.
options.ai_explanation: The t(9;22) translocation (BCR-ABL1) is the defining feature of CML.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60 year old woman presented with lethargy. Hb - 9g/dl WBC 50000/mm3 Platelet 500,000/mm3 Her blood picture shows full spectrum of myeloid cell maturation. Which of the following investigations would best support a diagnosis of chronic myeloid leukemia?

db.general_explanation: The presence of BCR-ABL fusion by PCR method is the gold standard for diagnosis in Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (95% are Philadelphia chromosome positive).

ai.answer_raw: B. Presence of BCR-ABL fusion by PCR method.
ai.clinical_core: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML) is defined by the presence of the Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22), resulting in the BCR-ABL1 fusion gene.

options.text: A. Low Neutrophil Alkaline Phosphatase (NAP) score
options.ai_explanation: Suggestive of CML but not as definitive as genetic testing.

options.text: B. Presence of BCR-ABL fusion by PCR method.
options.ai_explanation: The hallmark and diagnostic requirement for CML.
Common in CML but not diagnostic.
Common in CML but seen in many other conditions.

options.text: E. USS scan evidence of splenomegaly
options.ai_explanation: Common finding but non-specific.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 23yr old woman presented with fever & bleeding from puncture site. On examination she was febrile. Given below is her full blood count & coagulation ptofile. WBC 15,000/mm3 (4,000-11,000), Hb 10.5g/dL, Platelets 50,000. Prothrombin time, APTT & TT are prolonged. Most likely diagnosis,

db.general_explanation: High WBC, increased PT, TT, APTT and reduced platelet count suggests DIC.

ai.clinical_core: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC) is a consumptive coagulopathy characterized by simultaneous microvascular thrombosis and hemorrhage, leading to low platelets and prolongation of all coagulation times (PT, APTT, TT).

options.ai_explanation: The combination of thrombocytopenia and prolongation of PT, APTT, and TT (Thrombin Time) indicates a global consumption of clotting factors and platelets.
Immune Thrombocytopenic Purpura (ITP) presents with isolated thrombocytopenia; coagulation profiles (PT/APTT) are normal.
Platelet function defects (like Glanzmann's) show normal platelet counts and normal PT/APTT.
TTP presents with thrombocytopenia and microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, but coagulation studies (PT/APTT) are typically normal.
vWD usually has a normal platelet count (except Type 2B) and normal PT; only APTT may be prolonged.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 30-year-old women awaiting extraction of a wisdom tooth. She gives a history of easy bruising. Her coagulation profile is as follows: Prothrombin time :12 minutes (10 - 12 minutes), APTT: 50 minutes (10 - 12 minutes), Thrombin time: 12 minutes (10 - 12 minutes), Platelet count: 230,000/ml, Bleeding time: 8 minutes (3 -5 minutes). Which of the following statement best describes the pathogenesis of her condition?

db.general_explanation: APTT is increased, which relates to the intrinsic pathway, and bleeding time is increased, which relates to platelet function.

ai.answer_raw: C. Her intrinsic pathway and platelet function are impaired
ai.clinical_core: von Willebrand Disease (vWD) is the most common inherited bleeding disorder. It affects both platelet adhesion (prolonged BT) and Factor VIII stability (prolonged APTT).

options.text: A. Her intrinsic pathway is impaired.
options.ai_explanation: True (due to low FVIII), but incomplete as it doesn't mention platelets.
PT is normal in vWD.

options.text: C. Her intrinsic pathway and platelet function are impaired
options.ai_explanation: vWF is needed for platelet-subendothelial adhesion and as a carrier for Factor VIII.

options.text: D. Half-life of the intrinsic pathway proteins is reduced
options.ai_explanation: Only the half-life of Factor VIII is reduced.

options.text: E. Her platelet function is impaired
options.ai_explanation: True, but incomplete as it doesn't mention the intrinsic pathway (APTT).

subject: BLOOD
stem: 61 year old patient who is a diabetic patient and a smoker for 31 years presented with deep vein thrombosis. What is the next step in management?

db.general_explanation: Low Molecular Weight Heparin is the standard management for deep vein thrombosis.

ai.answer_raw: D. SUBCUTANEOUS LOW MOLECULAR WEIGHT HEPARIN
ai.clinical_core: Immediate anticoagulation is required for DVT. While DOACs are now often first-line, LMWH remains a standard initial step, especially when certain DOACs (like Dabigatran) require a parenteral lead-in.

options.ai_explanation: Aspirin is an antiplatelet agent and is insufficient for the treatment of venous thromboembolism.
Dabigatran requires at least 5 days of lead-in with a parenteral anticoagulant (like LMWH) before initiation.
Thrombolysis is reserved for massive PE or extensive iliofemoral DVT with limb-threatening ischemia.

options.text: D. SUBCUTANEOUS LOW MOLECULAR WEIGHT HEPARIN
options.ai_explanation: LMWH is the standard of care for the initial treatment of DVT due to its predictable pharmacokinetics.
UFH is typically given intravenously and is reserved for patients with severe renal failure or those needing rapid reversal.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 65-year-old man was admitted to the hospital with high fever and SOB for 7 days. He looks severely ill. Following is his coagulation profile. PT 17 (10-12), APPT 30 (10-12), TT 18 (10-12), Platelets 30,000. What could be the most probable condition he is having?

db.general_explanation: DIC is triggered by sepsis, cancers, trauma, and pregnancy. Blood clots form while simultaneously causing bleeding due to consumption of clotting factors and platelets. Clinical signs include thrombosis (causing SOB) and bleeding (petechiae, ecchymosis, hematuria).

ai.clinical_core: DIC is a systemic activation of coagulation often triggered by sepsis. It is characterized by consumption of platelets and clotting factors, leading to prolonged PT, APTT, and TT.

options.ai_explanation: Can cause thrombocytopenia, but the global prolongation of PT, APTT, and TT in a septic patient is more characteristic of DIC.
Can cause DIC, but DIC is the specific hematological diagnosis for these lab findings.
The combination of prolonged PT, APTT, TT, and severe thrombocytopenia in an acutely ill patient is diagnostic of DIC.
This is the likely clinical trigger, but not the hematological diagnosis.
Sepsis is the underlying cause, but the coagulation profile specifically defines DIC.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Essential nutrients for erythropoiesis includes,

db.general_explanation: Factors necessary for erythropoiesis are Fe2+, B12, Folate, Vit C, pyridoxine, T4, Zn, Androgens, Erythropoietin, Protein.

ai.clinical_core: Erythropoiesis requires various vitamins and minerals for DNA synthesis, heme production, and iron metabolism.

options.ai_explanation: Essential for DNA synthesis and nuclear maturation of RBCs.
Vitamin B6 is a cofactor for the first step of heme synthesis.
Enhances iron absorption in the gut.
Essential for coagulation factor synthesis, not erythropoiesis.
Zinc is a cofactor for several enzymes involved in heme synthesis and cell division.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are true or false regarding folic acid?

db.general_explanation: B12 reserves last 2 years, not folate. Folate deficiency does not cause neurological manifestations.

answer_raw: C. Deficiency is usually due to poor dietary intake
E. It is found in green vegetables
ai.clinical_core: Folic acid is a water-soluble vitamin essential for DNA synthesis. Unlike Vitamin B12, body stores are limited, and deficiency develops rapidly (months) primarily due to poor diet or increased demand.

options.text: A. Body reserves last up to 2 years
options.ai_explanation: Folate stores in the liver only last for about 3-4 months, unlike B12 stores which last years.

options.text: B. Deficiency is known to cause neurological manifestations
options.ai_explanation: Subacute combined degeneration of the cord is specific to B12 deficiency; folate deficiency does not cause these neurological symptoms.

options.text: C. Deficiency is usually due to poor dietary intake
options.ai_explanation: Inadequate intake of fresh vegetables and overcooking food are the most common causes of folate deficiency.

options.text: D. It is absorbed in ileum
options.ai_explanation: Folate is primarily absorbed in the duodenum and jejunum; the ileum is the site for B12-intrinsic factor complex absorption.

options.text: E. It is found in green vegetables
options.ai_explanation: Green leafy vegetables (spinach, broccoli) are rich sources of dietary folate.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following statements are correct regarding Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency?

db.general_explanation: Brewer's test gives false negatives in the acute stage. It is X-linked recessive, not autosomal. Heinz bodies are seen.

answer_raw: C. It is a cause for neonatal jaundice.
ai.clinical_core: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked recessive enzymopathy where red cells are susceptible to oxidative stress, leading to acute episodic hemolysis. Diagnosis is made by enzyme assay, but testing during an acute crisis can yield false negatives as older, enzyme-deficient cells are destroyed first.

options.text: A. Brewer’s test done during an acute period of haemolysis is diagnostic.
options.ai_explanation: During acute hemolysis, the G6PD-deficient older cells are lysed, leaving behind younger reticulocytes with higher enzyme levels, which can lead to a false-negative result.
Oxidative damage leads to the formation of Heinz bodies and subsequent intravascular hemolysis during acute triggers like fava beans or infections.

options.text: C. It is a cause for neonatal jaundice.
options.ai_explanation: G6PD deficiency is a major cause of neonatal hyperbilirubinemia, particularly in certain ethnic groups, and can lead to kernicterus.

options.text: D. It is inherited as an autosomal recessive disease.
options.ai_explanation: G6PD deficiency is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern.

options.text: E. Red cells show characteristic basophilic stippling.
options.ai_explanation: Basophilic stippling is characteristic of lead poisoning or sideroblastic anemia; G6PD deficiency typically shows 'bite cells' and 'blister cells' on the blood film.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Blood picture of Glucose 6 phosphate dehydrogenase deficiency includes

db.general_explanation: A. T (Heinz body nibbled out). B. T (Loss of cytoplasm). C. T (Features of reticulocytosis). D. F. E. F (Spherocytes typical of immune-mediated hemolysis or congenital membrane defects).

ai.clinical_core: G6PD deficiency leads to oxidative stress and hemoglobin denaturation (Heinz bodies). Splenic macrophages 'bite' out these inclusions, creating characteristic red cell shapes.
ai.reference: Dacie and Lewis Practical Hematology, Chapter 5

options.ai_explanation: Formed when the spleen removes Heinz bodies from RBCs.
RBCs where the hemoglobin has contracted to one side, leaving a clear 'blister'.
Reflects the reticulocytosis following an acute hemolytic episode.
Associated with MDS or CML, not G6PD deficiency.
Small spherocytes can form after 'bites' are taken out of the RBC membrane.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding G6PD deficiency,

db.general_explanation: Refer to standard haematology handouts.

answer_raw: A. Bite cells are seen in acute haemolysis.
B. Denatured Hb is best demonstrated by Heinz body preparation.
ai.clinical_core: G6PD deficiency is an X-linked enzymopathy that makes RBCs susceptible to oxidative stress.

options.text: A. Bite cells are seen in acute haemolysis.
options.ai_explanation: Formed by splenic removal of Heinz bodies.

options.text: B. Denatured Hb is best demonstrated by Heinz body preparation.
options.ai_explanation: Supravital stains (like brilliant cresyl blue) are needed to see Heinz bodies.
Severe oxidative stress causes acute intravascular hemolysis.
It is X-linked recessive.
Splenectomy is not indicated as the defect is enzymatic and hemolysis is often episodic and intravascular.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following infection/s cause granulomatous inflammation in the lymph node?

db.general_explanation: These conditions are recognized as infectious granulomatous disorders causing granulomatous lymphadenitis, divided into suppurative (Cat scratch, LGV, Fungal) and non-suppurative (Tuberculosis, Lepra, Syphilis, Fungal) categories.

ai.clinical_core: Granulomatous inflammation is a form of chronic inflammation characterized by collections of macrophages (epithelioid cells).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Pathology Section

options.ai_explanation: Causes stellate necrotizing granulomas.
Fungal infection that causes granulomas.
Causes necrotizing granulomatous lymphadenitis.
Tuberculoid leprosy is characterized by well-formed granulomas.
Tertiary syphilis involves gummas (granulomas).

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 68 year old male presented with pale and generalized lymphadenopathy. His blood picture shows agranulated RBC, nucleated RBC and polychromatic cells. Further investigations revealed positive Coombs test. What is the most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The clinical picture and findings point to Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma.

ai.clinical_core: Generalized lymphadenopathy and a positive Coombs test in an elderly patient suggest a secondary autoimmune hemolytic anemia, most commonly associated with B-cell malignancies.

options.ai_explanation: Less likely in a 68-year-old; usually presents in adolescents.
Possible, but lymphoma is more common in this age group with this presentation.
Does not typically cause AIHA and generalized lymphadenopathy.
NHL often presents with lymphadenopathy and can trigger AIHA.
HS is Coombs negative.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following cause haemorrhagic disease of new-born

db.general_explanation: The answer key indicates all listed conditions are associated with the context provided.

ai.clinical_core: The question likely refers to 'Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn' (HDN) rather than 'Hemorrhagic Disease' (which is Vitamin K deficiency). HDN is caused by RBC destruction.

options.ai_explanation: A common cause of neonatal hemolysis.
Alpha thalassemia trait doesn't usually cause neonatal hemolysis; Hb Bart's (hydrops) is severe.
Beta thalassemia major does not manifest at birth because HbF (alpha2gamma2) is still predominant.
Can cause significant neonatal jaundice and hemolysis.
A known cause of neonatal hemolytic jaundice.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 2-day old neonate is diagnosed with hemolytic disease of the newborn. Which mechanism is most important in this condition?

db.general_explanation: The cause for HDN is Rh incompatibility (Rh isoimmunization) which occurs due to transplacental passage of maternal IgG alloantibodies binding to red cell antigens, forming antigen-antibody complexes.

ai.clinical_core: Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction where maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta and bind to fetal RBC antigens, leading to their destruction.

options.ai_explanation: Maternal IgG coats fetal RBCs, which are then recognized and destroyed by macrophages in the fetal spleen via Fc receptors (ADCC).

options.text: B. Cell destruction by reactive T cells.
options.ai_explanation: This describes Type IV hypersensitivity; HDN is antibody-mediated (Type II).

options.text: C. Formation of antigen antibody complexes.
options.ai_explanation: This describes Type III hypersensitivity (soluble complexes); HDN involves antibodies against cell-surface antigens.

options.text: D. Impaired clearance of immune complexes.
options.ai_explanation: This is a mechanism for systemic lupus erythematosus or other Type III reactions, not HDN.
While cytokines are involved in inflammation, they are not the primary mechanism of RBC destruction in HDN.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 50-year-old present with jaundice. Which of the following investigations favour haemolysis as the cause?

db.general_explanation: Haptoglobulin levels are low in haemolysis. Bile is not typically present in urine; rather, it is urobilinogen.

answer_raw: A. High level of indirect bilirubin
E. Presence of polychromatic cells in peripheral blood
ai.clinical_core: Hemolysis leads to increased RBC breakdown products and a compensatory increase in RBC production.

options.text: A. High level of indirect bilirubin
options.ai_explanation: Unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin is a product of heme breakdown.
Haptoglobin levels decrease in hemolysis as it binds free hemoglobin.
Suggests an immune-mediated cause of hemolysis.

options.text: D. Presence of bile in the urine
options.ai_explanation: Bilirubinuria occurs with conjugated bilirubin (obstructive jaundice).

options.text: E. Presence of polychromatic cells in peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Polychromasia represents reticulocytosis, indicating a marrow response to hemolysis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following investigation is the most specific for the detection of intravascular haemolysis?

db.general_explanation: Urine haemosiderin indicates chronic intravascular haemolysis.

ai.clinical_core: Intravascular hemolysis involves the destruction of RBCs within the circulation, releasing free hemoglobin. This leads to hemoglobinuria and the deposition of iron in renal tubular cells, which is later shed as hemosiderin.
ai.reference: Dacie and Lewis Practical Hematology, Chapter 10

options.ai_explanation: Reticulocytosis is a general marker of increased erythropoiesis seen in both intra- and extravascular hemolysis.
Indirect bilirubin is elevated in all forms of hemolysis and is not specific to the intravascular site.
Urobilinogen increases whenever there is increased bilirubin metabolism, common to all hemolytic anemias.
Urinary hemosiderin is a specific indicator of chronic intravascular hemolysis, reflecting the reabsorption and subsequent shedding of filtered hemoglobin by renal tubules.
Haptoglobin is a very sensitive marker for hemolysis but can be low in both intra- and extravascular hemolysis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following suggest ongoing hemolysis?

db.general_explanation: Free hemoglobin binds to haptoglobin causing serum levels to decrease. Positive direct (not indirect) Coombs test occurs in AIHA. Haem is broken down to bilirubin, resulting in elevated unconjugated bilirubin and increased urine urobilinogen. Hemolysis increases RBC turnover, consuming folate.

ai.clinical_core: Hemolysis is characterized by the premature destruction of red blood cells. Laboratory markers include signs of increased RBC breakdown (low haptoglobin, high unconjugated bilirubin, high LDH, urinary urobilinogen) and signs of increased RBC production (reticulocytosis).

options.ai_explanation: Haptoglobin binds free hemoglobin released during hemolysis and is subsequently cleared, leading to low serum levels.
The indirect Coombs test detects antibodies in the serum; it indicates sensitization but not necessarily active hemolysis.
Heme breakdown from destroyed RBCs leads to increased production of unconjugated bilirubin.
Increased bilirubin metabolism in the gut leads to higher levels of urobilinogen, which is excreted in urine.
Folate levels are typically decreased in chronic hemolysis due to high demand from increased erythropoiesis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 22-year-old presents with haemoglobin level 7 g/dL. Which of the following investigations favour haemolysis?

db.general_explanation: Positive direct Coombs test in autoimmune haemolytic anemia. Haem is broken down to bilirubin resulting in elevated unconjugated bilirubin. Schistocytes suggest red blood cell injury. No RBC are found in urine. Haptoglobin levels decrease in serum.

answer_raw: C. Presence of fragmented red cells in peripheral blood
ai.clinical_core: Hemolysis is the premature destruction of RBCs, which can be intravascular or extravascular.

options.ai_explanation: Indicates immune-mediated hemolysis.
A standard marker of increased heme catabolism.

options.text: C. Presence of fragmented red cells in peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: Schistocytes indicate microangiopathic (intravascular) hemolysis.

options.text: D. Presence of red cells in urine
options.ai_explanation: Hematuria (RBCs in urine) is different from hemoglobinuria (free Hb in urine seen in intravascular hemolysis).
Haptoglobin is depleted as it binds free hemoglobin.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding HDN

db.general_explanation: ABO incompatibility is indeed more common than rhesus isoimmunization. Blood film in ABO incompatibility shows spherocytosis. Treatment includes phototherapy, exchange transfusion, and IV immunoglobulin. Jaundice typically manifests at birth or in the first 24 hours of life. Regarding urine, there is unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia and increased urinary urobilinogen, but it is not bile positive.

answer_raw: A. ABO incompatibility is more common than rhesus isoimmunization
ai.clinical_core: Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) occurs when maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta and destroy fetal red cells. ABO incompatibility is now more common than Rh disease due to anti-D prophylaxis.

options.text: A. ABO incompatibility is more common than rhesus isoimmunization
options.ai_explanation: ABO incompatibility occurs in about 15-20% of pregnancies, though only a small fraction develop clinical HDN.
Spherocytes are a characteristic finding in ABO HDN, distinguishing it from Rh HDN.
Phototherapy is the standard treatment for neonatal jaundice to prevent kernicterus.

options.text: D. Jaundice typically appear in 48 hours of birth
options.ai_explanation: Pathological jaundice in HDN typically appears within the first 24 hours of life.
HDN causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia; unconjugated bilirubin is not water-soluble and does not appear in urine.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 68 year old male presented with pale and generalized lymphadenopathy. His blood picture shows agranulated RBC, nucleated RBC and polychromatic cells. Further investigations revealed positive Coombs test. What is the most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: No specific clinical explanation provided in the text, matched via the answer key.

ai.clinical_core: The presence of generalized lymphadenopathy and a positive Coombs test (Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia) in an elderly patient strongly suggests a lymphoproliferative disorder like Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma or CLL.

options.ai_explanation: Typically affects younger patients and causes a transient, often Coombs-negative anemia.
Can cause AIHA and lymphadenopathy, but is less likely than lymphoma in a 68-year-old male.
Rheumatoid arthritis does not typically cause generalized lymphadenopathy and AIHA.
NHL is a common cause of secondary AIHA (Coombs positive) and generalized lymphadenopathy in the elderly.
HS is a congenital membrane defect; it is Coombs negative and does not cause lymphadenopathy.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 45-year-old man presented with malaise. His FBC revealed Hb – 8 g/dl, WBC – 3,000/mm3, Platelets – 50,000/mm3. He complains of passing tea coloured urine early morning which clears by noon. He has no family history of haemolytic disease. What is the most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The clinical presentation of passing tea-coloured urine in the early morning is characteristic of Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH).

ai.clinical_core: PNH is an acquired stem cell disorder presenting with intravascular hemolysis (hemoglobinuria), often worse at night, and cytopenias.

options.ai_explanation: Causes pancytopenia but not hemoglobinuria.
Does not cause tea-colored urine.
Does not cause hemoglobinuria.
Does not cause hemoglobinuria.
The classic 'tea-colored urine in the morning' is due to nocturnal hemoglobinuria.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 50 year old women presented to the clinic with tiredness .which of the following suggest hemolytic anaemia

db.general_explanation: A. T (identifies ab-related hemolysis). B. T. C. T (Haptoglobin levels reduced). D. T (Urine bile increases with increased conjugated bilirubin). E. T (Hemosiderin in urine from tubular absorption of free Hb).

ai.clinical_core: Hemolytic anemia is characterized by shortened red cell survival. Markers include increased breakdown products and compensatory marrow response.

options.ai_explanation: Suggests autoimmune hemolytic anemia.
This is a normal finding and does not suggest hemolysis.
Haptoglobin binds free hemoglobin; its levels drop in both intra- and extravascular hemolysis.
Hemolysis increases unconjugated bilirubin, which is not water-soluble and does not appear in urine (acholuric jaundice).
A specific marker for chronic intravascular hemolysis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which are lis true / false regarding haemohytrc disease of new born?

db.general_explanation: Causes are Rh incompatibility, ABO incompatibility, other maternal antibodies anti kell/kidd/duffy

answer_raw: B. Minor blood group incompatibility is a known aetrology.
C. Spherocytes are found in blood picture ABO incompatibility.
E. Phototherapy is a treatment methrd.
ai.clinical_core: HDN results from maternal-fetal blood group incompatibility.

options.text: A. Rh incompatibility is more affect 1st born than subsequent born.
options.ai_explanation: Rh disease typically affects subsequent pregnancies after sensitization.

options.text: B. Minor blood group incompatibility is a known aetrology.
options.ai_explanation: Antigens like Kell, Duffy, and Kidd can cause HDN.

options.text: C. Spherocytes are found in blood picture ABO incompatibility.
options.ai_explanation: Characteristic finding in ABO HDN.

options.text: D. Direct bilirubin increase more than indirect bilirubin
options.ai_explanation: HDN causes unconjugated (indirect) hyperbilirubinemia.

options.text: E. Phototherapy is a treatment methrd.
options.ai_explanation: Standard treatment for neonatal jaundice.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 2-day old neonate is diagnosed with hemolytic disease of the newborn. Which mechanism is most important in this condition?

db.general_explanation: HDN is typically caused by Rh isoimmunization; transplacental passage of maternal IgG alloantibodies binds to red cell antigens, forming antigen-antibody complexes.

ai.clinical_core: Hemolytic disease of the newborn is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction where maternal IgG antibodies bind to fetal RBC antigens, leading to their destruction.

options.ai_explanation: Maternal antibodies opsonize fetal RBCs, which are then destroyed by splenic macrophages (Type II hypersensitivity).

options.text: B. Cell destruction by reactive T cells.
options.ai_explanation: This describes Type IV hypersensitivity, not HDN.

options.text: C. Formation of antigen antibody complexes.
options.ai_explanation: This describes Type III hypersensitivity.

While cytokines are involved in inflammation, they are not the primary mechanism of RBC destruction in HDN.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which combination causes severe ABO incompatible hemolytic disease in newborn?

db.general_explanation: ABO incompatibility occurs most significantly when the mother is blood group O. Group O mothers possess both Anti-A and Anti-B IgG antibodies which can cross the placenta. If the baby is blood group A (possessing A antigens) or blood group B (possessing B antigens), these antibodies can cause hemolysis.

ai.answer_raw: D. Mother O+ and baby A-
