ai.clinical_core: ABO Hemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN) almost exclusively occurs in infants of Group O mothers, as they possess IgG anti-A and anti-B antibodies that can cross the placenta.

options.ai_explanation: Mother has no anti-A or anti-B that would react with the child.
Child has no A or B antigens.

options.text: C. Mother AB+ and baby A+
options.ai_explanation: Mother has no anti-A or anti-B.

options.text: D. Mother O+ and baby A-
options.ai_explanation: Group O mothers have IgG anti-A antibodies that can cause hemolysis in a Group A baby.

options.text: E. Mother O+ and baby B+
options.ai_explanation: While this can cause HDN, Mother O with Baby A is statistically more common and often more severe in clinical scenarios.

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F Regarding Hemophilia A:

db.general_explanation: Correct answers: X-linked (T), Factor IX deficiency (F), Inhibitor formation (T), Tranexamic acid (T - anti fibrinolytic), Prophylactic factor replacement (T)

answer_raw: D. Tranexamic acid can be used as treatment
E. Can be prophylactically treated with factor replacement.
ai.clinical_core: Hemophilia A is an X-linked recessive deficiency of Factor VIII. Management involves factor replacement (prophylactic or on-demand), antifibrinolytics for mucosal bleeds, and monitoring for neutralizing antibodies (inhibitors).

options.ai_explanation: Hemophilia A and B both follow an X-linked recessive inheritance pattern.
Hemophilia A is Factor VIII deficiency; Factor IX deficiency is Hemophilia B (Christmas Disease).
About 25-30% of patients with severe Hemophilia A develop inhibitory antibodies against infused Factor VIII.

options.text: D. Tranexamic acid can be used as treatment
options.ai_explanation: Tranexamic acid is a useful adjunct, especially for dental extractions or mucosal bleeding.

options.text: E. Can be prophylactically treated with factor replacement.
options.ai_explanation: Prophylactic factor replacement is the standard of care to prevent joint bleeds (hemarthrosis) and subsequent arthropathy.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 2-year-old boy was brought to the hospital due to right sided knee joint swelling following a fall. He was afebrile and in pain. The joint was warm and movements restricted. Investigations revealed a prolonged APTT. What is the best investigation which will help you to confirm the diagnosis

db.general_explanation: Factor VIII assay will confirm hemophilia A.

ai.clinical_core: Hemophilia A presents with hemarthrosis and a prolonged APTT. Diagnosis is confirmed by showing correction with normal plasma and a specific deficiency in Factor VIII.

options.ai_explanation: Used to quantify inhibitors, not for the initial diagnosis of hemophilia.
A mixing study (factor correction) helps determine if the prolonged APTT is due to a deficiency or an inhibitor.
Specific factor assays are required to confirm the diagnosis and determine severity.
Platelet function is normal in hemophilia.
Thrombin time is normal in hemophilia as it measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following components are essential for normal haemopoiesis?

db.general_explanation: Erythropoietin, Granulocyte colony stimulating factor, Thrombopoietin, and Transforming growth factor β are essential components; Tumour necrosis factor is not.

ai.clinical_core: Hemopoiesis is regulated by specific growth factors that stimulate the proliferation and differentiation of hematopoietic stem cells into mature blood cells.

options.ai_explanation: EPO is the primary hormone stimulating red blood cell production.
G-CSF is essential for the production and release of neutrophils.
TPO is the primary regulator of megakaryocyte differentiation and platelet production.
TGF-beta is generally an inhibitor of hematopoietic stem cell proliferation.
TNF is a pro-inflammatory cytokine that typically suppresses normal hemopoiesis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Sites of haemopoiesis include

db.general_explanation: Haemopoiesis occurs in the blood islands of the yolk sac, bone marrow, foetal liver, and foetal spleen.

answer_raw: A. Blood islands of the yolk sac
ai.clinical_core: Hematopoiesis occurs in stages: Mesoblastic (yolk sac), Hepatic (liver and spleen), and Medullary (bone marrow). By birth, the bone marrow is the primary site.

options.text: A. Blood islands of the yolk sac
options.ai_explanation: This is the site of the earliest (mesoblastic) stage of hematopoiesis in the embryo.
The bone marrow becomes the main site of hematopoiesis from the second trimester onwards and is the sole site in healthy adults.
The liver is the predominant site of hematopoiesis during the second trimester.
The lungs are not a recognized site of physiological hematopoiesis.
The spleen contributes to hematopoiesis during the hepatic stage (approx. 3-7 months of gestation).

subject: BLOOD
stem: T/F regarding the haemopoiesis,

db.general_explanation: Adult heamoglobin molecule has 2 alpha & 2 beta chains. Erythropoietin is a glycoprotein; a metalloprotein contains a metal iron cofactor. Erythropoietin is produced by the liver/kidney due to a hypoxic stimulus. Primitive heamatopoesis begins in the blood islands of the yolk sac. Factors necessary for erythropoesis include Fe2+, B12, Folate, Vit C, Pyridoxine, T4, Zn, Androgens, and Erythropoietin.

answer_raw: C. Hypoxia is a potent stimulator of erythropoesis
D. The most primitive organ of heamopoesis in embryogenesis are blood islands.
E. Zinc is a micronutrient factor that favours heamopoesis.
ai.clinical_core: Hematopoiesis is the process of blood cell production, regulated by hormones like EPO and requiring various micronutrients.

options.text: A. Adult heamoglobin molecule has 2 alpha & 2 gamma chains.
options.ai_explanation: Adult Hb (HbA) has 2 alpha and 2 beta chains; 2 alpha and 2 gamma is fetal Hb (HbF).
EPO is a glycoprotein.

options.text: C. Hypoxia is a potent stimulator of erythropoesis
options.ai_explanation: Hypoxia induces EPO production in the kidneys.

options.text: D. The most primitive organ of heamopoesis in embryogenesis are blood islands.
options.ai_explanation: Hematopoiesis begins in the yolk sac blood islands.

options.text: E. Zinc is a micronutrient factor that favours heamopoesis.
options.ai_explanation: Zinc is involved in many enzymatic processes required for cell division and growth.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is / are essential for haemopoiesis?

db.general_explanation: Cell adhesion molecules (CAMs) hold stem cells to the stroma, hence it is required for receiving nutrients from the stroma. Factors necessary for erythropoiesis are: Heme – Fe, Globin – protein, 4 vitamins – B6 (Pyridoxine), B9 (Folic acid), B12, C (Ascorbic acid), 3 Hormones – Erythropoietin, T4, Androgens, Cortisol, Growth hormone, Zn, Co, Amino acids.

ai.clinical_core: Hematopoiesis is regulated by hormones, vitamins, minerals, and the marrow microenvironment.

options.ai_explanation: Androgens stimulate erythropoietin production and have direct effects on marrow precursors.
Essential for the interaction between hematopoietic stem cells and the marrow niche.
Required for heme synthesis.
Thyroid hormones are necessary for normal metabolic activity of the marrow.
A cofactor for many enzymes involved in nucleic acid synthesis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 35-year-old primi with blood group A Rh negative is fond to have a post partum haemorrhage. There is no group A Rh negative blood in the blood bank. Which of the following is the next most compatible?

db.general_explanation: No specific text explanation provided.

ai.clinical_core: In emergency situations where the patient's specific blood group is unavailable, O Rh-negative blood is used as the universal donor for red cells because it lacks A, B, and RhD antigens.

options.ai_explanation: O negative is the universal donor and the safest choice when A negative is unavailable.
Rh positive blood should be avoided in Rh negative females of childbearing age to prevent sensitization.
Rh positive blood can cause Rh sensitization in an Rh negative patient.
AB positive blood contains A and B antigens and RhD, making it incompatible with an A negative recipient.
AB negative blood contains the B antigen, which would react with the anti-B antibodies in a Group A recipient.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following statements are correct regarding haemostasis?

db.general_explanation: Fibrinogen is factor I. Vitamin K dependent factors are 2, 7, 9, 10. Coagulation factor deficiency typically presents with haematoma/haemarthrosis rather than petechiae.

answer_raw: A. Factor x is in the common pathway
D. Platelet plug formation requires the presence of von Willebrand’s factor.
ai.clinical_core: Hemostasis involves a complex interplay between platelets, vessel walls, and coagulation factors.

options.text: A. Factor x is in the common pathway
options.ai_explanation: The common pathway begins with the activation of Factor X.

options.text: B. Fibrinogen is a vitamin K dependent clotting factor
options.ai_explanation: Vitamin K dependent factors are II, VII, IX, and X.

options.text: C. Petechiae is a feature of coagulation factor deficiency
options.ai_explanation: Petechiae are characteristic of platelet disorders; factor deficiencies cause deep tissue bleeds and hemarthroses.

options.text: D. Platelet plug formation requires the presence of von Willebrand’s factor.
options.ai_explanation: vWF is essential for platelet adhesion to the subendothelium.

options.text: E. Warfarin therapy leads to prolongation of thrombin time
options.ai_explanation: Warfarin prolongs the PT; the TT measures the conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin and is unaffected by warfarin.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are true regarding Von Willebrand factor?

db.general_explanation: VWF increases in the 3rd trimester of pregnancy, occurs as a multimer, and facilitates links between platelets and endothelium.

answer_raw: A. It forms large molecules in Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.
D. Make links between platelets and endothelium.
ai.clinical_core: Von Willebrand Factor (vWF) is a large multimeric glycoprotein essential for platelet adhesion and carrying Factor VIII.

options.text: A. It forms large molecules in Thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura.
options.ai_explanation: In TTP, deficiency of ADAMTS13 leads to 'ultra-large' vWF multimers that cause microthrombi.
vWF is an acute phase reactant and increases significantly during pregnancy.
vWF circulates as a series of multimers of varying sizes.

options.text: D. Make links between platelets and endothelium.
options.ai_explanation: vWF acts as a bridge between platelet GPIb receptors and subendothelial collagen.

options.text: E. Vitamin K is essential for its production.
options.ai_explanation: vWF is produced in endothelial cells and megakaryocytes; it is not Vitamin K dependent.

subject: BLOOD
stem: True or false regarding measures of hemostasis?

db.general_explanation: PT is part of the extrinsic pathway and is affected by chronic liver disease. Heparin affects APTT. Warfarin does not affect TT. Clotting screening tests are normal in factor XIII deficiency. In DIC, clotting factors are consumed and PT, APTT, and TT are all prolonged.

answer_raw: A. Prolonged PT can be seen in chronic liver cell disease.
B. APTT is prolonged in heparin therapy
D. TT normal in factor XIII deficiency
ai.clinical_core: Coagulation tests measure different parts of the cascade. PT (extrinsic/common), APTT (intrinsic/common), and TT (fibrinogen conversion).

options.text: A. Prolonged PT can be seen in chronic liver cell disease.
options.ai_explanation: The liver synthesizes most clotting factors; PT is a sensitive marker of liver function.

options.text: B. APTT is prolonged in heparin therapy
options.ai_explanation: Unfractionated heparin is monitored using the APTT.

options.text: C. Warfarin treatment can increase the TT
options.ai_explanation: Warfarin affects Factors II, VII, IX, and X; it does not affect the Thrombin Time.

options.text: D. TT normal in factor XIII deficiency
options.ai_explanation: Factor XIII acts after fibrin formation; therefore, PT, APTT, and TT are all normal.
TT is usually prolonged in DIC due to hypofibrinogenemia and interference by FDPs.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding haemostasis and thrombosis

db.general_explanation: A) Haemophilia A is X-linked recessive. B) D-dimers are seen in DIC. C) Inherited causes of Thrombophilia include Protein C, S, and Anti-thrombin deficiency. D) Clinical features include bleeding following medical interventions. E) Prothrombin time is normal in haemolytic jaundice.

ai.answer_raw: B. D-dimers can be seen in DIC
ai.clinical_core: Hemostasis involves platelets and coagulation factors. DIC involves widespread fibrin formation and subsequent fibrinolysis, producing D-dimers.

options.text: A. Prolonged bleeding time can be seen in haemophelia A
options.ai_explanation: Bleeding time measures platelet function, which is normal in Hemophilia A.

options.text: B. D-dimers can be seen in DIC
options.ai_explanation: D-dimers are cross-linked fibrin degradation products, elevated in DIC.

options.text: C. Deficiency of protein c ia an inherited cause of thrombophilia
options.ai_explanation: Protein C is a natural anticoagulant; its deficiency is a known thrombophilic state.

options.text: D. Factor viii deficiency cause slow wound heeling
options.ai_explanation: Factor XIII deficiency is more specifically associated with poor wound healing.

options.text: E. Haemolitc jaundice leads to elevated prothrombin level
options.ai_explanation: Hemolytic jaundice does not affect prothrombin levels; obstructive jaundice might (via Vitamin K malabsorption).

subject: BLOOD
stem: 45 year old man presents with fever cough and SOB. He noticed passing dark colored urine and yellowish discoloration of his eyes. His blood picture was reported of having numerous red cell agglutinations. What is the most probable cause for this condition?

db.general_explanation: Passing dark urine and jaundice suggest hemolytic anemia. Red cell agglutination suggests IgM-mediated cold AIHA. Symptoms of fever, cough, and SOB point to Mycoplasma pneumonia.

ai.clinical_core: Cold Agglutinin Disease (CAD) is a form of autoimmune hemolytic anemia caused by IgM antibodies. It often follows Mycoplasma pneumoniae infection, presenting with respiratory symptoms, jaundice, and red cell agglutination on the blood film.

options.ai_explanation: EBV can cause cold agglutinin disease (anti-i specificity), but the respiratory presentation (fever, cough, SOB) strongly points to Mycoplasma.
HCV is associated with mixed cryoglobulinemia, not typically acute cold agglutinin hemolysis.
HIV seroconversion presents as a mononucleosis-like illness but not typically with acute cold-induced hemolysis.
Mycoplasma pneumoniae is the classic cause of secondary cold agglutinin disease (anti-I specificity) following a respiratory infection.
While NHL can cause chronic cold agglutinin disease, the acute respiratory symptoms make Mycoplasma the most probable cause in this scenario.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 50-year-old man presented with icterus and passing dark urine. His hemoglobin level was 7g/dl. What are the investigations that confirm he is having hemolytic anemia?

db.general_explanation: Hemolytic anemia features include elevated reticulocytes, elevated urine urobilinogen, and reduced haptoglobin (binds free Hb).

ai.clinical_core: Hemolytic anemia is confirmed by evidence of increased RBC destruction and compensatory increased production.

options.ai_explanation: Indicates increased erythropoiesis in response to RBC loss.
Increased heme breakdown leads to more urobilinogen in the gut and urine.

options.text: C. Presence of bile salts in urine.
options.ai_explanation: Bile salts are seen in obstructive jaundice, not hemolysis.
A sensitive marker for intravascular and significant extravascular hemolysis.

options.text: E. Conjugated Bilirubin of 4 mg/dl in serum.
options.ai_explanation: Hemolysis causes unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 6 year old boy is investigated for jaundice. Which of the following is most useful in confirming a diagnosis of hereditary spherocytosis in this patient?

db.general_explanation: Hereditary spherocytosis = Negative Coombs + Spherocytosis + Family history.

ai.answer_raw: D. Spherocytes on the blood film and negative Coombs test
ai.clinical_core: Hereditary Spherocytosis (HS) is a red cell membrane defect. Diagnosis relies on morphology and ruling out immune causes (AIHA).

options.ai_explanation: Nonspecific; seen in all hemolytic anemias.

options.text: B. Anaemia and right shift in the osmotic fragility tests
options.ai_explanation: HS shows an *increased* osmotic fragility (left shift).

options.text: C. Spherocytes on the blood film and indirect hyperbilirubinaemia
options.ai_explanation: Suggestive, but spherocytes are also seen in AIHA.

options.text: D. Spherocytes on the blood film and negative Coombs test
options.ai_explanation: The presence of spherocytes with a negative Coombs test (DAT) strongly supports a non-immune cause like HS.

options.text: E. Spherocytes on the blood film and raised LDH
options.ai_explanation: LDH is a general marker of hemolysis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following mediator is the most important in immediate response during type I hypersensitivity?

db.general_explanation: Histamine is the primary mediator released from mast cells responsible for the immediate phase.

ai.clinical_core: Type I hypersensitivity involves IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation. The immediate response (within minutes) is driven by pre-formed mediators stored in granules, primarily histamine, which causes vasodilation and smooth muscle contraction.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Histamine is a pre-formed mediator released immediately upon mast cell degranulation.
Leukotrienes are synthesized de novo from arachidonic acid and contribute more to the late-phase response.
PAF is a secondary mediator synthesized after activation, not the primary immediate mediator.
Prostaglandins are lipid mediators synthesized de novo, appearing after the initial histamine release.
TNF-alpha can be pre-formed in mast cells but histamine is the most important mediator for the classic immediate symptoms.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Antibody mediated hypersensitivity reactions include

db.general_explanation: Type II hypersensitivity involves antibody-mediated damage (ABO incompatibility, Goodpasture syndrome). B and C are Type I.

ai.clinical_core: Hypersensitivity reactions Types I, II, and III are mediated by antibodies (IgE, IgG, IgM). Type IV is cell-mediated.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Type II hypersensitivity mediated by IgM/IgG antibodies against RBC antigens.
Type I hypersensitivity mediated by IgE.
Type I hypersensitivity mediated by IgE.
Type II hypersensitivity mediated by antibodies against the glomerular basement membrane.
Type IV (delayed-type) hypersensitivity mediated by T-cells.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 35 year old male presented with cellulitis complains shortness of breath, faintness and pleuritis after IV antibiotic. What are the initial management of this patient

db.general_explanation: Anaphylaxis initial management includes adrenaline IM and oxygen support.

answer_raw: A. Give high flow oxygen via face mask
ai.clinical_core: Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency. Immediate management includes removing the trigger, giving IM adrenaline, and providing respiratory/circulatory support.

options.text: A. Give high flow oxygen via face mask
options.ai_explanation: Essential to maintain oxygenation during respiratory distress.
Adrenaline is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.

options.text: C. Position the patient with 30head-up tilt
options.ai_explanation: Patients should be laid flat with legs elevated to treat hypotension.
Oral steroids are too slow; IV hydrocortisone is used as a second-line adjunct.
Normal saline (crystalloid) is preferred for volume expansion in anaphylactic shock.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 22-year-old presented with multiple spontaneous bruises. She gave no history of a recent viral infection. On examination she had no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Her platelet count was found to be 35,000 mm3. Her coagulation profile, full blood count and blood picture were normal except for the thrombocytopenia.

db.general_explanation: Clinical presentation is consistent with ITP.

ai.clinical_core: Isolated thrombocytopenia in an otherwise healthy young woman with no other abnormalities on the blood film or coagulation profile is the classic presentation of ITP.

options.ai_explanation: TTP would show schistocytes on the blood film and signs of hemolysis.
DIC would show a prolonged coagulation profile.
ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion for isolated thrombocytopenia.
Aplastic anemia would show pancytopenia (low Hb and WBC as well).
vWD typically has a normal platelet count but a prolonged bleeding time.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 6 year old girl presented with multiple ecchymotic patches for 3 days duration. She is afebrile and looks well. Physical examination is unremarkable except ecchymosis. Her full blood count reveals Hb level- 12g/dl, WBC- 9000/mm3 and PLT- 25000/mm3. Blood film reveals low platelet count and large platelets. What’s the most appropriate next step in the management?

db.general_explanation: She has ITP. In children, acute disease usually remits spontaneously. Treatment is typically started when platelet count is <10,000/mm3 or if there is major bleeding. As she is clinically well, observation is appropriate.

ai.clinical_core: Acute ITP in children is often a self-limiting condition. Management is based on bleeding severity; for those with only skin manifestations (ecchymosis), observation is preferred regardless of the count.

options.text: A. Bone marrow aspiration and biopsy
options.ai_explanation: Not necessary in a typical presentation of childhood ITP unless the diagnosis is in doubt.
Reserved for severe bleeding or very low counts with high risk.
Can be used but observation is preferred for 'wet' vs 'dry' purpura assessment in children.
Most children with ITP and only skin symptoms recover spontaneously and do not require active treatment.
Contraindicated in ITP unless there is life-threatening hemorrhage, as platelets are rapidly destroyed.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 50yr old man present with mild pallor and mild icteric he is suspect on having immune hemolytic anaemia what are the supportive diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: A. F (MCV should be in normal range 75-95fL). B. T. C. F. D. T (due to partial phagocytosis). E. T (mild splenomegaly).

answer_raw: D. Spherocytes present in blood picture.
ai.clinical_core: Autoimmune Hemolytic Anemia (AIHA) is characterized by antibody-mediated destruction of RBCs, often resulting in spherocytosis and a positive Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT).

options.ai_explanation: MCV is usually high (>100 fl) due to reticulocytosis, but >50 is too low a threshold to be specific.
The Direct Coombs test (DAT) detects antibodies or complement on the RBC surface.

options.text: C. Serum ferritin less than 20 (30-150 normal)
options.ai_explanation: Ferritin is usually normal or high in hemolysis as iron is recycled.

options.text: D. Spherocytes present in blood picture.
options.ai_explanation: Partial phagocytosis of antibody-coated RBCs in the spleen results in spherocytes.
The spleen is the primary site of RBC destruction in warm AIHA.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following drugs are indicated as the first line treatment of immune thrombocytopenia?

db.general_explanation: First-line treatment includes IV Ig, IV Anti-D, and corticosteroids. Second-line: Splenectomy, Rituximab, TRA. Emergency: IV Ig, Methyl prednisolone, Dexamethasone, Plt transfusion.

ai.clinical_core: First-line treatment for ITP includes corticosteroids and IVIG. Methylprednisolone is also used in urgent settings.

options.ai_explanation: A second-line immunosuppressant.
A standard first-line therapy, especially when a rapid rise in platelets is needed.
While a steroid, oral prednisolone or high-dose dexamethasone are the standard 'first-line' steroid choices.
A second-line therapy.
Corticosteroids (Prednisolone/Dexamethasone) are the primary first-line treatment.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Passive immunization and active immunization both are given together for,

db.general_explanation: Combined immunization is used for post-exposure prophylaxis in Tetanus and Rabies.

answer_raw: B. A suspected case of tetanus infection
C. A non-immune person exposed to hepatitis B
D. A person bitten by a rabid dog
ai.clinical_core: Post-exposure prophylaxis often requires both immediate passive immunity (immunoglobulins) to neutralize the pathogen and active immunity (vaccination) to provide long-term protection.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Infectious Diseases

options.text: A. A patient diagnosed with chickenpox
options.ai_explanation: Treatment for active chickenpox involves antivirals; passive/active immunization is used for exposure in high-risk individuals, not typically together for a diagnosed case.

options.text: B. A suspected case of tetanus infection
options.ai_explanation: Tetanus-prone wounds in non-immunized individuals require both Tetanus Immunoglobulin (TIG) and the Tetanus toxoid vaccine.

options.text: C. A non-immune person exposed to hepatitis B
options.ai_explanation: Post-exposure prophylaxis for Hep B involves both Hepatitis B Immunoglobulin (HBIG) and the Hep B vaccine series.

options.text: D. A person bitten by a rabid dog
options.ai_explanation: Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis requires both Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) and the rabies vaccine.

options.text: E. A person exposed to measles
options.ai_explanation: Post-exposure management usually involves either the MMR vaccine (within 72 hours) or Immunoglobulin (within 6 days), but they are not typically administered together as a standard protocol.

subject: BLOOD
stem: What is the characteristic feature that differentiate between live attenuated and killed vaccines?

db.general_explanation: Live attenuated vaccines mimic natural infection, often providing stronger, longer-lasting immunity.

ai.clinical_core: Live attenuated vaccines contain weakened pathogens that replicate in the host, providing a robust immune response similar to natural infection.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Live vaccines replicate and stimulate both humoral and cellular immunity, closely mimicking a natural infection.
Both live and killed vaccines stimulate antibody production.
While side effects differ, this is not the defining characteristic.
Killed vaccines generally require more boosters, but this is a consequence, not the primary differentiating feature.
Both can be given via various routes (oral, IM, etc.).

subject: BLOOD
stem: Primary immune deficiency,

db.general_explanation: Primary immunodeficiency features include early presentation (FTT/diarrhea), autoimmune associations, and often require IVIG.

answer_raw: B. Is suspected in a 3 month year old baby with a chronic diarrhoea and failure to thrive
D. Is associate with autoimmune disease
E. An indication for IV immunoglobin therapy
ai.clinical_core: Primary immunodeficiencies (PIDs) are rare genetic disorders. They often present in infancy with severe infections and are paradoxically associated with autoimmunity.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.text: A. Is commoner than secondary immuno deficiency
options.ai_explanation: Secondary immunodeficiencies (due to HIV, drugs, malnutrition) are far more common than PIDs.

options.text: B. Is suspected in a 3 month year old baby with a chronic diarrhoea and failure to thrive
options.ai_explanation: This is a classic presentation for Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID).

options.text: C. Due to antibody deficiency lead to recurrent viral infections
options.ai_explanation: Antibody deficiency primarily leads to recurrent sinopulmonary infections with encapsulated bacteria.

options.text: D. Is associate with autoimmune disease
options.ai_explanation: Immune dysregulation in PIDs often leads to autoimmune manifestations (e.g., in CVID).

options.text: E. An indication for IV immunoglobin therapy
options.ai_explanation: IVIG is the standard of care for primary antibody deficiencies.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is the most important anti- viral mechanism against a viral infected cell?

db.general_explanation: Cytotoxic T cells are the primary effectors for clearing intracellular viral infections.

ai.clinical_core: Once a virus has infected a host cell, it is sequestered from antibodies. The primary mechanism for eliminating these intracellular pathogens is the recognition of viral peptides on MHC Class I by CD8+ Cytotoxic T cells, which then induce apoptosis in the infected cell.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Complement is more effective against extracellular bacteria or free virus particles, not intracellularly infected cells.
CD8+ T cells are the definitive mechanism for killing cells that have already been infected by viruses.
Interferons induce an antiviral state in neighboring uninfected cells but are not the primary mechanism for killing the already infected cell.
Secretory IgA prevents viral attachment at mucosal surfaces (neutralization) but cannot clear infected cells.
IgM is effective for neutralizing free virus in the blood but cannot reach the intracellular compartment.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which one of the following functions of macrophage best describes its role in mediating adoptive immune response?

db.general_explanation: Antigen presentation via MHC is the key bridge between innate and adaptive immunity.

ai.answer_raw: B. Presenting antigens displayed with MHC molecule
ai.clinical_core: Macrophages bridge innate and adaptive immunity by acting as professional antigen-presenting cells (APCs).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: This is an innate function.

options.text: B. Presenting antigens displayed with MHC molecule
options.ai_explanation: This is the essential step for activating T-lymphocytes in the adaptive response.

options.text: C. Recognition of pathogen associated molecular patterns of microbes
options.ai_explanation: This is an innate function.
Cytokines are secreted in both innate and adaptive responses.
Primarily involved in tissue repair, not the adaptive immune response.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Live attenuated vaccines are used in the prevention of,

db.general_explanation: Live attenuated vaccines include Varicella (Chickenpox) and BCG (Tuberculosis).

ai.clinical_core: Live attenuated vaccines use a weakened form of the germ. Examples include MMR, BCG, and Varicella.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: The varicella vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine.
The Hep B vaccine is a recombinant subunit vaccine.
Modern pertussis vaccines are acellular (subunit).
Pneumococcal vaccines are polysaccharide or conjugate vaccines.
The BCG vaccine is a live attenuated strain of Mycobacterium bovis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: T lymphocytes,

db.general_explanation: T cells are MHC restricted, require cytokines for activation, and need antigen presentation.

answer_raw: C. Needed cytokines for their activation
D. Need antigen presentation by antigen presenting cell
E. They can identify complex lipid antigens
ai.clinical_core: T cells recognize antigens presented by MHC molecules on APCs. They require co-stimulation and cytokines for full activation.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Complement is activated by antibodies (classical) or directly by pathogens (alternative/lectin).
CD4+ cells are MHC II restricted; CD8+ cells are MHC I restricted.

options.text: C. Needed cytokines for their activation
options.ai_explanation: Cytokines like IL-2 are essential for T cell proliferation and differentiation.

options.text: D. Need antigen presentation by antigen presenting cell
options.ai_explanation: T cells cannot recognize 'naked' antigens; they must be processed and presented.

options.text: E. They can identify complex lipid antigens
options.ai_explanation: Certain T cells (NKT cells) recognize lipid antigens presented by CD1 molecules.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A primary immune deficiency is suspected in

db.correct_answer_raw: A) T B) T C) F D) T E) T
db.general_explanation: A) F - Oral candidiasis fungal infection; B) T - B and T cells take time to develop; C) F - Causes for recurrent UTI are typically anatomical (stones, etc); D) T - Weakened immune system can lead to recurrent pneumonia; E) T - Pneumocystis carinii is a cause of diffuse pneumonia in immunocompromised hosts.

answer_raw: B. 9 month old boy with slowly resolving pneumonia.
D. A 2 year old boy with 3 episodes of bacterial pneumonias within 6 months.
E. A 3 year old girl with Pneumocystis jeroveci pneumonia
ai.clinical_core: Primary Immunodeficiencies (PID) are suspected when infections are unusually frequent, severe, persistent, or caused by opportunistic organisms.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 18; Jeffrey Modell Foundation Criteria

options.text: A. 3 month old baby having oral thrush.
options.ai_explanation: Mild oral thrush is common in healthy infants; it only suggests PID if it is refractory, severe, or associated with other signs.

options.text: B. 9 month old boy with slowly resolving pneumonia.
options.ai_explanation: Slowly resolving or 'unusual' progression of common infections is a warning sign for PID.

options.text: C. A 28 year old woman with recurrent lower urinary tract infections.
options.ai_explanation: Recurrent UTIs in adult women are usually due to anatomical or behavioral factors, not PID.

options.text: D. A 2 year old boy with 3 episodes of bacterial pneumonias within 6 months.
options.ai_explanation: Two or more serious sinopulmonary infections within a year is a major red flag for PID.

options.text: E. A 3 year old girl with Pneumocystis jeroveci pneumonia
options.ai_explanation: PJP is an opportunistic infection that strongly indicates a T-cell or combined immunodeficiency.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following are the T lymphocyte subsets which are involved in immune mediated renal cell destruction?

db.correct_answer_raw: A) T B) T C) T D) ... E) F
db.general_explanation: A) T - TH1 cells produce TNF/IFN-gamma and activate macrophages (Type IV hypersensitivity). B) T - TH2 activate eosinophils and basophils. C) T. E) F - T-reg cells suppress autoreactive T cells.

ai.clinical_core: Cell-mediated immunity in the kidney, such as in transplant rejection or certain glomerulonephritides, is primarily driven by TH1 cytokines and direct cell lysis by CD8+ cytotoxic T cells.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Immunology

options.ai_explanation: TH1 cells produce IFN-gamma and IL-2, which activate macrophages and cytotoxic T cells involved in tissue destruction.
TH2 cells are primarily involved in humoral immunity and allergic responses, not direct cell-mediated destruction.
CD8+ cytotoxic T cells directly recognize and kill target cells via perforins and granzymes.

options.text: D. T helper 1 T cells (TH1 T)
options.ai_explanation: This is a redundant or non-standard nomenclature for TH1 cells.
T regulatory cells function to suppress immune responses and prevent tissue destruction.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Neutrophils

db.general_explanation: A) F – Not a function of neutrophils (Mainly lymphocytes play an important role in viral infections). B) T – Phagocytosis. C) T – Activated neutrophils produce large amounts of reactive oxygen species. D) F – Not an antigen presenting cell (APC = Dendritic cells, Macrophages, B cells). E) T – Via Pattern Recognition Receptors.

answer_raw: C. Kill phagocytosed bacteria by an oxidative burst reaction.
ai.clinical_core: Neutrophils are the primary effectors of the innate immune response against bacterial infections, utilizing phagocytosis and intracellular killing.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Destroy viral infected cells by forming pores in the cell membrane.
options.ai_explanation: This is the function of Cytotoxic T cells and NK cells.
Neutrophils are professional phagocytes that engulf bacteria.

options.text: C. Kill phagocytosed bacteria by an oxidative burst reaction.
options.ai_explanation: They use reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the respiratory burst to kill pathogens.

options.text: D. Present antigen to T lymphocytes.
options.ai_explanation: Antigen presentation is the role of professional APCs like dendritic cells and macrophages.

options.text: E. Recognize specific antigen on the surface of pathogen.
options.ai_explanation: Neutrophils recognize broad patterns (PAMPs), not specific antigens like B or T cells.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is the most important for the control of intercellular virus infection?

db.general_explanation: No explanation provided in source text, but indicated as B.

ai.clinical_core: Control of intracellular viral infections relies on detecting and killing infected cells (CTLs, NK cells) and inducing an antiviral state (Interferons).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Complement is more effective against extracellular bacteria.
CTLs recognize viral peptides on MHC I and kill the infected cell.
Antibodies neutralize free viruses but are less effective once the virus is intracellular.
Type I interferons (IFN-alpha/beta) inhibit viral replication in neighboring cells.
NK cells kill cells that down-regulate MHC I, a common viral evasion tactic.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60 year old patient was admitted to the ward with necrotizing fasciitis and her tissue culture reveals Staphylococcus aureus. Which of the following immune mechanisms is the most effective against the infection?

db.general_explanation: Against gram-positive bacteria, defense is through opsonization and specific immunity by antibodies. Toxins are neutralized by antibodies, and bacteria are then killed by phagocytic cells.

answer_raw: B. Binding of antibodies to toxins of Staphylococcus aureus
ai.clinical_core: Immunity to extracellular bacteria like S. aureus involves opsonization by antibodies and complement, leading to phagocytosis.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Activation of cytotoxic T cells.
options.ai_explanation: CTLs are primarily for intracellular pathogens (viruses).

options.text: B. Binding of antibodies to toxins of Staphylococcus aureus
options.ai_explanation: Neutralization of toxins is a key humoral defense.
Antibody binding triggers the classical pathway, aiding opsonization.

options.text: D. Cytokines released by T helper 2 cells.
options.ai_explanation: Th2 is more involved in parasite defense; Th1/Th17 are more relevant for bacteria.

options.text: E. Destruction of Staphylococcus aureus infected cells by NK cells
options.ai_explanation: S. aureus is primarily extracellular; NK cells target intracellular issues.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 3-month-old baby is presented with recurrent diarrhoea and candidiasis. What is the most probable primary immune deficiency of this patient?

db.general_explanation: T-cell deficiencies often manifest early (around 4 months) with opportunistic and fungal infections like candidiasis.

ai.clinical_core: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) or primary T-cell defects typically present in early infancy (3-6 months) with opportunistic infections like Candida, persistent diarrhea, and failure to thrive.

options.ai_explanation: Pure antibody deficiencies (like X-linked agammaglobulinemia) usually present after 6 months when maternal IgG wanes and typically involve pyogenic bacteria.
Complement deficiencies usually present with recurrent Neisserial infections or autoimmune diseases.
While cytokines are involved, the clinical syndrome of early candidiasis and diarrhea is the hallmark of T-cell/cellular immunity failure.
Phagocytic defects (like CGD) usually present with skin abscesses and granulomas, not typically early-onset candidiasis/diarrhea.
T-cells are essential for defense against fungi (Candida) and viruses; their deficiency leads to early-onset opportunistic infections.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Monoclonal antibodies are used as the treatment of,

db.general_explanation: Monoclonal antibodies are used for allergic, inflammatory, and cancer immunotherapy. Rheumatic fever is treated with antibiotics to eradicate Streptococci, not monoclonal antibodies.

ai.clinical_core: Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) have revolutionized therapy across specialties by targeting specific proteins. They are used in oncology (lymphoma), autoimmune diseases (IBD), allergy (asthma), and transplant medicine.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Clinical Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Omalizumab is a monoclonal antibody targeting IgE used in severe allergic asthma.
Anti-TNF antibodies like Infliximab and Adalimumab are standard treatments for refractory IBD.
Rituximab (anti-CD20) is a cornerstone in the treatment of B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas.
Rheumatic fever is treated with antibiotics (penicillin) and anti-inflammatory agents (aspirin/steroids), not monoclonal antibodies.
Monoclonal antibodies like Basiliximab (anti-CD25) are used for induction therapy to prevent transplant rejection.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following secondary immunodeficiency is correctly matched with the main underlying mechanism of immunodeficiency?

db.general_explanation: All matches are considered correct. Age results in thymic involution. Diabetes involves defective phagocytosis and T-cell anergy. HIV affects CD4+ and CD8+ cells. Malnutrition reduces T-cell function. Steroids inhibit transcription of pro-inflammatory cytokines.

answer_raw: A. Advance age - Limitation of the thymus to generate naive T cell
E. Steroids - Diminished transcription of pro inflammatory cytokines
ai.clinical_core: Secondary immunodeficiencies result from external factors. Aging causes thymic involution; diabetes impairs neutrophil function; malnutrition is the global leading cause; and steroids act via genomic mechanisms to suppress inflammation.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Secondary Immunodeficiency

options.text: A. Advance age - Limitation of the thymus to generate naive T cell
options.ai_explanation: Thymic involution with age reduces the output of new T cells, contributing to immunosenescence.
Hyperglycemia impairs neutrophil chemotaxis, adherence, and phagocytic activity.

options.text: C. HIV - Defective CD8 T cell function
options.ai_explanation: The hallmark of HIV is the depletion and defective function of CD4+ T helper cells.
Protein-energy malnutrition leads to atrophy of lymphoid tissues and impaired cell-mediated immunity.

options.text: E. Steroids - Diminished transcription of pro inflammatory cytokines
options.ai_explanation: Glucocorticoids bind to intracellular receptors to inhibit the transcription of genes for IL-1, IL-2, and other cytokines.

subject: BLOOD
stem: What is the cell which mediates type IV hypersensitivity?

db.general_explanation: Type IV hypersensitivity is a delayed-type reaction mediated by TH1 cells and macrophages.

ai.clinical_core: Type IV hypersensitivity is a delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) mediated by T cells rather than antibodies. Th1 cells and CD8+ cytotoxic T cells are the primary effectors.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: B cells mediate Types I, II, and III hypersensitivity via antibody production.
Mast cells mediate Type I (immediate) hypersensitivity.
While involved in some Type IV reactions (like contact dermatitis), Th1 cells are the classic mediators of the DTH response.
Th1 cells release cytokines (like IFN-gamma) that activate macrophages, the hallmark of Type IV DTH.
Th2 cells are primarily involved in Type I hypersensitivity and parasite defense.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 45-year-old renal transplant patient was observed in the ICU for 24 hours. Following transfer to the ward on day 5 developed fever and purulent discharge in the surgical site. He was diagnosed to have surgical site infection. What is the most likely aetiological agent?

db.general_explanation: Post-surgical infections associated with purulent discharge within the first few days are strongly suggestive of Staphylococcus aureus, particularly MRSA in hospital-acquired cases.

ai.clinical_core: Surgical site infections (SSIs) occurring within the first week post-operatively are most commonly caused by skin flora, with S. aureus being the leading pathogen.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Transplant Section

options.ai_explanation: Fungal infections usually occur later in the course of immunosuppression.
Can cause SSI, but less common than S. aureus.
More common in abdominal surgeries, but S. aureus is the overall most likely agent for early SSI.
S. aureus is the most frequent cause of surgical site infections globally.
Can cause rapid, severe SSI, but is less common than S. aureus.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 35-year-old male admitted to the emergency casualty unit after a motor traffic accident. Investigations revealed a rupture of the spleen. So an emergency splenectomy was planned. What is the best time to give him the vaccination against Haemophilus, Pneumococcus, Meningococcus?

db.general_explanation: Vaccinations should be administered at least 2 weeks before surgery in elective cases, or at least 2 weeks after surgery in emergency cases.

ai.clinical_core: Post-splenectomy patients are at high risk for Overwhelming Post-Splenectomy Infection (OPSI). Vaccination timing is critical for optimal antibody response.

options.text: A. Just before the induction of the surgery
options.ai_explanation: In an emergency setting, the immune system is stressed and may not respond well immediately before surgery.

options.text: B. Soon after the decision was made for splenectomy
options.ai_explanation: In an emergency (trauma), there is no time for this.
Immediate post-op vaccination is less effective than waiting for the patient to stabilize.
For emergency splenectomy, guidelines recommend waiting 14 days post-op to allow for recovery and better immune response.

options.text: E. When patient develops early symptoms of infection
options.ai_explanation: Vaccination is preventive; it must be given before infection occurs.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 25-year-old previously healthy man presents to the emergency unit of the hospital with swelling of lips, hoarseness, shortness of breath, and dizziness following the ingestion of peanuts. The most appropriate drug to be used in the immediate management of the patient is,

db.general_explanation: The patient is experiencing an anaphylactic reaction (Type 1 hypersensitivity). Adrenaline is the first-line treatment to provide vasoconstriction and bronchodilatation.

ai.clinical_core: Anaphylaxis is a life-threatening Type I hypersensitivity reaction. Adrenaline is the only drug that treats all the physiological components of anaphylaxis.

options.ai_explanation: The first-line and most important treatment for anaphylaxis.
An antihistamine; used as an adjunct but not for immediate life-saving.
A steroid; used as an adjunct to prevent late-phase reactions.
Used for septic shock, not the first-line for anaphylaxis.
Oral steroid; not for immediate emergency management.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regulations of the compliment system include,

db.general_explanation: Only CR1 is a regulatory element of the complement system.

ai.clinical_core: The complement system is tightly regulated by membrane-bound and soluble proteins to prevent host tissue damage.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: C3b is an opsonin and part of the activation cascade, not a regulator.
CR1 acts as a cofactor for Factor I to degrade C3b and C4b, regulating the cascade.
CTLA-4 is a regulator of T-cell activation.
KIRs regulate Natural Killer (NK) cell activity.
PRRs (like TLRs) trigger innate responses but do not regulate the complement system.

subject: BLOOD
stem: What is the most important cell type in mediating immune response against cancer cells?

db.general_explanation: CD8+ Cytotoxic T cells are the most prominent anti-tumor cells that induce apoptosis in malignant cells.

ai.clinical_core: The immune system identifies cancer cells via tumor-associated antigens. CD8+ cytotoxic T cells are the primary effectors that recognize and kill these cells.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: B cells produce antibodies, which play a secondary role in tumor immunity.
CD8+ T cells are the most important cells for direct killing of tumor cells.
T-regs often suppress the anti-tumor immune response.
Th1 cells support CTLs but are not the primary killers.
Th2 cells are generally not involved in effective anti-tumor immunity.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Antigen specific cells involved in cell mediated immunity,

db.general_explanation: Cells involving adaptive immunity (T cells) are antigen specific. Innate cells (Dendritic, Macrophages) are not antigen specific.

ai.clinical_core: Cell-mediated immunity (CMI) involves T lymphocytes that recognize specific antigens.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Antigen-specific effectors of CMI.
Involved in CMI but are not antigen-specific (they are APCs).
Antigen-specific orchestrators of CMI.
Effectors of CMI but are not antigen-specific.
Antigen-specific suppressors of CMI.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following hypersensitivity reactions are correctly matched with the most important component of immune system which mediates them?

db.general_explanation: Type 1 is mediated by IgE. Type 2 is mediated by both IgG and IgM. Type 3 is mediated by IgG and IgM. Type 4 is mediated by TH1 cells, macrophages, dendritic cells, cytotoxic T cells, and cytokines. Type 5 is mediated by IgG and IgM.

answer_raw: D. Type 4 hypersensitivity - T helper 1 cells
ai.clinical_core: Hypersensitivity reactions are classified by the Gell and Coombs system.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Type 1 hypersensitivity reaction - IgM antibodies
options.ai_explanation: Type 1 is mediated by IgE.
Type 2 involves IgG or IgM binding to cell surface antigens.

options.text: C. Type 3 hypersensitivity - T helper 2 cells
options.ai_explanation: Type 3 is mediated by immune complexes.

options.text: D. Type 4 hypersensitivity - T helper 1 cells
options.ai_explanation: Type 4 is cell-mediated, often involving Th1 cells and macrophages.

options.text: E. Type 5 hypersensitivity - Ig E Antibodies
options.ai_explanation: Type 5 is sometimes used for stimulatory antibodies (like Graves'); IgE is Type 1.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following cells is the most efficient for antigen presentation?

db.general_explanation: Dendritic cells are the most efficient professional antigen-presenting cells that can stimulate naive T lymphocytes.

ai.clinical_core: Dendritic cells are the most potent professional antigen-presenting cells, uniquely capable of activating naive T cells.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Professional APC, but less efficient than dendritic cells at priming naive T cells.
The most efficient APC for initiating primary immune responses.
Non-professional APC.
Non-professional APC.
Professional APC, but primarily presents to already activated T cells.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding immune response in transplantation,

db.general_explanation: Acute rejection is mediated by T cells. Cytotoxic T cells (not CD4 alone) are primarily involved. Chronic rejection causes fibrosis/narrowing. Hyper-acute is via pre-existing antibodies. MHC proteins are major stimulators.

answer_raw: A. Acute graft rejection is mediated by T cells
D. Hyper-acute rejection is mediated by pre-existing antibodies
E. MHC protein are the major antigen that stimulate graft rejection
ai.clinical_core: Transplant rejection is a complex immune response against non-self MHC antigens.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Acute graft rejection is mediated by T cells
options.ai_explanation: Acute cellular rejection is primarily a T-cell mediated process.

options.text: B. Cells of transplanted tissue destroyed by CD4 T cells
options.ai_explanation: CD8+ T cells are the primary effectors of direct cell destruction.
Chronic rejection is characterized by vascular intimal thickening and fibrosis.

options.text: D. Hyper-acute rejection is mediated by pre-existing antibodies
options.ai_explanation: Occurs within minutes due to pre-formed anti-HLA or ABO antibodies.

options.text: E. MHC protein are the major antigen that stimulate graft rejection
options.ai_explanation: HLA (MHC) mismatch is the primary trigger for rejection.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which cell is mainly involved in disseminated Tuberculosis?

db.general_explanation: CD4 T cells are critical for macrophage activation in containing TB.

ai.clinical_core: The immune response to Mycobacterium tuberculosis is primarily cell-mediated, relying on CD4+ Th1 cells to activate macrophages.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Infectious Diseases Section

options.ai_explanation: Antibodies are not effective against intracellular TB.
CD4+ T cells (Th1) produce IFN-gamma, which is essential for macrophage activation and granuloma formation.
While they play a role, CD4+ cells are the primary orchestrators.
Involved in parasitic infections and allergies.
Involved in early viral defense and tumor surveillance.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Side effects of desferrioxamine is/are,

db.general_explanation: Common side effects include arthropathy, growth retardation, and hearing impairment.

ai.clinical_core: Desferrioxamine is an effective chelator but has a significant side effect profile, especially at high doses in young children.

options.ai_explanation: High doses can cause joint pains and bone changes.
Excessive chelation in children can interfere with growth.
Ototoxicity is a well-known side effect.
Not a recognized side effect of desferrioxamine.
Desferrioxamine acts as a siderophore for Yersinia, increasing the risk of systemic infection.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 75-year-old patient presents with a Hb of 8 g/dl. Which of the following would favour a diagnosis of iron deficiency?

db.general_explanation: Reticulocyte count is low in iron deficiency anaemia. Transferrin saturation should be less than 10% for iron deficiency.

answer_raw: A. An elevated iron total iron binding capacity
ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia is characterized by microcytosis, low ferritin, and a compensatory increase in TIBC.

options.text: A. An elevated iron total iron binding capacity
options.ai_explanation: TIBC increases as the body attempts to capture more iron.
Microcytosis is a classic feature of iron deficiency.
This is a normal reticulocyte count; it doesn't specifically favor IDA over other causes.
Low serum ferritin is the most specific biochemical marker for depleted iron stores.
Transferrin saturation is low in IDA (typically <15%).

subject: BLOOD
stem: 35-year-old woman presented with menorrhagia is suspected of having Iron deficiency anaemia. Which of the following investigation findings are compatible with the diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Biochemical findings of Iron deficiency anaemia include reduced serum ferritin, increased TIBC, reduced percentage saturation, reduced serum iron, increased free erythrocyte protoporphyrin, increased soluble transferrin receptors, and reduced hepcidin.

answer_raw: D. Increased serum soluble transferrin receptors level.
ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia is diagnosed by low iron stores and evidence of iron-restricted erythropoiesis.

options.ai_explanation: RDW is typically increased in IDA.
Associated with lead poisoning or thalassemia, not IDA.

options.text: C. Elevated serum transferrin saturation level.
options.ai_explanation: Transferrin saturation is low in IDA.

options.text: D. Increased serum soluble transferrin receptors level.
options.ai_explanation: sTfR levels increase when cells are iron-starved.
Low serum ferritin is the most specific marker for iron deficiency.

subject: BLOOD
stem: What is the most specific statement that suggest iron deficiency anemia?

db.general_explanation: Low serum iron and ferritin levels with an elevated TIBC are diagnostic of iron deficiency. A low serum ferritin is virtually diagnostic of iron deficiency.

ai.clinical_core: Serum ferritin is the most specific biochemical test for iron deficiency, as a low level directly reflects exhausted iron stores.

options.ai_explanation: This is also seen in thalassemia trait, sideroblastic anemia, and anemia of chronic disease.
Low serum iron is also found in anemia of chronic disease (where iron is sequestered).
This is a non-specific finding in many hypoproliferative anemias.
A low serum ferritin is diagnostic of iron deficiency (though a normal level doesn't exclude it if inflammation is present).

options.text: E. Reduced total iron binding capacity
options.ai_explanation: TIBC is actually increased in iron deficiency anemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 40-year-old woman presents with lethargy. Which of the following investigations favour an iron deficiency anaemia?

db.general_explanation: In nutrient deficiencies the bone marrow is hypercellular but the reticulocyte count is low. Fe deficiency anemia is microcytic (low MCV). Saturated iron binding capacity is low due to low serum iron. Red cell distribution is usually increased. Ferritin levels decrease in Fe deficiency anemia.

answer_raw: C. A reduced percentage saturation of transferrin
D. An elevated red cell distribution width
ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is characterized by microcytosis, low iron stores, and increased variability in red cell size.

options.ai_explanation: IDA is a microcytic anemia with a low or inappropriately normal reticulocyte count due to lack of iron for RBC production.
Microcytosis (low MCV) is a hallmark of established iron deficiency anemia.

options.text: C. A reduced percentage saturation of transferrin
options.ai_explanation: Low serum iron and high TIBC result in a low transferrin saturation (typically <15%).

options.text: D. An elevated red cell distribution width
options.ai_explanation: RDW is typically elevated in IDA, reflecting anisocytosis (variation in cell size).
Serum ferritin is low in IDA; it is the most specific test for iron deficiency.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 30 years old woman is complaining of menorrhagia. How would you diagnose iron deficiency anemia?

db.general_explanation: Ferritin and hepcidin are low in iron deficiency anemia; transferrin is high, and transferrin saturation is <10%.

answer_raw: A. High level of free soluble transferrin receptor levels
ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) is characterized by depleted iron stores (low ferritin) and a compensatory increase in iron transport proteins (high transferrin/TIBC). Menorrhagia is the most common cause of IDA in premenopausal women.

options.text: A. High level of free soluble transferrin receptor levels
options.ai_explanation: Soluble transferrin receptor (sTfR) levels increase in iron deficiency as cells attempt to capture more iron.

options.text: B. High level of serum ferratin
options.ai_explanation: Serum ferritin is low in iron deficiency anemia as it reflects depleted iron stores.
Hepcidin is suppressed in iron deficiency to allow for increased iron absorption and release from stores.
Serum transferrin (measured as TIBC) increases in IDA as a physiological response to low iron.
Transferrin saturation (Serum Iron/TIBC) is characteristically low (usually <15%) in IDA.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A six month old child is investigated for anaemia. Which of the following is diagnosis of iron deficiency anaemia more likely than that of thalassemia major.

db.general_explanation: Increased platelet count, increased RDW, and history of prematurity are suggestive of iron deficiency anaemia rather than thalassemia.

ai.clinical_core: Iron deficiency anemia (IDA) and Thalassemia Major both present with microcytic anemia in childhood, but IDA is associated with nutritional/developmental risk factors and reactive thrombocytosis.

options.ai_explanation: Reactive thrombocytosis is common in iron deficiency anemia.
RDW is increased in both IDA and Thalassemia Major (though often normal in Thalassemia Trait).
Prematurity is a major risk factor for iron deficiency due to reduced birth stores.
RBC count is typically low in IDA and variable in Thalassemia Major.
Hepatomegaly is more characteristic of Thalassemia Major due to extramedullary hematopoiesis and iron overload.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding ferritin,

db.general_explanation: Ferritin is an acute phase protein, stained by Prussian blue, synthesized by the liver, and reflects body iron stores. It is water soluble.

answer_raw: A. It is an acute phase protein
C. It is synthesized by the liver
E. It reflects body iron stores
ai.clinical_core: Ferritin is the primary intracellular iron-storage protein. Serum ferritin is a widely used clinical marker for iron stores.

options.text: A. It is an acute phase protein
options.ai_explanation: Ferritin levels rise during inflammation, which can mask iron deficiency.

options.text: B. It is stained by Prussian blue colour with Pearl’s reagent
options.ai_explanation: Prussian blue stains hemosiderin, not ferritin.

options.text: C. It is synthesized by the liver
options.ai_explanation: The liver is a major site of ferritin synthesis and iron storage.
Ferritin is water-soluble; hemosiderin is insoluble.

options.text: E. It reflects body iron stores
options.ai_explanation: In the absence of inflammation, serum ferritin is proportional to total body iron stores.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding iron overload in thalassemia, A) Decrease excretion of iron is a contributory factor.

db.general_explanation: Excretion is not a contributory factor. It occurs even without transfusion and is measured by serum ferritin.

answer_raw: A. Decrease excretion of iron is a contributory factor
B. It is measured by serum ferritin.
C. It is the primary cause of mortality.
D. Occurs even without blood transfusion.
ai.clinical_core: Iron overload in Thalassemia is due to both chronic transfusions and increased gastrointestinal absorption (due to ineffective erythropoiesis).

options.text: A. Decrease excretion of iron is a contributory factor
options.ai_explanation: Humans have no active mechanism to excrete excess iron.

options.text: B. It is measured by serum ferritin.
options.ai_explanation: Ferritin is a standard surrogate marker for body iron stores.

options.text: C. It is the primary cause of mortality.
options.ai_explanation: Iron-induced heart failure is the leading cause of death in Thalassemia Major.

options.text: D. Occurs even without blood transfusion.
options.ai_explanation: Hepcidin suppression in thalassemia leads to hyperabsorption of dietary iron.
Iron deposition in endocrine glands causes growth hormone deficiency and delayed puberty.

subject: BLOOD
stem: True / False regarding intravenous iron therapy,

db.general_explanation: It causes iron toxicity and anaphylaxis. It is indicated in malabsorptive states and when oral therapy is not tolerated. IV infusion does not increase the haematopoietic response compared to oral, it only replenishes iron stores faster. It is indicated in CKD patients.

answer_raw: B. Indicated when oral iron therapy is not tolerated
D. It is indicated in CKD patients
E. It replenishes iron stores faster than oral iron therapy
ai.clinical_core: Intravenous iron is used when oral iron is ineffective, not tolerated, or when rapid replenishment is required.
ai.reference: NICE Guidelines on Iron Deficiency Anemia

options.ai_explanation: Though rare with modern formulations, hypersensitivity and anaphylactoid reactions are known risks.

options.text: B. Indicated when oral iron therapy is not tolerated
options.ai_explanation: A primary indication for switching to IV iron.

options.text: C. IV iron infusion is more efficient than oral iron therapy
options.ai_explanation: Efficiency (total Hb rise) is similar over time, but IV is faster.

options.text: D. It is indicated in CKD patients
options.ai_explanation: IV iron is preferred in CKD, especially those on dialysis or EPO.

options.text: E. It replenishes iron stores faster than oral iron therapy
options.ai_explanation: IV iron bypasses gut absorption limits, allowing for rapid store replenishment.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following test and container for sample collection is/are correctly paired

db.general_explanation: FBC and Reticulocyte counts are collected in EDTA. PT/INR requires citrate tubes.

answer_raw: D. Reticulocyte count – EDTA containing tube
ai.clinical_core: Correct sample collection is vital. EDTA is the anticoagulant of choice for cell counts and morphology.
ai.reference: Dacie and Lewis Practical Hematology, Chapter 1

options.text: A. Coomb’s test – citrate containing tube
options.ai_explanation: Coombs test is usually done on EDTA or plain (clotted) samples.
EDTA preserves cell morphology for full blood counts.
PCV is part of the FBC and uses EDTA.

options.text: D. Reticulocyte count – EDTA containing tube
options.ai_explanation: Reticulocyte counts are performed on EDTA-anticoagulated blood.
PT requires trisodium citrate (blue top) to chelate calcium.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A leucoerythroblastic blood picture is seen in

db.general_explanation: Causes of leukoerythroblastic blood picture include bone marrow infiltration (e.g., leukaemia, solid tumours like breast cancer, myelofibrosis), extramedullary haematopoiesis, megaloblastic anaemia, and widespread bone marrow activation (e.g., sepsis, severe haemorrhage, inflammatory states).

ai.clinical_core: A leucoerythroblastic blood picture (immature white and red cells) indicates bone marrow stress or infiltration, most classically seen in myelofibrosis.

options.ai_explanation: Marrow is empty; no immature cells are pushed out.
Shows schistocytes and low platelets, not leucoerythroblastosis.
Fibrosis and extramedullary hematopoiesis lead to the release of immature cells.
Can cause a leukemoid reaction but rarely a full leucoerythroblastic picture.
Shows macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 3 year old boy presented with fever and generalized lymphadenopathy. A diagnosis of Acute lymphoblastic Leukaemia. Which of the following investigations are most significant for the prognosis?

db.general_explanation: White blood cell count at the time of diagnosis is a significant prognostic indicator in Acute lymphoblastic Leukaemia.

ai.answer_raw: E. WBC in the peripheral blood at the time of diagnosis
ai.clinical_core: In childhood ALL, the presenting white blood cell (WBC) count and age are the most important clinical prognostic factors.

options.ai_explanation: Non-specific and not used for prognosis.

options.text: B. Serum LDH level at the diagnosis
options.ai_explanation: Reflects tumor burden but is not a primary prognostic marker in ALL.
Reflects cell turnover but not prognosis.

options.text: D. Blast cells in the peripheral blood
options.ai_explanation: The presence of blasts is diagnostic, but the total WBC count is the prognostic marker.

options.text: E. WBC in the peripheral blood at the time of diagnosis
options.ai_explanation: A WBC > 50,000/mm3 in B-ALL is a high-risk feature.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 3-year-old child presented with fever, recurrent attacks of upper respiratory tract infections, irritability and a petechial rash. On examination he was found to have cervical lymphadenopathy. His full blood count is as follows: Hb – 8 g/dl, WBC – 40,000/mm3, Platelets – 20,000/mm3. What is the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The clinical presentation of fever, pallor (Hb 8g/dl), thrombocytopenia (petechial rash, platelets 20,000/mm3), and lymphadenopathy, combined with a high white cell count in a young child, is classic for Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia (ALL).

ai.clinical_core: ALL is the most common childhood malignancy. It typically presents with bone marrow failure (anemia, thrombocytopenia) and lymphadenopathy.

options.ai_explanation: The combination of pancytopenia (Hb 8, Plt 20k), high WBC (40k), and lymphadenopathy in a 3-year-old is classic for ALL.
Less common in children than ALL.
Causes thrombocytopenia and leukopenia, not a WBC of 40,000.
Causes lymphadenopathy and lymphocytosis, but not severe anemia and thrombocytopenia.
Presents with rash and respiratory symptoms, not this blood picture.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 60 year old man presents for a routine checkup. His investigations are Hb 13g/dl, WBC 40,000, Neutrophils 4.0% Lymphocytes 96.0%, Platelet 200,000. What is the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The FBC report shows an increased lymphocyte count (lymphocytosis). Mature lymphocytosis is a characteristic feature of chronic lymphocytic leukemia.

ai.clinical_core: Chronic Lymphocytic Leukemia (CLL) is the most common leukemia in elderly adults, characterized by an absolute lymphocytosis (>5,000/µL) of mature-appearing lymphocytes.

options.ai_explanation: ALL usually presents with cytopenias (anemia, thrombocytopenia) and immature lymphoblasts, not mature lymphocytosis in an asymptomatic 60-year-old.
AML would show myeloblasts and typically presents with acute illness and significant cytopenias.
The age (60), incidental finding, and high absolute lymphocyte count (38,400/µL) with normal Hb and platelets are classic for CLL.
CML is characterized by a high WBC count with a full spectrum of myeloid cells (neutrophils, myelocytes), not isolated lymphocytosis.
EBV (Infectious Mononucleosis) causes lymphocytosis in young adults and is usually accompanied by pharyngitis and fever.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 70-year-old man had following FBC: Hb 12 g/dL, WBC 19,000/mm^3, Platelets 250,000/mm^3. DC: N 5%, L 90%, E 2%, M 2%, B 1%. No hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy. Most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Isolated lymphocytosis without blast cells points to CLL. Stage 0 CLL can present with bone marrow infiltration and lymphocytosis only, without lymphadenopathy.

ai.clinical_core: CLL is the most common leukemia in the elderly, often discovered incidentally on FBC showing an absolute lymphocytosis.

options.ai_explanation: Would typically show blasts and cytopenias.
Less common in this age group and usually presents with acute symptoms.
Isolated lymphocytosis (90% of 19,000) in an asymptomatic 70-year-old is classic for CLL.
Usually presents with lymphadenopathy and does not typically cause lymphocytosis.
Usually presents with lymphadenopathy and more aggressive symptoms.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 3 year old baby presents with rapidly enlarging LN in submandibular region with mild fever ill looking serum LDH level is 400

db.general_explanation: The clinical presentation of a rapidly enlarging lymph node in a young child combined with high LDH levels is highly suggestive of Burkitt lymphoma.

ai.clinical_core: Burkitt Lymphoma is a highly aggressive B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphoma characterized by extremely rapid doubling time, often presenting in children with extranodal involvement (jaw/submandibular in endemic forms) and very high LDH levels due to high cell turnover.

options.ai_explanation: Small lymphocytic lymphoma is typically an indolent disease of the elderly, not a rapid childhood presentation.
The rapid growth, submandibular location in a child, and high LDH are classic for Burkitt lymphoma.
This is generally an indolent lymphoma and does not present with rapid enlargement and high LDH in children.
MALT lymphomas are low-grade and usually associated with chronic inflammation (e.g., H. pylori), not rapid childhood growth.
Follicular lymphoma is rare in children and typically follows an indolent course.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following give rise to a leuko erythroblastic blood picture,

db.correct_answer_raw: A. F B. T C. F D. T E. F
db.general_explanation: Leukoerythroblastic blood film is the presence of erythroblasts and granulocyte precursors in the blood. Causes include metastatic neoplasm in bone marrow, myelofibrosis, acute and chronic myeloid leukemia, myeloma, lymphoma, miliary TB, severe megaloblastic anaemia, severe haemolysis, and osteoporosis.

ai.clinical_core: A leukoerythroblastic blood picture is characterized by the presence of immature red cells (nucleated RBCs) and immature white cells in the peripheral blood, often indicating marrow stress or infiltration.
ai.reference: Dacie and Lewis Practical Hematology, Chapter 3

options.ai_explanation: Severe marrow stress from massive hemorrhage can cause premature release of precursors.
AML shows blasts, but 'leukoerythroblastic' specifically refers to a mix of myeloid and erythroid precursors, often seen in marrow fibrosis or displacement.
Aplastic anemia shows pancytopenia with a lack of precursors.
The classic cause of a leukoerythroblastic picture due to extramedullary hematopoiesis.
Severe DIC can cause a leukoerythroblastic picture due to extreme marrow stress and microangiopathic hemolysis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding germinal center of a reactive follicle,

db.general_explanation: Germinal centers are arranged in pale and dark areas. While cell number is increasing, apoptosis is not downregulated. Centroblasts are enlarged rapidly proliferating B cells. There is high mitotic activity of immune cells. They consist mainly of B cells.

answer_raw: A. Arranged in polarized areas as pale and dark.
C. increased number of Centro blasts.
ai.clinical_core: Reactive germinal centers are sites of B-cell proliferation, somatic hypermutation, and selection. They show distinct polarization and high turnover.

options.text: A. Arranged in polarized areas as pale and dark.
options.ai_explanation: Germinal centers have a dark zone (centroblasts) and a light zone (centrocytes/FDCs).
Apoptosis is actually very high in germinal centers as B-cells that fail selection are eliminated (tingible body macrophages).

options.text: C. increased number of Centro blasts.
options.ai_explanation: Centroblasts are the proliferating B-cells found in the dark zone of the germinal center.
Germinal centers are areas of rapid B-cell clonal expansion, showing many mitotic figures.

options.text: E. T cell number is increased
options.ai_explanation: While T-follicular helper cells are present, the germinal center is predominantly a B-cell structure.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60-year-old man presented with left supraclavicular lymph node. The excision biopsy revealed expanded subcapsular sinus which contained sheets and clusters of cells with irregular, eccentric, hyperchromatic nuclei. The rest of the node had expanded cortical and medullary sinuses with abundant histiocytes. Which of the following is the most likely condition?

db.general_explanation: Sinus Histiocytosis is a reactive pattern characterized by distension and prominence of the lymphatic sinusoids, owing to a marked hypertrophy of lining endothelial cells and an infiltrate of macrophages (histiocytes). Sinus histiocytosis is often encountered in lymph nodes draining cancers and may represent an immune response to the tumour or its products.

ai.answer_raw: C. Metastasis of signet ring cell carcinoma
ai.clinical_core: A left supraclavicular node (Virchow's node) containing clusters of cells with eccentric nuclei is highly suggestive of metastatic adenocarcinoma, often from a gastric primary.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Oncology Section

options.ai_explanation: A reactive process with preserved architecture and germinal centers.
Characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells, not clusters of signet-ring-like cells.

options.text: C. Metastasis of signet ring cell carcinoma
options.ai_explanation: The description of clusters of cells with eccentric nuclei in the subcapsular sinus is classic for metastatic carcinoma.
Usually involves a more diffuse or nodular replacement of the node by lymphoid cells.
A reactive expansion of sinuses with benign histiocytes, not malignant clusters.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 70-year-old presented with painless lymphadenopathy of lateral neck for past year. His lymph node examination diagnoses as follicular lymphoma grade 1. Which of the following is consistent with above diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: In follicular lymphoma, there is expression of BCL2 in germinal center cells.

ai.answer_raw: C. Expression of BCL2 in germinal center cells
ai.clinical_core: Follicular lymphoma is characterized by the t(14;18) translocation, which leads to the overexpression of BCL2, an anti-apoptotic protein.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Hematology Section

options.text: A. Absence of mantel zone in follicles
options.ai_explanation: Mantle zones are often attenuated but not necessarily absent.

options.text: B. Absence of polarity of germinal center of follicles
options.ai_explanation: Malignant follicles lack the dark/light zone polarity of reactive follicles.

options.text: C. Expression of BCL2 in germinal center cells
options.ai_explanation: Normal germinal center B cells are BCL2 negative; expression is diagnostic of follicular lymphoma.

options.text: D. Atrophy lymphoid follicles extend to paracortex and medulla
options.ai_explanation: Not a diagnostic feature.

options.text: E. Extranodal distribution of lymphoid follicles
options.ai_explanation: Follicular lymphoma is primarily nodal.

subject: BLOOD
stem:
Which of the following pathologies/ diseases are correctly matched with their histological appearance?
Collagen Vascular Disease - follicular hyperplasia.
Kikuchi Disease - necrotizing lymphadenitis.
Leprosy - necrotizing granuloma.
Sarcoidosis - non necrotizing granuloma.
Toxoplasmosis- microgranuloma.

db.general_explanation: Causes for follicular hyperplasia are collagen vascular diseases, infections; bacterial, parasitic, viral. Kikuchi disease is a type of lymphadenopathy with unknown etiology. Paracortical well circumscribed necrotizing lesions are there. Leprosy is non-necrotizing granuloma with foamy histiocytes. Sarcoidosis is non-necrotizing granuloma with large langhans giant cells. Toxoplasmosis is microgranulomata and follicular hyperplasia with effacement of follicular margins.

ai.clinical_core: Lymph node pathology requires specific histological patterns for diagnosis.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Pathology Section

options.text: A. Collagen Vascular Disease - follicular hyperplasia
options.ai_explanation: Follicular hyperplasia is non-specific; CVD often shows more specific features like hematoxylin bodies in SLE.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding the reactive germinal center of lymph node,
