db.general_explanation: The germinal center is bordered by a marginal zone (True), it consists of B cells rather than being highly T cell-populated (False), Centroblasts are not antigen selected (False), they are rich in follicular dendritic cells (True), and they contain polarized dark and light zones (True).

answer_raw: A. Bordered by a marginal zone of small mature B cell
C. Found of antigen selected centroblasts and centrocytes
D. Are rich in follicular dendritic cells
E. Consists of polarised dark and light zones
ai.clinical_core: Germinal centers are specialized structures in lymph nodes where B cells undergo proliferation, somatic hypermutation, and selection.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Bordered by a marginal zone of small mature B cell
options.ai_explanation: The germinal center is surrounded by the mantle zone and marginal zone.

options.text: B. Highly consists of T cells
options.ai_explanation: Germinal centers are primarily B-cell structures (though some T-follicular helper cells are present).

options.text: C. Found of antigen selected centroblasts and centrocytes
options.ai_explanation: These are the two main B-cell stages within the germinal center.

options.text: D. Are rich in follicular dendritic cells
options.ai_explanation: FDCs provide the scaffold and present trapped antigens to B cells.

options.text: E. Consists of polarised dark and light zones
options.ai_explanation: Dark zone (centroblasts) and light zone (centrocytes/FDCs) are characteristic.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 28 years old female presented with cervical lumps for 2 months. On physical examination found to have mildly tender right side lymph nodes. Biopsy was done. On microscope was large node, varying sizes of follicles, and micro granulomas affecting the margins of follicles. No evidence of atypical lymphocytes, giant cells or necrosis found.

db.general_explanation: The histological findings of varying sizes of follicles and microgranulomas affecting the margins, without atypical lymphocytes, giant cells, or caseous necrosis, are characteristic of Toxoplasmosis lymphadenitis.

ai.clinical_core: Toxoplasmic lymphadenitis (Piringer-Kuchinka lymphadenitis) presents with a classic triad of reactive follicular hyperplasia, clusters of epithelioid histiocytes (microgranulomas) encroaching on germinal centers, and monocytoid B-cell hyperplasia.
ai.reference: Wintrobe's Clinical Hematology, Chapter on Lymphadenopathy

options.ai_explanation: Follicular lymphoma shows monotonous follicles lacking tingible body macrophages and would not typically show microgranulomas.
While present, the specific finding of microgranulomas at the margins of follicles points specifically to Toxoplasmosis.

db.general_explanation: Reed Sternberg Cells are large bi-nucleated cells with an Owl’s eye nucleus and eosinophilic cytoplasm. Their presence with non-neoplastic cells in the background (plasma cells, lymphocytes, eosinophils) suggests Hodgkin’s lymphoma.

stem: Macrocytosis can be seen in

ai.clinical_core: Malignant lymphomas are clonal proliferations. Demonstrating light chain restriction (monoclonality) is the gold standard for distinguishing a malignant B-cell process from reactive hyperplasia.

options.text: E. Uniform deposition of kappa chains but not lambda chains
options.ai_explanation: Light chain restriction (expressing only kappa or only lambda) proves the population is monoclonal and therefore malignant.

options.ai_explanation: Approximately 50-60% of PMF patients harbor the JAK2 V617F mutation, which is a major WHO diagnostic criterion.

options.text: B. Positive PCR for BCR - ABL mutation
options.ai_explanation: BCR-ABL is the hallmark of Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML), not primary myelofibrosis.

options.text: C. Strong family history of disease
options.ai_explanation: While familial clusters exist, PMF is typically an acquired somatic mutation and family history is not a confirmatory finding.

options.text: D. An elevation serum LDH level
options.ai_explanation: Elevated LDH reflects increased cell turnover and ineffective hematopoiesis common in myelofibrosis.
As marrow fibrosis progresses, normal hematopoiesis fails, leading to anemia, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia (pancytopenia).

subject: BLOOD
stem: True / False regarding myeloproliferative neoplasms

db.general_explanation: Serum erythropoietin is reduced in primary polycythemia due to increased turnover of RBC production. Splenomegaly occurs in ET due to thrombosis and in primary myelofibrosis due to extramedullary haematopoiesis.

answer_raw: A. Myelofibrosis is regarded as a complication of essential thrombocytopenia
B. PCR for JAK 2 mutation is present in primary polycythemia
E. Venesection is a known therapy for primary polycythemia
ai.clinical_core: Myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs) are clonal disorders of the hematopoietic stem cell, often sharing the JAK2 mutation.

options.text: A. Myelofibrosis is regarded as a complication of essential thrombocytopenia
options.ai_explanation: ET and PV can both progress to a 'spent phase' of post-ET/PV myelofibrosis.

options.text: B. PCR for JAK 2 mutation is present in primary polycythemia
options.ai_explanation: JAK2 V617F is present in >95% of PV cases.

options.text: C. Serum erythropoietin is elevated in primary polycythemia
options.ai_explanation: Serum EPO is characteristically low in PV; high EPO suggests secondary polycythemia.
Splenomegaly is common in all MPNs due to extramedullary hematopoiesis or congestion.

options.text: E. Venesection is a known therapy for primary polycythemia
options.ai_explanation: Venesection is the first-line treatment to maintain hematocrit <45%.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60-year-old man presents with headache and blurring of vision. He complains significant weight loss over the past 2 months. On examination he is plethoric. His FBC shows Hb 19 g/dL, WBC 17x10^9/L, Platelets 550x10^9/L. Most probable diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Elevated Hb, WBC, and Platelets with plethora indicates polycythemia vera. Symptoms of headache and blurred vision are consistent with hyper-viscosity.

ai.clinical_core: Polycythemia Vera is an MPN presenting with panmyelosis (elevated Hb, WBC, and platelets) and symptoms of hyperviscosity (headache, blurring of vision).

options.ai_explanation: Can cause erythrocytosis but not typically thrombocytosis and leukocytosis.
Hb would be normal.
Usually presents with anemia, not high Hb.
The combination of plethora, high Hb, high WBC, and high platelets is classic.
Can cause secondary erythrocytosis (high Hb) but not panmyelosis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: The most important treatment option for a 57 years old male smoker presenting to medical casualty with an anterior STEMI within 6 hrs of onset of chest pain is

db.general_explanation: IV streptokinase (Thrombolysis) is indicated for STEMI within the 6-hour window.

ai.clinical_core: In STEMI, the priority is rapid reperfusion. If primary PCI is not available, fibrinolysis (e.g., with streptokinase) is the most important treatment within the early window.

options.ai_explanation: Crucial, but reperfusion therapy (D) is the definitive treatment for the blocked artery.
Used for stabilization but not for reperfusion.
Adjunctive anticoagulant.
Fibrinolysis is the primary reperfusion strategy when PCI is not immediately available.
Long-term secondary prevention.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 15 hour old baby girl born at 36 weeks gestation is found to be jaundiced. Her mother’s blood group is O Rh positive. The bilirubin is at exchange transfusion level. The baby’s blood group is A Rh negative, with direct Coombs test being weekly positive. She is otherwise well, active and feeds satisfactorily. What is the most likely cause for jaundice?

db.general_explanation: The infant is blood group A and the mother is group O, which is consistent with ABO incompatibility. The positive direct Coombs test supports an immune-mediated hemolysis.

ai.clinical_core: ABO HDN typically occurs in O mothers with A or B babies. It can affect the first pregnancy and often presents with early jaundice.

options.ai_explanation: Mother O and Baby A is the classic setup for ABO HDN; the weak positive Coombs is also typical.
Possible, but the blood group mismatch makes ABO incompatibility more likely.
Usually appears later than 15 hours.
Never appears within the first 24 hours.
Mother is Rh positive, so she cannot form anti-D antibodies.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with malaise. His full blood count is as follows: Hb - 6 g/dl, WBC - 3,000/mm3, Platelets – 20,000/mm3. Which of the following conditions should be considered as a differential diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: Dengue and HIV usually do not cause profound anaemia. In severe sepsis, WBC is typically high.

ai.clinical_core: Pancytopenia (low Hb, WBC, and platelets) has a wide differential including marrow failure, infection, and drug effects.

options.ai_explanation: A primary cause of pancytopenia due to marrow failure.
Can cause transient but severe pancytopenia during the critical phase.
Can cause cytopenias through direct marrow effects or opportunistic infections.
A very common cause of iatrogenic pancytopenia.
Can cause pancytopenia due to marrow suppression and peripheral consumption (DIC).

subject: BLOOD
stem: In 56 years old women who presents with tiredness, mild icterus,pallor. What is /are the suggestives of pernicious anaemia?

db.general_explanation: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune disorder characterized by anti-parietal cells or anti-intrinsic factor antibodies. Associated with blood group A. Hypersegmented neutrophils are pathognomonic. Can be associated with vitiligo. Steatorrhea is noted in Megaloblastic anemia due to B12 deficiency.

ai.clinical_core: Pernicious anemia is an autoimmune condition where antibodies against gastric parietal cells or intrinsic factor lead to Vitamin B12 deficiency. It presents with megaloblastic anemia (hypersegmented neutrophils) and is frequently associated with other autoimmune disorders like vitiligo.

options.ai_explanation: Anti-parietal cell antibodies are present in approximately 90% of patients with pernicious anemia.
Pernicious anemia is statistically associated with blood group A, not blood group O.
Hypersegmented neutrophils (5 or more lobes) are a classic morphological feature of megaloblastic anemia caused by B12 deficiency.
Pernicious anemia is an organ-specific autoimmune disease and often co-exists with other autoimmune conditions like vitiligo or thyroid disease.
Steatorrhoea is a feature of generalized malabsorption (e.g., Celiac disease) rather than the specific gastric pathology of pernicious anemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: True/false regarding warfarin

db.general_explanation: Warfarin is given orally, monitored by INR (not APTT), has a delayed onset of action (not 24 hours), and is not used in acute unstable angina.

ai.clinical_core: Warfarin is a vitamin K antagonist that inhibits the synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, and X. It requires several days to reach therapeutic levels and is monitored using the International Normalized Ratio (INR).

options.ai_explanation: Warfarin is primarily administered as an oral tablet with high bioavailability.

options.text: B. It is indicated in acute stage of unstable angina
options.ai_explanation: Heparin and antiplatelet agents are used in the acute stage; warfarin is not indicated for acute coronary syndromes.

options.text: C. Its action is monitored by APTT
options.ai_explanation: Warfarin is monitored by Prothrombin Time (PT) and INR; APTT is used to monitor unfractionated heparin.

options.text: D. Its therapeutic effect is achieved within 24 hours of administration
options.ai_explanation: It takes 48-72 hours for the full anticoagulant effect to manifest as existing clotting factors must be depleted.

options.text: E. Tranaxemic acid reverse the action of warfarin
options.ai_explanation: Warfarin is reversed by Vitamin K, Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (PCC), or Fresh Frozen Plasma (FFP); Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is/are true or false regarding aspirin

db.general_explanation: Aspirin blocks endothelial PGI2 (prostacyclin), inhibits platelet COX-1, can trigger bronchospasm in susceptible individuals (aspirin-induced asthma), but does not cause natriuresis or prolonged labor.

answer_raw: A. It blocks the endothelial prostaglandin synthesis
C. It is known to cause bronchospasm
E. It is known to cause prolonged labour
ai.clinical_core: Aspirin is an irreversible inhibitor of cyclooxygenase (COX), affecting both platelets (TXA2) and endothelial cells (PGI2).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Clinical Pharmacology Section

options.text: A. It blocks the endothelial prostaglandin synthesis
options.ai_explanation: Aspirin inhibits COX-1 and COX-2 in endothelial cells, reducing prostacyclin (PGI2) production.
Aspirin irreversibly inhibits COX-1 in platelets, preventing thromboxane A2 synthesis for the life of the platelet.

options.text: C. It is known to cause bronchospasm
options.ai_explanation: In sensitive individuals (Aspirin-Exacerbated Respiratory Disease), it shunts arachidonic acid to the leukotriene pathway.

options.text: D. It is known to cause natriuresis
options.ai_explanation: NSAIDs like aspirin typically cause sodium and water retention by inhibiting renal prostaglandins.

options.text: E. It is known to cause prolonged labour
options.ai_explanation: Prostaglandins are essential for uterine contractions; COX inhibition can delay or prolong labor.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 70 year old man presented with headache and confusion. His full blood count is provided. Hb 18g/dl Platelet 900,000 WBC 15000 On examination he was found to have splenomegaly. What is the test to confirm the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: JAK2 V617F mutation is present in 95% of patients with Polycythaemia Vera.

ai.clinical_core: The presentation of high Hb, high platelets, high WBC, and splenomegaly is highly suggestive of Polycythemia Vera, a myeloproliferative neoplasm.

options.ai_explanation: Shows features but is not confirmatory for PV.
Not diagnostic for PV.
JAK2 V617F mutation is present in >95% of PV cases and is a major diagnostic criterion.

options.text: E. USS for abdomen and pelvis
options.ai_explanation: Confirms splenomegaly but not the underlying cause.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with tiredness. His investigations are given below: Hb - 20.0 g/dl, Platelets - 700,000/mm3, White cell count - 20,000/mm3, DC - Neutrophils 56%, Lymphocytes 35%, Eosinophils 6%, Basophils 3%. Which of the following investigations is most helpful in arriving at a definitive diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The patient shows polycythemia with elevated platelets and white cell count, highly suggestive of Polycythemia Rubra Vera. JAK2 mutation testing is the definitive diagnostic investigation.

ai.clinical_core: The patient presents with panmyelosis (elevated Hb, WBC, and platelets), which is highly suggestive of Polycythemia Vera (PV). The JAK2 V617F mutation is present in over 95% of PV cases and is a primary diagnostic criterion.

options.ai_explanation: While the blood picture suggests a myeloproliferative neoplasm, it is not definitive for PV on its own.
NAP score is elevated in PV but is non-specific and largely replaced by molecular testing.

options.text: C. Ultrasound scan of the abdomen
options.ai_explanation: US may show splenomegaly, which supports the diagnosis, but is not definitive.
JAK2 mutation is the most sensitive and specific test for confirming Polycythemia Vera in the context of erythrocytosis.
The Philadelphia chromosome (BCR-ABL) is diagnostic for CML, not PV.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 55 year old man presented with the Hb of 20g/dl and splenomegaly. Which of the following findings is the most likely to confirm the presence of primary proliferative polycythemia?

db.general_explanation: Positive PCR for JAK 2 mutation confirms primary proliferative polycythemia (Polycythaemia Vera).

ai.answer_raw: C. Positive PCR for JAK 2 mutation
ai.clinical_core: Polycythemia Vera is a myeloproliferative neoplasm. The JAK2 V617F mutation is present in >95% of cases and is a major diagnostic criterion.

options.ai_explanation: Common in PV but non-specific.
NAP score is usually high in PV; it is low in CML.

options.text: C. Positive PCR for JAK 2 mutation
options.ai_explanation: JAK2 mutation is the most specific confirmatory test for PV.
Highly suggestive but not confirmatory.
Common in PV but seen in many other conditions.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Recognized early post-operative complications following splenectomy includes,

db.general_explanation: Gastric dilation, pancreatitis, and pleural effusion are recognized complications.

ai.clinical_core: Splenectomy complications can be immediate (bleeding), early post-operative (pancreatitis, gastric issues), or late (infections).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Hematology Section

options.ai_explanation: Can occur due to manipulation of the stomach during surgery.
The tail of the pancreas is in close proximity to the splenic hilum and can be injured.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Immune complexes consisting predominantly of IgA antibodies are deposited in the glomeruli in

db.general_explanation: IgA deposits are characteristic of IgA nephropathy and Henoch-Schönlein purpura.

ai.clinical_core: IgA nephropathy (Berger's disease) and Henoch-Schönlein Purpura (HSP) are related conditions characterized by the deposition of galactose-deficient IgA1 immune complexes in the glomerular mesangium.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 21: Nephrology

options.text: A. Anti glomerular basement membrane disease
options.ai_explanation: This involves IgG antibodies against the Type IV collagen in the GBM.
HSP is a systemic vasculitis characterized by IgA immune complex deposition in small vessels and the kidneys.
The hallmark of this disease is mesangial IgA deposits.
Lupus nephritis typically shows 'full house' staining (IgG, IgM, IgA, C3, C1q), but IgG is usually predominant.
MPGN is usually characterized by IgG and complement (C3) deposits.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following is the most important immunological mechanism responsible for the pathogenesis of minimal change nephrotic syndrome?

db.general_explanation: Minimal change disease is thought to be mediated by T-cell derived cytokines damaging the glomerular basement membrane.

ai.answer_raw: D. T lymphocyte derived mediators causing increased permeability in podocytes
ai.clinical_core: Minimal Change Disease is thought to be caused by a circulating 'permeability factor' produced by dysfunctional T-cells, which leads to podocyte foot process effacement.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Nephrology Section

options.ai_explanation: This is more characteristic of Membranous Nephropathy (anti-PLA2R).

options.text: B. Complement mediated damage to podocytes
options.ai_explanation: Complement is not a primary driver in Minimal Change Disease.

options.text: C. Deposition of antigen antibody complex in podocytes
options.ai_explanation: MCD is characterized by the absence of immune complex deposits on immunofluorescence.

options.text: D. T lymphocyte derived mediators causing increased permeability in podocytes
options.ai_explanation: The 'cytokine theory' suggests T-cell products disrupt the glomerular basement membrane's charge barrier.

options.text: E. Type II hypersensitivity reaction against podocytes
options.ai_explanation: MCD is not an antibody-mediated Type II reaction.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following best explain the cause for intrauterine death in Rh incompatibility?

db.general_explanation: The most severe form of erythroblastosis foetalis is hydrops foetalis, which is characterized by high output cardiac failure, oedema, ascites, pericardial effusion, and extramedullary haematopoiesis. Therefore, the best answer for intrauterine death is heart failure due to anaemia.

ai.answer_raw: C. Heart failure due to anaemia
ai.clinical_core: In Rh incompatibility, maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta and cause hemolysis of fetal Rh-positive red cells. Severe fetal anemia leads to high-output cardiac failure, hepatic congestion, and generalized edema (hydrops fetalis), which is the primary cause of intrauterine death.

options.ai_explanation: Bilirubin is cleared by the placenta in utero; kernicterus is a postnatal complication.

options.text: B. Direct toxic effect of bilirubin on heart
options.ai_explanation: Bilirubin is not directly cardiotoxic in this manner.

options.text: C. Heart failure due to anaemia
options.ai_explanation: Severe anemia leads to hydrops fetalis and high-output heart failure, the leading cause of fetal demise.

options.text: D. Renal failure due to hyperuricaemia
options.ai_explanation: This is not a recognized mechanism for death in Rh hemolytic disease of the fetus.

options.text: E. Tissue hypoxia due to anaemia
options.ai_explanation: While hypoxia occurs, the terminal event leading to death is typically cardiac failure (hydrops).

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding sepsis,

db.general_explanation: No explanation provided in source text.

answer_raw: A. C5a component plays a significant role.
B. It is associated with multi organ failure.
D. Results in transient immunosuppression in survivors.
E. Procalcitonin is a marker of sepsis.
ai.clinical_core: Sepsis is a life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by a dysregulated host response to infection. It involves massive cytokine release and complement activation.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Sepsis Section

options.text: A. C5a component plays a significant role.
options.ai_explanation: C5a is a potent anaphylatoxin that contributes to the inflammatory surge and capillary leak in sepsis.

options.text: B. It is associated with multi organ failure.
options.ai_explanation: The definition of sepsis (Sepsis-3) centers on infection-induced organ dysfunction.

options.text: C. Is characterized by refractory hypotension.
options.ai_explanation: Refractory hypotension is the hallmark of septic shock, a subset of sepsis, not all sepsis.

options.text: D. Results in transient immunosuppression in survivors.
options.ai_explanation: Many sepsis survivors enter a compensatory anti-inflammatory response syndrome (CARS) phase.

options.text: E. Procalcitonin is a marker of sepsis.
options.ai_explanation: Procalcitonin is widely used as a biomarker to support the diagnosis of bacterial sepsis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Recognized complications of sickle cell anaemia

db.general_explanation: All listed are recognized complications of sickle cell anaemia.

ai.clinical_core: Sickle cell disease is a multi-system disorder characterized by vaso-occlusion and chronic hemolysis.

options.ai_explanation: A major chronic complication in adults.
Common due to chronic ischemia.

subject: BLOOD
stem: The following drugs are routinely used in patients with sickle cell anaemia. Which drug alters the pathogenesis of sickling of red cells?

db.general_explanation: Hydroxyurea increases HbF levels, which interferes with the polymerization of HbS.

ai.clinical_core: Sickle cell pathogenesis involves the polymerization of HbS. Hydroxyurea increases the production of HbF, which inhibits this polymerization.

options.ai_explanation: Supports erythropoiesis but doesn't affect sickling.
Increases HbF levels, which directly interferes with the sickling process.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding sickle cell anaemia

db.general_explanation: A. T. B. T (pathogenesis: dehydration caused by K+ leaving red cells). C. F (Exchange transfusions in severe/recurrent crisis or before emergency surgery). D. F (Hydroxyurea increases HbF concentration). E. T (complication: vaso-occlusive crisis, pulmonary hypertension, acute chest syndrome, anaemia).

answer_raw: A. Auto splenectomy occurs in homogeneous individuals
E. Pulmonary hypertension is known complication.
ai.clinical_core: Sickle cell anemia (HbSS) involves chronic hemolysis and vaso-occlusive events, leading to progressive organ damage.

options.text: A. Auto splenectomy occurs in homogeneous individuals
options.ai_explanation: Repeated splenic infarctions lead to a shrunken, non-functional spleen by adulthood.

options.text: B. Dehydration occurs with repeated sickling
options.ai_explanation: The RBC itself becomes dehydrated, but 'dehydration occurs' is a vague phrasing; usually, systemic dehydration triggers sickling.

options.text: C. Exchange transfusion is indicated for aplastic crisis
options.ai_explanation: Simple transfusion is usually sufficient for aplastic crisis; exchange is for stroke or acute chest syndrome.
Hydroxyurea increases HbF to prevent sickling, but it does not 'irreversibly reverse' it.

options.text: E. Pulmonary hypertension is known complication.
options.ai_explanation: Chronic hemolysis and thromboembolism lead to pulmonary hypertension in many adults.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Recognized complication/s of sickle cell anaemia,

db.general_explanation: Ulcers are a result of vascular stasis/ischemia. Retinopathy occurs due to vessel occlusion. Pulmonary hypertension is caused by NO deficiency and endothelial dysfunction. Pigment (bilirubin) gallstones are frequent due to excessive Hb breakdown.

ai.clinical_core: Sickle cell disease causes chronic hemolysis and vaso-occlusion, leading to multi-organ damage.

options.ai_explanation: Sickle cell disease is not a pre-leukemic condition.
Chronic leg ulcers are a common complication due to ischemia and venous incompetence.
Vaso-occlusion in retinal vessels leads to ischemia and neovascularization.
A major cause of morbidity and mortality in adults with sickle cell disease.
Patients get pigment (bilirubin) stones, which are radiopaque, not translucent.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 3 year old boy diagnosed with sickle cell anemia presents with fever, shortness of breath, and pain in the left hypochondrium. Which of the following is the most likely in this patient?

db.general_explanation: Shortness of breath and left hypochondrial pain are classic signs of splenic sequestration, a major complication of sickle cell anemia.

ai.clinical_core: Splenic sequestration crisis is a life-threatening complication in young children with sickle cell anemia, where blood is trapped in the spleen, leading to acute anemia and shock.

options.ai_explanation: Usually caused by Parvovirus B19; presents with low reticulocytes but not typically acute abdominal pain.
Common in older sickle cell patients due to pigment stones, but less likely to cause acute SOB and pain in a 3-year-old.
Uncommon in this age group.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Recognized complications of sickle cell disease include,

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 5-year-old presented with pallor and icterus. He was found to have painful swollen fingers of different sizes. He also complained of pain in his left upper abdomen. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

db.general_explanation: The patient exhibits signs of haemolytic crises (pallor and icterus), vaso-occlusive painful crises (swollen fingers), and splenic sequestration (pain in left upper abdomen), which are characteristic of sickle cell disease.

ai.clinical_core: Dactylitis (painful swelling of fingers/toes) is often the first sign of sickle cell disease in infants. Splenic infarction causes left upper quadrant pain.

Causes joint bleeds, not dactylitis.
Causes joint bleeds, not dactylitis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Recognized complications following splenectomy include

db.general_explanation: Splenectomy complications include pancreatic injury, DVT, gastric dilatation, hemorrhage, and susceptibility to encapsulated organisms like pneumococcus.

ai.clinical_core: Splenectomy carries both immediate surgical risks (injury to adjacent organs) and long-term hematological/immunological risks, including thrombocytosis and overwhelming post-splenectomy infection (OPSI).

options.ai_explanation: The tail of the pancreas lies in the splenic hilum and is at risk of injury, leading to pancreatitis or fistula.

This is a recognized post-operative complication of splenectomy due to manipulation of the stomach or short gastric vessels.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Iron chelation therapy in thalassaemia major.

db.general_explanation: Chelation is started after iron overload is documented. Desferrioxamine requires hearing assessment. Serum ferritin is used for monitoring. Deferasirox can cause liver dysfunction. Deferiprone is oral.

ai.clinical_core: Iron chelation is vital in transfusion-dependent thalassemia to prevent organ damage from iron overload.

options.text: A. With started with 1st blood transfusion.
options.ai_explanation: Chelation usually starts after 10-20 transfusions or when ferritin exceeds 1000 ng/mL.

options.text: B. With destcrrioxamine require regular hecrry assessment.

options.text: C. Regular monitor icy needed with serum forrtra assay.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 10-year-old child with thalassaemia major was found to have increased annual demand of blood transfusions with a serum feritin of 2500 IU/I. On examination he was found to have a spleen of 4cm. Splenectomy was planned due to hypersplenism. Which statement best describes his immunization requirement prior to splenectomy?

db.general_explanation: Post-splenectomy patients are at risk of sepsis from encapsulated organisms (Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, Haemophilus influenzae). Immunization should ideally be done 2 weeks before elective splenectomy.

ai.answer_raw: B. Administration of meningococcal and pneumococcal vaccines two weeks prior to surgery
ai.clinical_core: Splenectomy increases the risk of Overwhelming Post-Splenectomy Infection (OPSI), primarily by encapsulated bacteria. Pre-operative immunization is critical for protection.
ai.reference: British Society for Hematology Guidelines on Splenectomy

options.text: A. Administration of Haemophilus influenza b (Hib) vaccine two weeks prior to surgery
options.ai_explanation: While Hib is needed, it is usually given alongside pneumococcal and meningococcal vaccines; B is more comprehensive.

options.text: B. Administration of meningococcal and pneumococcal vaccines two weeks prior to surgery
options.ai_explanation: Standard protocol requires immunization against Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Hib at least 2 weeks before elective splenectomy.

options.text: C. Adnimistration of Varicella zoster vaccine two weeks prior to surgery
options.ai_explanation: Varicella is not a standard requirement for post-splenectomy prophylaxis against encapsulated bacteria.

options.text: D. If the EPI schedule has been completed no extra vaccines are needed
options.ai_explanation: Extra doses and specific vaccines (like meningococcal) are required regardless of the standard EPI schedule.

options.text: E. Vaccination should be decided during the immediate post operative period
options.ai_explanation: Pre-operative vaccination is preferred to allow for an adequate immune response before the spleen is removed.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Hemoglobin E/B thalassemia :

options.text: B. It is the commonest cause for thalassemia intermedia in Sri lanka.

options.text: D. Pt. develop vaso occlusive crises.

subject: BLOOD
stem: In β thalassemia major,

db.general_explanation: Low platelets indicate hypersplenism, the gene is on chromosome 11, it is autosomal recessive, and RDW is increased. Reticulocytosis is usually inappropriately low in relation to the degree of anaemia.

answer_raw: A. Low platelet count indicates hypersplenism
B. The affected gene is located on chromosome 11
C. Inheritance pattern is autosomal recessive
D. RBC distribution width is increased
ai.clinical_core: Beta Thalassemia Major is an autosomal recessive disorder caused by mutations in the HBB gene on chromosome 11, leading to absent or reduced beta-globin synthesis.

options.text: A. Low platelet count indicates hypersplenism
options.ai_explanation: Splenomegaly in thalassemia can lead to sequestration and low platelet counts.

options.text: B. The affected gene is located on chromosome 11
options.ai_explanation: The beta-globin gene cluster is located on the short arm of chromosome 11.

options.text: C. Inheritance pattern is autosomal recessive
options.ai_explanation: Thalassemia major occurs when an individual inherits two mutated alleles.

options.text: D. RBC distribution width is increased
options.ai_explanation: RDW is high in Thalassemia Major due to significant anisocytosis.

options.text: E. The reticulocytosis is in keeping with the degree of haemolysis
options.ai_explanation: Reticulocytosis is often inappropriately low for the degree of anemia due to ineffective erythropoiesis.

subject: BLOOD
stem: True or false regarding pathophysiology of beta thalassemia,

db.general_explanation: Bone marrow overactivity leads to maxillary and mandibular hyperplasia. Excess alpha globin chain deposition occurs. Mutation is on the Beta gene (chromosome 11).

answer_raw: D. Ineffective erythropoiesis is a characteristic feature
ai.clinical_core: Beta thalassemia is caused by reduced synthesis of beta-globin chains, leading to an excess of alpha-globin chains which precipitate and damage RBCs.

options.ai_explanation: Massive erythroid hyperplasia leads to expansion of the marrow cavity and skeletal deformities.

options.text: B. Gamma globulin chain deposition in red blood cells
options.ai_explanation: It is the excess alpha-globin chains that deposit and cause damage.

options.text: C. Genetic mutation is on chromosome 16
options.ai_explanation: The beta-globin gene is on chromosome 11; alpha-globin is on chromosome 16.

options.text: D. Ineffective erythropoiesis is a characteristic feature
options.ai_explanation: Most erythroid precursors die in the marrow due to the toxic effects of alpha-chain precipitates.

options.text: E. Production of alpha to beta globulin is 1:1
options.ai_explanation: The hallmark of thalassemia is the imbalance in chain production.

subject: BLOOD
stem: What is the best blood product that can be given to a child with beta thalassemia major with Hb level of 6g/dl?

db.general_explanation: Ideally, they should receive extended phenotype red cells to avoid future complications, but routinely, leucocyte-depleted red cells are given at the hospital.

ai.answer_raw: C. Leucocyte depleted packed red cells
ai.clinical_core: Patients with Beta Thalassemia Major require lifelong regular transfusions. To minimize the risk of febrile non-hemolytic transfusion reactions and HLA sensitization, leucocyte-depleted red cells are the standard of care.
ai.reference: Hoffbrand's Essential Hematology, Chapter 7 and Chapter 26

options.ai_explanation: While phenotyping for Rh and Kell is standard, leucocyte depletion is the primary requirement to prevent common transfusion reactions in chronically transfused patients.
Irradiation is used to prevent Transfusion-Associated Graft-Versus-Host Disease in severely immunocompromised patients, which is not the primary concern in thalassemia.

options.text: C. Leucocyte depleted packed red cells
options.ai_explanation: Leucocyte depletion reduces the risk of febrile reactions and alloimmunization, which is critical for patients requiring lifelong transfusion support.
Washed red cells are indicated for patients with severe allergic reactions or IgA deficiency, not as a routine for thalassemia.
Frozen red cells are used for rare blood types and are not practical or necessary for routine thalassemia management.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 25 years old female patient known for beta thalassemia was admitted due to abdominal pain. She has been vomiting for 2 days before the admission and had fever. she was on Deferasirox. Her investigation results were Hb 7.5 mmHg, WBC 55000, Platelet 650000, Serum ferritin 5500. From following measurements what is the least thing that you would take after her admission.

db.general_explanation: We cannot stop chelating iron (Deferasirox) given the high ferritin levels. The patient needs antibiotics for high WBC, transfusion for low Hb, US scan for abdominal pain, and IV fluids for vomiting.

ai.clinical_core: Patients with iron overload are susceptible to Yersinia enterocolitica, which thrives on iron. Deferasirox can exacerbate this, and abdominal pain/fever in this context suggests infection.
ai.reference: British Society for Hematology Guidelines on Thalassemia

options.ai_explanation: Hb 7.5 is low but not necessarily an emergency compared to potential sepsis.
Chelators should be stopped during acute febrile illnesses, especially if Yersinia is suspected.
Important, but stopping the chelator is a specific management step for this patient.
Useful but not the most immediate priority.
Supportive care, but not the most specific action.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Laboratory investigations seen in thalassemia trait include,

db.general_explanation: Thalassemia trait features hypochromic and microcytic RBCs (Low MCV, MCH, MCHC). Serum ferritin and iron stores are normal. Spherocytes are seen in hereditary spherocytosis/AIHA.

ai.clinical_core: Thalassemia trait is characterized by a mild microcytic anemia with a disproportionately low MCV relative to the degree of anemia.

options.ai_explanation: HbF is usually normal in beta-thal trait; HbA2 is elevated.
MCH is low in thalassemia trait.
Microcytosis is a hallmark of thalassemia trait.

subject: BLOOD
stem: 7 year old girl with beta thalassemia major is admitted with pallor. Her post transfusional Hb level following transfusion 2 weeks ago was 13.5 g/dl. for the past 6 months she has required blood transfusions within 2-3 weeks to maintain Hb level at 9.5 g/dl. Her spleen was palpated 10cm below the costal margin. Her investigations are given below. Hb 7g/dl, WBC 6000/mm3, platelet 100 x 10^9/L, serum ferritin 1900ng/L what is the most appropriate investigations in her overall management?

db.general_explanation: The patient shows clear evidence of hypersplenism (rapid transfusion requirement, splenomegaly, and thrombocytopenia), making splenectomy the appropriate management.

ai.clinical_core: In Thalassemia Major, a significant increase in transfusion requirements (e.g., >200-250 ml/kg/year) accompanied by massive splenomegaly and cytopenias suggests hypersplenism.

options.text: A. Change iron chelation therapy to Deferrisirox
options.ai_explanation: While chelation is needed, it doesn't address the increased transfusion demand caused by the spleen.

options.text: B. Increase iron chelation by increasing Desferrioxamine dose
options.ai_explanation: This addresses iron overload but not the underlying cause of rapid Hb drop.

options.text: C. Optimize the blood transfusion regimen
options.ai_explanation: The regimen is already failing to maintain Hb due to splenic sequestration/destruction.

options.text: D. Plan a bone marrow transplantation
options.ai_explanation: BMT is a curative option but not the immediate management for symptomatic hypersplenism.
Splenectomy is indicated when hypersplenism increases transfusion requirements or causes symptomatic splenomegaly.

subject: BLOOD

answer_raw: A. Anti thrombin deficiency is an inherited disorder
B. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome presents as recurrent first trimester abortions
D. Protein C is a naturally occurring anticoagulant
E. Protein S is a vitamin K dependent anticoagulant
ai.clinical_core: Thrombophilia can be inherited (e.g., Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency) or acquired (e.g., APS). These conditions shift the hemostatic balance toward thrombosis.

options.text: A. Anti thrombin deficiency is an inherited disorder
options.ai_explanation: Antithrombin deficiency is an autosomal dominant inherited thrombophilia.

options.text: B. Antiphospholipid antibody syndrome presents as recurrent first trimester abortions
options.ai_explanation: Recurrent pregnancy loss (both early and late) is a major clinical criterion for APS.

options.text: C. Inherited causes are more common than acquired
options.ai_explanation: Acquired risk factors (surgery, malignancy, immobilization, APS) are more prevalent in the general population than inherited deficiencies.

options.text: D. Protein C is a naturally occurring anticoagulant
options.ai_explanation: Activated Protein C degrades Factors Va and VIIIa, serving as a critical natural anticoagulant.

options.text: E. Protein S is a vitamin K dependent anticoagulant
options.ai_explanation: Protein S is a Vitamin K-dependent protein that acts as a cofactor for activated Protein C.

subject: BLOOD

ai.clinical_core: Thrombophilia refers to a predisposition to thrombosis. Deficiencies in natural anticoagulants (Protein C, S, and Antithrombin) lead to a prothrombotic state.

options.ai_explanation: Protein C is a natural anticoagulant; its deficiency increases the risk of venous thromboembolism.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 65 year old man is admitted to the casualty ward within 3 hours of ST elevated myocardial infarction. Which of the following caution you most against administration of Streptokinase?

db.general_explanation: Recent major surgery is a significant contraindication for thrombolytic therapy due to the risk of severe haemorrhage.

ai.answer_raw: B. History of administration of streptokinase 3 weeks ago
ai.clinical_core: Streptokinase is antigenic. Previous administration leads to high titers of neutralizing antibodies, which can cause treatment failure or severe allergic reactions if repeated within 6 months to a year.

options.ai_explanation: Age 65 is not a contraindication for thrombolysis.

options.text: D. History of having had a major surgery 3 weeks ago

options.text: E. History of a cardio pulmonary resuscitation a week ago
options.ai_explanation: Traumatic CPR is a relative contraindication.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Which of the following conditions predispose to thrombosis?

db.general_explanation: Nephrotic syndrome (not nephritic) leads to loss of anti-thrombin. PNH is associated with thrombosis, haemolysis, and impaired bone marrow function.

ai.clinical_core: Thrombophilia can be inherited or acquired. Malignancy, myeloproliferative neoplasms (ET, PV), and PNH are well-recognized states of hypercoagulability leading to venous and arterial thrombosis.

ai.clinical_core: Transfusion therapy in Thalassemia Major aims to suppress ineffective erythropoiesis while minimizing complications like alloimmunization and iron overload.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Regarding transplant associated infections,

ai.clinical_core: Post-transplant infections follow a predictable timeline: early (surgical/donor-derived), middle (opportunistic/CMV), and late (community-acquired). The type of organ also dictates the risk profile.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Transplantation

options.text: A. BK virus infection is the commonest following liver transplantation.

options.text: C. Donor is a source of infection in early transplantation period.

options.text: D. Frequency of transplant related infection is higher in lung than in kidney transplantation
options.ai_explanation: Lung transplants have higher infection rates due to constant environmental exposure and impaired ciliary function.

options.text: E. Infection by opportunistic pathogens occur within one week after transplantation.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Hydroxyuria is used in treatment of

db.general_explanation: Used in Myeloproliferative diseases (Polycythemia vera and ET), Sickle-cell disease, Psoriasis, Systemic mastocytosis, and CML.

ai.clinical_core: Hydroxyurea is a cytotoxic agent that inhibits ribonucleotide reductase. In hemoglobinopathies, it increases fetal hemoglobin (HbF) levels, while in myeloproliferative neoplasms, it acts as a cytoreductive agent.

options.ai_explanation: Hydroxyurea is contraindicated in aplastic anemia as it causes bone marrow suppression.
Hydroxyurea can increase HbF levels and reduce transfusion requirements in some patients with thalassemia.
Similar to beta-thalassemia major, it helps increase HbF to improve anemia.
It is used as a first-line cytoreductive therapy to control high hematocrit and reduce thrombotic risk.
It is the standard of care to increase HbF, which inhibits the polymerization of HbS and reduces vaso-occlusive crises.

subject: BLOOD
stem: How tumour cells evade immune mechanisms

db.general_explanation: Tumor evasion mechanisms include loss of co-stimulation, antigen loss, and production of immunosuppressive cytokines like TGF-β.

answer_raw: B. Loss of antigen peptide expression
D. Production of tumour growth factor β
ai.clinical_core: Tumors evade the immune system by reducing their visibility (losing antigens/MHC) or creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Without co-stimulation, T cells that recognize tumor antigens become anergic.

options.text: B. Loss of antigen peptide expression
options.ai_explanation: Tumors may stop expressing the antigens that the immune system targets.

options.text: C. Presence of tumour specific CD8 cells
options.ai_explanation: This is an immune defense, not an evasion mechanism.

options.text: D. Production of tumour growth factor β
options.ai_explanation: TGF-beta is a potent immunosuppressive cytokine produced by many tumors.

options.text: E. Up regulation of MSC molecules expression on tumour cells
options.ai_explanation: Tumors typically down-regulate MHC (MSC) to avoid detection by T cells.

subject: BLOOD
stem: Vitamin B12

db.general_explanation: A. F - VB12 is water soluble vitamin excess amount execrate via urine, therefore does not accumulate in the body. B. F - Absorbed in terminal ilium. T – found in animal product. T – mainly stored in liver. F – intrinsic factor found in the stomach, in blood- transported bound to Trance cobalamin.

answer_raw: A. Accumulates in the body with repeated dose.
C. Is found exclusively in foods of animal origin.
ai.clinical_core: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) is essential for DNA synthesis. It is obtained from animal products and has significant hepatic stores.

options.text: A. Accumulates in the body with repeated dose.
options.ai_explanation: The body has a high storage capacity for B12, primarily in the liver.

options.text: B. Is absorbed in the duodenum.
options.ai_explanation: B12 is absorbed in the terminal ileum.

options.text: C. Is found exclusively in foods of animal origin.
options.ai_explanation: Plants do not synthesize B12; it is found in meat, eggs, and dairy.
The liver stores enough B12 to last for several years.

options.text: E. Is Transported bound to Intrinsic factor in blood.
options.ai_explanation: IF is for absorption; in the blood, B12 is bound to transcobalamin.

subject: BLOOD
stem: A 60 year old woman presents with lethargy. Her Hb is 9 g/dl. Which of the following would best support a diagnosis of vitamin B12 deficiency in this patient?

db.general_explanation: B12 is a cofactor in the conversion of homocysteine to methionine.

ai.answer_raw: D. Oval macrocytes on the blood film.
ai.clinical_core: Vitamin B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia. While many markers are suggestive, the presence of oval macrocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils is highly characteristic.

options.ai_explanation: Elevated in both B12 and folate deficiency; not specific.
Indicates ineffective erythropoiesis but is seen in many hemolytic and megaloblastic states.
Macrocytosis has many causes (alcohol, liver disease, etc.).

options.text: D. Oval macrocytes on the blood film.
options.ai_explanation: Oval macrocytes are a specific morphological feature of megaloblastic anemia.
Seen in any condition with increased RBC turnover.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Appropriate Mx of anaphalaetic shaek.

db.general_explanation: The correct management includes Adrenalin IM 1:1000 0.5ml, IV 1:10000 0.5ml, Hydrocortisone IV 200mg, and Chloropiniramine 10mg IV.

ai.clinical_core: Adrenaline is the life-saving treatment for anaphylaxis. The intramuscular route is preferred for its safety and rapid absorption.

options.ai_explanation: Adjunctive treatment, not the first-line emergency drug.
Adjunctive treatment for skin symptoms.
Subcutaneous absorption is too slow and unreliable in shock.
Incorrect concentration and route.
IM adrenaline (0.5mg-1mg) is the standard emergency dose for adults.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: A 25-year-old patient with cellulitis of the leg complains of difficulty in breathing and dizziness after an intravenous injection of co-amoxiclav. Immediate management of this patient include

db.general_explanation: Elevation of the foot end (not head end) is required to increase venous return. Adrenaline, hydrocortisone, oxygen, and rapid assessment are all appropriate.

answer_raw: B. Intra muscular injection of adrenalin
C. Intra venous injection of hydrocortisone
D. Provision of high flow oxygen
E. Rapid assessment of airway breathing and circulation
ai.clinical_core: Anaphylaxis is a medical emergency requiring rapid recognition and treatment with adrenaline.

options.text: A. Elevation of the head end of the patient
options.ai_explanation: Patients in anaphylactic shock should be kept flat with legs elevated to maintain venous return.

options.text: B. Intra muscular injection of adrenalin
options.ai_explanation: IM adrenaline is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.

options.text: C. Intra venous injection of hydrocortisone
options.ai_explanation: Used to prevent late-phase reactions, though not the immediate life-saving step.

options.text: D. Provision of high flow oxygen
options.ai_explanation: Essential for managing respiratory distress in anaphylaxis.

options.text: E. Rapid assessment of airway breathing and circulation
options.ai_explanation: The standard ABC approach is vital in any emergency.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following is the most specific agent used in the treatment of autoimmune disease?

db.general_explanation: Monoclonal antibodies are highly specific therapeutic agents for autoimmune conditions.

ai.clinical_core: While steroids and cytotoxic drugs are widely used, monoclonal antibodies (e.g., Rituximab, anti-TNF) are the most specific as they target single molecules or cell types involved in the pathogenesis.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Broadly immunosuppressive with many off-target effects.
Inhibit all rapidly dividing cells, not specific to the autoimmune process.
Usually used to stimulate the immune system (e.g., G-CSF) rather than treat autoimmunity.
Target specific antigens (like CD20 or TNF-alpha), providing the highest specificity.
Treat symptoms of inflammation but do not modify the underlying autoimmune disease.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Machanisms of autoimmunity include;

db.general_explanation: Mechanisms include availability of sequestered antigens, dysregulation of idiotype network, extra-thymic T cell development, and infection of APCs.

answer_raw: A. Availability of normally sequestered self antigens in circulation.
D. Infection of antigen presenting cell.
E. Polyclonal activation of T cells
ai.clinical_core: Autoimmunity results from a breakdown in self-tolerance through various mechanisms including molecular mimicry, release of sequestered antigens, and bypass of T-cell help.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.text: A. Availability of normally sequestered self antigens in circulation.
options.ai_explanation: Antigens in 'immune privileged' sites (like the eye) can trigger autoimmunity if released into circulation.
The idiotype-anti-idiotype network normally regulates immune responses; its failure can lead to autoimmunity.
T cells that bypass central thymic tolerance may be autoreactive.

options.text: D. Infection of antigen presenting cell.
options.ai_explanation: Infections can induce co-stimulatory molecules on APCs presenting self-antigens, breaking peripheral tolerance.

options.text: E. Polyclonal activation of T cells
options.ai_explanation: Superantigens can polyclonally activate T cells, including those that are autoreactive.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Regarding activation of B cells:

db.general_explanation: Memory cells are activated faster. Signal 2 is provided by the T cell.

answer_raw: B. Memory cells are activated faster than naïve B cells
ai.clinical_core: B cell activation requires antigen binding and, for T-dependent antigens, interaction with T-helper cells via MHC II.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. B cells differentiate in to helper cells.
options.ai_explanation: B cells differentiate into plasma cells and memory cells.

options.text: B. Memory cells are activated faster than naïve B cells
options.ai_explanation: Memory cells have a lower threshold for activation and respond more rapidly.
B cells must present antigen on MHC II to receive help from T cells.

options.text: D. Signal 1 is provided by an accessory cell.
options.ai_explanation: Signal 1 is provided by the antigen binding to the B-cell receptor.

options.text: E. Polyclonal activation of T cells.
options.ai_explanation: B cell activation is antigen-specific, not polyclonal.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: The most common B cell disorder is

db.general_explanation: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency.

ai.clinical_core: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency disorder, characterized by undetectable serum IgA levels with normal IgG and IgM. Many patients are asymptomatic.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Primary Immunodeficiency

options.ai_explanation: This is a rare X-linked condition involving a complete lack of B cells.
This is a rare defect in class-switch recombination.
With an incidence of approximately 1 in 500 to 1 in 700 in Caucasians, it is the most common B-cell/antibody disorder.
SCID is a rare, life-threatening pediatric emergency involving both T and B cell defects.
This is a rare X-linked recessive disease characterized by eczema, thrombocytopenia, and immune deficiency.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: What is the most important mechanism for initiation of cell mediated immunity.

db.general_explanation: Keyword is initiation. For the initiation of the T cell response a processed antigen which is presented by the APC has to be recognized by the T cell receptor.

ai.answer_raw: D. Receiving 1st and 2nd signals with cytokine stimulation
ai.clinical_core: T-cell activation (the basis of cell-mediated immunity) requires three signals: Signal 1 (Antigen/MHC), Signal 2 (Co-stimulation, e.g., B7/CD28), and Signal 3 (Cytokines to direct differentiation).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section on Immunology

options.text: A. Antigen recognition by T cell receptors
options.ai_explanation: This is Signal 1; without Signal 2, it leads to anergy rather than activation.
This is Signal 2; it is necessary but insufficient without Signal 1.
While these initiate activation, Signal 3 (cytokines) is essential for full effector function and proliferation.

options.text: D. Receiving 1st and 2nd signals with cytokine stimulation
options.ai_explanation: This encompasses the complete 'three-signal' model required for robust T-cell activation and differentiation.

options.text: E. memory response to previous antigen
options.ai_explanation: Memory is a result of the immune response, not the mechanism for its initial initiation.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: A 62 year old diabetic patient presented with 5 day history of fever and cellulitis secondary to a wound. What is the first step in the management?

db.general_explanation: Elevation is used to reduce edema and pain, prior to further diagnostic/therapeutic steps.

ai.clinical_core: In a diabetic patient with fever and cellulitis, the risk of rapid progression and necrotizing fasciitis is high. Prompt initiation of empirical antibiotics is the priority.

options.text: A. Advise to keep affected leg in an elevated position
options.ai_explanation: This is supportive care, not the first step in an acutely ill patient.

options.text: B. Obtain blood for haematological investigations
options.ai_explanation: Bloods should be drawn, but starting treatment for the infection is the clinical priority.

options.text: C. Obtain wound aspirate for culture and antibiotic sensitivity testing
options.ai_explanation: Cultures should be taken before antibiotics, but the 'management' priority is starting the therapy.
Early antibiotic administration is critical in diabetic patients to prevent systemic sepsis and tissue loss.
Debridement is necessary if there is necrosis, but empirical antibiotics are the first-line medical management.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Best description about classical pathway of compliment activation.

db.general_explanation: The classical pathway is defined by the utilization of the C1 complex.

ai.clinical_core: The classical pathway of complement is triggered by the binding of C1q to the Fc portion of IgM or IgG antibodies complexed with antigen.

options.ai_explanation: While true, this describes all complement pathways, not just the classical one.
All three pathways (classical, lectin, alternative) eventually produce C5 convertase.
The Membrane Attack Complex (MAC) is the common end-stage of all complement pathways.
C3 cleavage is the central event of all complement pathways.
The use of the C1 complex (C1q, C1r, C1s) is unique to the classical pathway.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following describes the most specific immune-pathological feature in causation of acute post streptococcal glomerulonephritis

db.general_explanation: Post-streptococcal GN is caused by the deposition of circulating immune complexes involving SpeB.

ai.answer_raw: B. Formation of circulating immune complexes against streptococcal pyogenic exotoxin B (SpeB)
ai.clinical_core: Post-streptococcal GN is a Type III hypersensitivity reaction where immune complexes deposit in the glomeruli, triggering inflammation.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Nephrology Section

options.ai_explanation: Characteristic of SLE, not PSGN.

options.text: B. Formation of circulating immune complexes against streptococcal pyogenic exotoxin B (SpeB)
options.ai_explanation: SpeB and NAPlr are the major streptococcal antigens involved in complex formation.

options.text: C. Formation of cross reacting T cells against streptococcus pyogenes
options.ai_explanation: PSGN is antibody-mediated, not T-cell mediated.

options.text: D. Formation of in-situ immune complexes against glomerular basement membrane antigens
options.ai_explanation: This describes Goodpasture syndrome.

options.text: E. Formation of pro-inflammatory cytokines following streptococcus pyogenes infection.
options.ai_explanation: Cytokines are involved, but the specific immunopathological feature is immune complex deposition.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: In a patient with HIV infection, presense of following indicate/s development of acquired immune deficiency syndrome

db.general_explanation: Cerebral toxoplasmosis, primary cerebral lymphoma, and specific respiratory conditions define AIDS, while oral candidiasis and standard pneumonia do not inherently mark the syndrome.

ai.clinical_core: AIDS is defined by HIV infection plus a CD4 count <200 or the presence of specific opportunistic infections or malignancies.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, HIV Section

options.ai_explanation: A classic AIDS-defining opportunistic infection.
Oral thrush is an HIV-related condition (Stage 3) but not an AIDS-defining illness (Stage 4).
Recurrent bacterial pneumonia is AIDS-defining, but a single episode of pneumococcal pneumonia is not.
An AIDS-defining malignancy.
Pulmonary TB is an AIDS-defining condition in the WHO clinical staging.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: A 45-year-old male hotel receptionist is admitted with fever for 14 days and shortness of breath on mild exertion... generalized lymphadenopathy, whitish patches over the palate and throat and shingles in L2,3 AND L5 dermatomes. What is the most likely illness in this patient?

db.general_explanation: Presence of multiple opportunistic infections (candidiasis, shingles) in a patient indicates AIDS.

ai.clinical_core: AIDS presents with profound immunosuppression, leading to opportunistic infections like oral candidiasis (thrush), multidermatomal shingles, and constitutional symptoms.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Infectious Diseases Section

options.ai_explanation: The combination of oral thrush, multidermatomal shingles, and lymphadenopathy is highly suggestive of advanced HIV/AIDS.
Can cause lymphadenopathy but thrush and shingles are more specific for T-cell deficiency.
Does not cause lymphadenopathy.
Shingles is a reactivation, not primary chickenpox; the other features suggest a broader immune defect.
Does not cause oral thrush or multidermatomal shingles.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which is the most important host defense mechanism against a virus infected cell?

db.general_explanation: NK and Tc cells are the primary targets for intracellular pathogens like viruses.

ai.clinical_core: While antibodies and interferon help prevent viral entry and spread, the elimination of cells already infected by a virus is primarily the role of CD8+ cytotoxic T cells.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Complement is more effective against extracellular bacteria than intracellular viruses.
CD8+ T cells recognize viral peptides on MHC Class I and induce apoptosis in the infected host cell.
Interferon alpha induces an antiviral state in neighboring cells but does not directly 'kill' the infected cell.
IgA prevents viral attachment at mucosal surfaces but cannot clear an established intracellular infection.
Antibodies neutralize free virus particles but cannot reach viruses inside host cells.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which are the following statement regarding hypersensitivity are correct

db.general_explanation: Type I involves IgE, not antigen-antibody complexes; Type III is not the primary mechanism for transplant rejection.

answer_raw: A. Immediate hypersensitivity is mediated by IgE
D. Type III hypersensitivity is seen in transplant rejection
E. Grannloma seen in type IV hypersensitivity.
ai.clinical_core: Hypersensitivity reactions mediate various pathological states, including allergies, autoimmune diseases, and transplant rejection.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Immediate hypersensitivity is mediated by IgE
options.ai_explanation: Type I hypersensitivity involves IgE binding to mast cells.

options.text: B. Type II hypersensitivity is responsible for transplant rejection
options.ai_explanation: Transplant rejection is primarily Type IV (cellular) and Type II (hyperacute).

options.text: C. Antigen-Antibody complexes are seen in type I hypersensitivity
options.ai_explanation: Immune complexes are the hallmark of Type III hypersensitivity.

options.text: D. Type III hypersensitivity is seen in transplant rejection
options.ai_explanation: Can occur in chronic rejection or hyperacute rejection (via complement).

options.text: E. Grannloma seen in type IV hypersensitivity.
options.ai_explanation: Granulomas are a classic feature of persistent Type IV cell-mediated responses.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Select the correct association of the disease and type of hypersensitivity reaction

db.general_explanation: Arthus is Type III, AIHA is Type II, Contact dermatitis is Type IV, Post-streptococcal GN is Type III.

answer_raw: E. Post streptococcal glomerularneprhitis – type III
ai.clinical_core: Hypersensitivity reactions are classified into four types (Gell and Coombs). Type III involves immune complex deposition, while Type IV is cell-mediated (delayed).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Arthus reaction is a localized Type III hypersensitivity reaction.

options.text: B. Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia – type III
options.ai_explanation: AIHA is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction (antibody-mediated).
Contact dermatitis is a classic example of T-cell mediated Type IV hypersensitivity.
Leprosy involves Type IV hypersensitivity (granulomatous response).

options.text: E. Post streptococcal glomerularneprhitis – type III
options.ai_explanation: PSGN is caused by the deposition of immune complexes in the glomeruli.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the follow is/are correctly matched with the hypersensitivity reaction

db.general_explanation: PSGN is Type 3 (immune complex deposition). Rheumatic heart disease is Type 2.

answer_raw: B. Auto immune haemolytic anaemia- type 2
ai.clinical_core: Hypersensitivity reactions are classified by their immune mechanism.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: IgE-mediated immediate hypersensitivity.

options.text: B. Auto immune haemolytic anaemia- type 2
options.ai_explanation: Antibody-mediated destruction of cells.
Delayed-type cell-mediated hypersensitivity.
PSGN is Type 3 (immune complex).
Rheumatic fever involves Type 2 hypersensitivity (molecular mimicry).

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following feature best describe the difference between Type 2 and Type 3 hypersensitivity?

db.general_explanation: Type 2 hypersensitivity reaction - Antibodies are directed cell surface antigens (eg: Antigens on erythrocytes) or surface autoantigens. Type 3 hypersensitivity reaction - Antibodies are directed against free antigen (eg: microbes, drugs, antisera, auto antigens)

ai.answer_raw: A. Bound and free nature of antigen-antibody complex
ai.clinical_core: Type II hypersensitivity involves antibodies binding to antigens on a cell surface or tissue (fixed), while Type III involves antibodies binding to soluble antigens, forming circulating complexes.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.text: A. Bound and free nature of antigen-antibody complex
options.ai_explanation: This is the fundamental difference: Type II is cell-bound/fixed antigen; Type III is soluble/circulating antigen.
Both types can activate complement.
Both types primarily involve IgG.
Both types can involve IgM.
T-cells are the hallmark of Type IV, not II or III.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following are antibody mediated hypersensitivity.

db.general_explanation: ABO incompatibility is type ii hypersensitivity reaction which mediated by IgG & IgM antibodies. Type I hypersensitivity reactions mediated by IgE antibodies. Contact dermatitis and pulmonary TB are type iv hypersensitivity reaction. Antibody independent and mediated by T cells. E. type ii hypersensitivity reaction.

ai.clinical_core: Antibody-mediated hypersensitivity includes Type I (IgE), Type II (IgG/IgM against cell surfaces), and Type III (Immune complexes). Type IV is cell-mediated.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: This is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction mediated by IgM/IgG antibodies.
This is a Type I hypersensitivity reaction mediated by IgE antibodies.
This is a Type IV hypersensitivity reaction mediated by T-cells.
The immune response to TB is a Type IV hypersensitivity reaction.
This is a Type II hypersensitivity reaction where antibodies attack the glomerular basement membrane.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following infections are mediated primarily by humoral immunity

db.general_explanation: Diphtheria, Influenza, Pneumococcal pneumonia, and Tetanus trigger humoral responses. Tuberculosis is primarily cell-mediated (Type 4 hypersensitivity).

ai.clinical_core: Humoral immunity (antibodies) is most effective against extracellular bacteria, toxins, and free viruses.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.ai_explanation: Antibodies neutralize the diphtheria toxin.
Antibodies against hemagglutinin and neuraminidase prevent infection.
Antibodies opsonize the capsule for phagocytosis.
Antibodies neutralize the tetanus toxin.
TB is primarily controlled by cell-mediated immunity.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: A 3 month old body presented with repeated extensive intection with candida. Which of following immune deficiency state is more likely,

db.general_explanation: Repeated Candida infections in infancy are strongly suggestive of combined T and B cell deficiency.

ai.clinical_core: Severe Combined Immunodeficiency (SCID) involves defects in both cellular (T-cell) and humoral (B-cell) immunity, presenting early with severe fungal, viral, and bacterial infections.

options.ai_explanation: Pure antibody deficiency usually presents later (after 6 months) and with pyogenic bacteria, not early Candida.
The early onset (3 months) and severe Candida infection are classic for SCID (Combined T and B cell deficiency).
Complement defects do not typically cause early-onset candidiasis.
Neutrophil defects (like CGD) cause skin and organ abscesses with specific organisms (Staph, Aspergillus), but SCID is more likely for this presentation.
This is likely a typo for ADA (Adenosine Deaminase) deficiency, which is a cause of SCID, but 'T & B lymphocyte deficiency' is the broader state.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Examples for active and passive immunity given together.

db.general_explanation: Active and passive immunization is used in conjunction in Rabies, Diphtheria, Hep B, Varicella. Note that Varicella treatment involves acyclovir, not a vaccine.

answer_raw: A. Prophylaxis of hepatitis B infection
ai.clinical_core: Combined active and passive immunization involves giving both a vaccine (active) and pre-formed antibodies (passive) to provide immediate and long-lasting protection.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.text: A. Prophylaxis of hepatitis B infection
options.ai_explanation: Post-exposure prophylaxis involves both the Hep B vaccine and Hepatitis B Immune Globulin (HBIG).
Standard prophylaxis is active immunization (MMR vaccine) only.
Post-exposure prophylaxis requires both the rabies vaccine and Rabies Immune Globulin (RIG).
Management of tetanus-prone wounds often involves tetanus toxoid (active) and Tetanus Immune Globulin (passive).
In high-risk exposures, both the vaccine and Varicella-Zoster Immune Globulin (VZIG) may be used.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Passive immunization in conjunction with active immunization

db.general_explanation: Hep B, tetanus, Rabies are treated with Passive immunization conjugated with active. Immune compromised patient with chicken pox is treated with IV Acyclovir

answer_raw: A. Non-immune patient with Hepatitis B
ai.clinical_core: Post-exposure prophylaxis often involves 'passive-active' immunization, providing immediate antibodies (passive) and stimulating long-term immunity (active).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Infectious Diseases Section

options.text: A. Non-immune patient with Hepatitis B
options.ai_explanation: HBIG and the Hep B vaccine are given together after exposure in non-immune individuals.
Tetanus immunoglobulin (TIG) and tetanus toxoid are given for tetanus-prone wounds.
Rabies immunoglobulin (RIG) and rabies vaccine are standard post-exposure treatment.
Cholera management does not involve passive immunization.
Immunocompromised patients receive VZIG (passive), but the active vaccine is live-attenuated and usually contraindicated.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Passive immunization in conjunction with active immunization

db.general_explanation: Hep B, tetanus, Rabies are treated with Passive immunization conjugated with active. Immune compromised patient with chicken pox is treated with IV Acyclovir

answer_raw: A. Non-immune patient with Hepatitis B
ai.clinical_core: Passive-active immunization provides both immediate protection and long-term immunity.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Immunology Section

options.text: A. Non-immune patient with Hepatitis B
options.ai_explanation: HBIG + Vaccine.
TIG + Toxoid.
RIG + Vaccine.
No passive immunization used.
Active vaccine is live and usually contraindicated.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: What is the most important investigation to confirm the diagnosis of common variable immunodeficiency syndrome?

db.general_explanation: CVID is characterized by deficiency of IgA and IgG, and plasma protein electrophoresis helps characterize these levels alongside normal B cell counts.

ai.clinical_core: Common Variable Immunodeficiency (CVID) is a primary immunodeficiency characterized by hypogammaglobulinemia and impaired antibody production. Diagnosis requires not only low serum immunoglobulin levels (IgG and IgA/IgM) but also a demonstrated failure to produce functional antibodies in response to antigens (vaccines).
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Functional antibody deficiency (poor response to protein or polysaccharide vaccines) is a core diagnostic criterion for CVID according to ESID/PAGID guidelines.
B cell counts are often normal or only slightly reduced in CVID; the defect is in B-cell differentiation into plasma cells.
Diagnosis requires quantitative measurement of immunoglobulin levels, not qualitative.
Electrophoresis can show a reduced gamma globulin peak but cannot confirm the functional defect required for CVID diagnosis.
Total lymphocyte counts are usually normal in CVID as T-cell numbers are often preserved.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Causes of secondary immunodeficiency include,

db.general_explanation: Secondary immunodeficiencies are acquired rather than genetic. DiGeorge is a primary immunodeficiency.

ai.clinical_core: Secondary immunodeficiencies are acquired due to external factors such as infections, drugs, or metabolic states, unlike primary immunodeficiencies which are genetic.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: This is a primary (congenital) immunodeficiency caused by a microdeletion on chromosome 22q11.2.
Protein-energy malnutrition is the most common cause of secondary immunodeficiency worldwide.
Measles causes transient but profound immunosuppression by affecting T-cell function.
Burns lead to loss of skin barrier and significant loss of immunoglobulins and lymphocytes.
Corticosteroids are potent immunosuppressive agents that inhibit multiple arms of the immune system.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: What is the most commen type of infection that occur in primary antibody defiency ?

db.general_explanation: Primary antibody deficiency leads to an inability to opsonize and clear capsulated bacteria.

ai.clinical_core: Antibodies (immunoglobulins) are essential for the opsonization and clearance of encapsulated bacteria. Patients with primary antibody deficiencies (e.g., X-linked agammaglobulinemia) are highly susceptible to these organisms.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Amoebic infections are not the primary hallmark of antibody deficiency.
S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and N. meningitidis are the most common pathogens in antibody-deficient patients.
Fungal infections are more characteristic of T-cell or neutrophil deficiencies.
Intracellular bacteria (like Mycobacteria) are primarily controlled by T-cell mediated immunity.
While some viral susceptibility exists (e.g., enteroviruses), bacterial infections are much more common.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Correctly paired primary immunodeficiency with disorder.

db.general_explanation: No specific clinical reasoning provided for each pair but confirmed as T T F T T.

answer_raw: D. Interferon gamma deficiency - low virulent micro bacterial infection
ai.clinical_core: Primary immunodeficiencies are characterized by specific susceptibility to certain types of pathogens based on the affected immune component.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Chronic Granulomatous Disease leads to infections with catalase-positive organisms (S. aureus, Aspergillus).
T-cell defects in SCID lead to severe viral, fungal, and opportunistic infections.

options.text: C. Ig A deficiency- disseminated candida infection
options.ai_explanation: IgA deficiency is often asymptomatic or causes mucosal infections; disseminated candida is seen in neutrophil or T-cell defects.

options.text: D. Interferon gamma deficiency - low virulent micro bacterial infection
options.ai_explanation: Defects in the IL-12/IFN-gamma axis lead to Mendelian Susceptibility to Mycobacterial Disease (MSMD).

options.text: E. Neutrophil deficiency - disseminated viral infection
options.ai_explanation: Neutrophil deficiency leads to bacterial and fungal infections, not viral.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Phagocytes action against bacterial infection inhibited by

db.general_explanation: Phagocytosis is inhibited by the capsule of N. meningitides, M protein of S. pyogenes, and toxin of Mycobacterium tuberculosis.

answer_raw: B. M protan of S. pyogenel
ai.clinical_core: Pathogens have evolved various mechanisms to evade phagocytosis, including physical barriers and interference with intracellular killing.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.ai_explanation: Polysaccharide capsules inhibit opsonization and phagocytic ingestion.

options.text: B. M protan of S. pyogenel
options.ai_explanation: M protein is anti-phagocytic and interferes with complement-mediated opsonization.
M. tuberculosis prevents phagosome-lysosome fusion, allowing it to survive inside macrophages.
Complement (especially C3b) promotes phagocytosis via opsonization.
IFN-gamma is a major activator of macrophages, enhancing their phagocytic and killing capacity.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Innate immune response

db.general_explanation: Innate immunity does not show memory (response enhancement on repeat exposure).

answer_raw: A. Direct the adaptive immune response in the correct pathway
D. Leads to inflammation by compliment activation
E. Recognizes microbes by Toll like receptors
ai.clinical_core: The innate immune response is the immediate, non-specific defense mechanism that recognizes pathogens via pattern recognition receptors (PRRs) like TLRs.
ai.reference: Oxford Textbook of Medicine, Section 4: Immunology

options.text: A. Direct the adaptive immune response in the correct pathway
options.ai_explanation: Innate cells (like dendritic cells) provide the necessary signals (cytokines/co-stimulation) to activate the adaptive response.
Proteins like CRP and mannose-binding lectin are part of the innate humoral response.

options.text: C. Is enhanced by repeated exposure to same micro-oraganism
options.ai_explanation: This describes immunological memory, which is exclusive to the adaptive immune system.

options.text: D. Leads to inflammation by compliment activation
options.ai_explanation: Complement activation (alternative and lectin pathways) is a major innate inflammatory mechanism.

options.text: E. Recognizes microbes by Toll like receptors
options.ai_explanation: TLRs are classic pattern recognition receptors used by innate cells to detect conserved microbial structures.

subject: IMMUNOLOGY
stem: Which of the following components involve in regulation of innate immune system

db.general_explanation: No specific clinical reasoning provided.
